A naive implementation would need to hold N copies of a multi-billion parameter model in memory, which is an immediate non-starter. The authors' algorithm circumvents this with three key innovations.
First is the concept of "virtual noise" via seed regeneration. The ES algorithm operates by perturbing a central parameter vector θ with Gaussian noise to create N offspring for evaluation. Storing these N noise vectors, each the size of the model, is itself intractable. The authors' solution is to only store a small collection of random seeds, one for each member of the population. During the evaluation and update phases, each parallel process can perfectly regenerate the exact same pseudo-random noise vector by re-initializing its random number generator with a specific seed. The noise becomes ephemeral, a "just-in-time" quantity that is generated, used, and discarded without ever occupying significant persistent memory.
Second is the in-place, layer-level perturbation and restoration cycle. To evaluate a population member, the algorithm does not create a new model instance. Instead, it takes the single, shared base model and performs a temporary, surgical modification. It iterates through the model's layers one by one. For each layer, it generates the corresponding slice of the noise vector (from the seed) and adds it directly to the layer's weights in-place. After this process is complete for all layers, the now-perturbed model must be evaluated to determine its fitness.
The evaluation itself is a critical step. The perturbed model performs a single, deterministic forward pass using greedy decoding to generate a response to a given prompt. This deterministic generation is a crucial design choice, as it ensures that any variation in performance between population members stems purely from their different parameters, not from randomness in the token sampling (i.e., the action space). This generated response is then scored by a reward function, which distills the model's complex, high-dimensional output into a single scalar value that represents its fitness. The nature of this function is task-dependent. For instance, in a symbolic reasoning task like Countdown, the reward is a sparse, outcome-only signal given only if the final arithmetic expression is correct. For a behavioral goal like conciseness, the reward is calculated based on the absolute difference in length between the model's response and a verified, short solution.
Once this scalar reward is obtained, the model is precisely reverted to its original state by regenerating the identical noise vector from the same seed and subtracting it from each layer's weights, again, in-place.
Third is the decomposed, cumulative parameter update. After all N population members are evaluated, the central parameters must be updated. The ES update rule is effectively a weighted sum of all the noise vectors, with weights determined by the normalized reward scores. Materializing this final update vector, a single vector with billions of dimensions, would create another memory bottleneck. The algorithm avoids this by decomposing the update. The main process iterates through the N seeds one last time. For each seed, it regenerates the corresponding noise vector, weights it by the normalized reward, and adds its contribution cumulatively to the main model's parameters, proceeding layer by layer. The full update vector is never explicitly formed in memory; it is built up piece by piece directly within the model's existing parameter tensors.
This algorithmic blueprint shifts the paradigm of LLM fine-tuning. It replaces the calculus-heavy machinery of backpropagation and the complexities of policy/value functions in RL with a highly parallelizable, forward-pass-only system whose primary challenge is memory orchestration. The paper's contribution is not simply suggesting ES as an alternative, but providing a concrete and novel algorithmic implementation that makes it a viable contender at a scale previously thought to be out of reach.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.24372

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 10.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 10.10.25 COMPLLM: COMPRESSION FOR LONG CONTEXT Q&A The Achilles' heel of the Transformer architecture is, and has always been, its self-attention mechanism. Its computational cost scales quadratically with the length of the input sequence. This isn't just a theoretical nuisance; it's a practical, wallet-draining barrier that makes processing truly long contexts, entire c
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 10.10.25
COMPLLM: COMPRESSION FOR LONG CONTEXT Q&A
The Achilles' heel of the Transformer architecture is, and has always been, its self-attention mechanism. Its computational cost scales quadratically with the length of the input sequence. This isn't just a theoretical nuisance; it's a practical, wallet-draining barrier that makes processing truly long contexts, entire codebases, lengthy legal documents, or full novels, prohibitively expensive. For a sequence of length N, the cost is proportional to N squared. Double the context, and you quadruple the cost of the initial processing pass.
The field has grappled with this by exploring "soft compression," where the long context is squished into a smaller set of latent vectors before being fed to the main LLM. While clever, most of these methods share a fundamental flaw: they treat the context as a single, monolithic block. They attempt to create one holistic, compressed representation of the entire input. In doing so, the compression step itself often becomes a quadratic problem, merely shifting the bottleneck rather than solving it.
This paper, CompLLM, introduces a refreshingly pragmatic and powerful conceptual shift. The core novelty isn't some labyrinthine new architecture, but a simple, elegant design choice: don't compress the context as a whole. Instead, the model first divides the long context into a series of small, independent segments and then compresses each one in isolation.
This seemingly minor decision is the key that unlocks three critical properties essential for real-world deployment.
First is efficiency. By breaking the context into small, fixed-size chunks (say, 20 tokens each), the quadratic attention cost is confined within these tiny segments. The overall cost of the compression step, therefore, scales linearly with the length of the full context. Instead of every token needing to attend to every preceding token across a 100K-token document, it only ever attends to its handful of neighbors within its local segment. This transforms the compression cost from a daunting quadratic curve into a manageable straight line. The main LLM's initial pass (the prefill) still faces a quadratic cost relative to the compressed sequence length, but if you reduce the sequence length by a compression factor C, you reduce this dominant prefill cost by a factor of C squared, a massive theoretical gain.
Second is scalability. The compression model is only ever exposed to short text segments during training. This means a model can be trained on relatively short sequences (e.g., 2K tokens) and then, at inference time, be confidently applied to contexts orders of magnitude larger (100K+ tokens). The model doesn't need to learn about long-range dependencies during compression; it simply learns a localized mapping from a sequence of standard Token Embeddings to a shorter sequence of synthetic "Concept Embeddings." The task of reasoning over the full, compressed context is left to the main LLM.
Third, and perhaps most impactful for practical applications, is reusability. Because each segment is compressed independently, its compressed representation can be cached. Imagine a system where a user queries a large knowledge base. If Document A is part of the context for ten different queries, its compressed segments need only be computed once. In a coding assistant, if a developer changes a single function, only the segments corresponding to that function need re-compressing, not the entire multi-file codebase. This is impossible with holistic compression schemes, where a change to a single token could alter the entire compressed output, forcing a complete recalculation.
The training itself is also novel. Rather than just forcing the model to produce the same final text, it uses a sophisticated distillation process focused on the LLM's internal computations. The objective isn't to match the final output, but to align the hidden activations layer by layer. For each layer in the LLM, a Smooth L1 loss is calculated between the activations produced using the compressed context and those from the original, uncompressed context. Critically, this loss is computed only on the tokens corresponding to the answer, providing a dense and highly relevant training signal. To handle the wide variation in activation magnitudes across the network's depth, the loss for each layer is normalized by the standard deviation of the teacher model's activations. This multi-layered, normalized alignment ensures the compressor learns to preserve the precise internal representations the LLM needs to formulate a correct answer.
In essence, CompLLM's novelty lies in reframing the problem. It abandons the quest for a perfect, singular compression of an entire document. Instead, it creates a library of compressed, independent, and reusable parts, leaving the final synthesis to the LLM. It's an engineering-first approach that prioritizes linear scaling and caching, tackling the quadratic bottleneck head-on in a way that is not just theoretically interesting, but practically deployable.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.19228

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 11.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 11.10.25 Less is More: Recursive Reasoning with Tiny Networks This paper introduces the Tiny Recursive Model (TRM), a sharp and compelling rebuttal to the recently proposed Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM). Where HRM built a complex, biologically-inspired system, TRM systematically(more or less) dismantles its core components, replacing them with simpler and more effec
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 11.10.25
Less is More: Recursive Reasoning with Tiny Networks
This paper introduces the Tiny Recursive Model (TRM), a sharp and compelling rebuttal to the recently proposed Hierarchical Reasoning Model (HRM). Where HRM built a complex, biologically-inspired system, TRM systematically(more or less) dismantles its core components, replacing them with simpler and more effective mechanisms. The novelty here isn't a single new technique, but a philosophical shift for a narrow problem domain: demonstrating that powerful iterative reasoning on puzzles can emerge from radical architectural minimalism.
First, TRM abandons the shaky theoretical scaffolding of its predecessor. HRM leans heavily on the Implicit Function Theorem to justify its gradient calculations, approximating a long recursive chain by only backpropagating through the final step. TRM correctly identifies this as a potential weak point, as the conditions for the theorem are unlikely to be met in practice. Its solution is brutally direct: define a full recursive block and backpropagate through the entire computational graph. This removes the need for fixed-point assumptions and grounds the learning process in a more stable, end-to-end optimization.
Second, the paper offers a reinterpretation of HRM's latent space. HRM posits two distinct latent variables, and zH​, corresponding to "low-level" and "high-level" reasoning. TRM’s novelty is to re-conceptualize this into something more intuitive: a current best-guess answer (y) and a latent reasoning scratchpad (z). The model's single network first iterates on the reasoning latent z, then uses the refined z to update its answer y. This demystifies the process, transforming it from an abstract hierarchy into a concrete, iterative refinement loop.
Building on this simplification, TRM collapses HRM’s two separate networks into a single, unified one. The insight is that the network's task, either refining reasoning or updating the answer, can be implicitly defined by its inputs. The presence of the original question x signals a reasoning step, while its absence signals an answer-update step. This input-driven conditioning allows a single set of weights to perform both functions, halving the parameter count.
Perhaps the most revealing, and ultimately limiting, novelty is the paper's "less is more" discovery. Instead of scaling up, TRM finds that shrinking the network, while increasing internal recursions, yields superior generalization. While framed as a strength, this is a significant red flag. In an era dominated by scaling laws, this "optimal tininess" casts serious doubt on the approach's scalability and relevance to real-world problems. It strongly suggests that, for the heavily augmented puzzle data tested, the model is merely a brittle memorization machine, whose main challenge is avoiding overfitting, not developing true reasoning capacity. This severely limits its applicability to complex, open-ended tasks where larger models are essential.
In essence, TRM’s contribution is an elegant but narrow exercise in simplification for a niche set of problems. Its success on these highly structured puzzles, propped up by extreme data augmentation, should be viewed with significant skepticism. The model's inverse scaling behavior confirms it is a highly specialized, and likely fragile, tool, not a general-purpose reasoner. While recursion is a powerful concept, the path to complex, human-like reasoning requires architectures that can effectively scale with both data and complexity. TRM, while a clever piece of engineering, is ultimately a detour, not a destination on that path.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.04871

points.title: Shmulik and Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 12.10.25
points.preview: Inadequacies of Large Language Model Benchmarks in the Era of Generative Artificial Intelligence In recent years, with the dramatic increase in the use of LLMs, a new generation of benchmarks has developed: tests designed to evaluate and compare models across a variety of tasks. Unlike classic benchmarks from the world of hardware and software, which were often standard and one-dimensional, the te
points.body:
Inadequacies of Large Language Model Benchmarks in the Era of Generative Artificial Intelligence
In recent years, with the dramatic increase in the use of LLMs, a new generation of benchmarks has developed: tests designed to evaluate and compare models across a variety of tasks. Unlike classic benchmarks from the world of hardware and software, which were often standard and one-dimensional, the tests for the GenAI world are characterized by great diversity in terms of approaches and methodologies. Some focus on accuracy or efficiency, others on creativity, reasoning, or even ethical aspects such as bias and fairness.
The paper examines 23 leading benchmarks, including MMLU (Massive Multitask Language Understanding - a general knowledge test in the style of standardized exams), HumanEval (assessment of Python code capabilities), LegalBench (law), MultiMedQA (medicine), SciBench (scientific problems), BIG-bench (general tests created by hundreds of researchers), AGIEval (real exams that models are tested on, like SAT), and also relatively new benchmarks like AgentBench and ToolBench that test interaction with external tools.
The core argument: Functionality and Integrity
The authors argue that every good benchmark should be examined in two dimensions:
Functionality - Does the test truly measure useful and relevant real-world capabilities?
For example, a test like MMLU can reveal if the model knows how to answer multiple-choice questions, but this does not necessarily indicate its ability to converse, help a programmer, or write creative content.
Integrity - Is the test immune to manipulation and cheating?
Here, the problem of overfitting arises: a large model that has learned the entire internet may simply memorize the answers appearing in the test (consciously or unconsciously), without developing a true generalization ability.
Key Problems Found
The study points to a long list of common inadequacies:
Response variability - The model's answers are extremely sensitive to small changes. The article gives an example: switching from the format "[A]" to "(A)" can lead to a completely different score, which may indicate the fragility of the test.
Single correct answer assumption - Most benchmarks assume there is only one correct answer. This might work in mathematics, but for questions about religion, culture, or ethics - the picture is much more complex. The researchers note that this is a serious distortion that makes reliable evaluation difficult.
Ignoring conversational context - Interaction with an LLM usually occurs in a continuous conversation, with follow-up questions, clarifications, and corrections. Most benchmarks settle for only the first answer.
Helpfulness vs Harmlessness tension - The article dwells on the gap between being helpful and being safe and harmless. For example, in medical tests like MultiMedQA, a model can "evade" an answer in the name of safety - and receive a high score, even though it did not provide a helpful answer to the user.
Biases in benchmarks built by LLMs - Some of the tests themselves were created by LLMs. For example, in M3KE, a model was used to create new questions. This leads to the problem of "LLM judging LLM," where the model both creates the test and is tested on it.
Scalability and reproducibility issues - Some tests are complex to run and not always well-documented. In SciBench, for example, installing the execution environment is complicated, which makes it difficult to rerun and re-examine.
Peer review - Out of 23 benchmarks, only 6 underwent peer review. This means that most were distributed without a full academic process, which raises questions about their reliability.
What do the researchers propose?
The researchers are not content with just criticism, but also offer a direction for a solution from the world of Cyber Security - the PPT Framework (People, Process, Technology):
People - Who builds the test and who uses it? Is there enough diversity among different experts (e.g., doctors, lawyers, programmers)?
Process - Is the process consistent, documented, and standard, yet flexible enough for the rapid development of models?
Technology - Are the infrastructures (Datasets, APIs, evaluation systems) reliable and scalable?
In addition, they emphasize the need to move from static to dynamic benchmarks, meaning dynamic tests that are updated and change over time, with periodic checks that identify new capabilities or deterioration in model behavior.
Critique of the Article
Despite its significant contribution, the article suffers from several weaknesses:
The proposed solutions remain high-level. The PPT Framework sounds good as a theory, but it lacks sufficient detail on how to actually build a benchmark using it.
The researchers did not run the benchmarks themselves, but rather settled for a review. An empirical step might have revealed additional problems.
Some of the problems the researchers point out are inherent to benchmarks and do not have a simple solution; it is not always clear what the researchers expected benchmark creators to do differently to solve the problem.
The article focuses mainly on relatively old benchmarks like HumanEval or MMLU, but does not review newer ones like Swe-Bench (which measures code bug fixes) or Aider Benchmark, which are already trying to address some of the mentioned failures.
There is a strong emphasis on cultural and ethical gaps, which is important, but not very relevant to a large part of the benchmarks, such as those that measure code writing abilities.
Summary
This is a critical and eye-opening article that provides a systematic mapping of the failures in the world of LLM benchmarks. It provides a structured language for problems familiar to many researchers, such as Response variability, Overfitting, and Lack of reproducibility, and suggests rethinking how tests are built: not just static, but dynamic, multi-dimensional, and based on a structured methodology.
Drawback: It contributes greatly to formulating the central problems in the field and presenting new directions of thought, but lacks practical tools and practical proposals for implementation. It is unclear which of the problems presented in the article are solvable and which are inherent to LLM benchmarks.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.09880

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 14.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 14.10.25 LIMITATIONS OF NORMALIZATION IN ATTENTION MECHANISM The deep learning community has long treated the limitations of softmax attention as an empirical reality, a known-unknown that we patch with architectural band-aids. We observe that as context length grows, attention "blurs," losing its ability to focus. We know that aggressively sharpening the distribution wi
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 14.10.25
LIMITATIONS OF NORMALIZATION IN ATTENTION MECHANISM
The deep learning community has long treated the limitations of softmax attention as an empirical reality, a known-unknown that we patch with architectural band-aids. We observe that as context length grows, attention "blurs," losing its ability to focus. We know that aggressively sharpening the distribution with low temperatures can destabilize training. But the fundamental "why" has often been met with intuition rather than formal proof. This paper changes that, trading heuristics for hard mathematical boundaries. Its novelty isn't a new attention mechanism, but rather a powerful theoretical framework for understanding the intrinsic, unavoidable limits of any attention normalization scheme.
The first core innovation is to formalize the concept of a "selection capacity." Imagine attention's job is to pick N important tokens from a context of length L. The paper derives a non-asymptotic upper bound on the representational distance between the aggregated "selected" tokens and the remaining "non-selected" ones. This isn't just an observation; it's a proof. The framework demonstrates that as the number of selected tokens (N) grows proportionally to the context length (L), the model's ability to distinguish the two sets is mathematically guaranteed to collapse. The distance between them must shrink, effectively blurring the line between signal and noise. This elegantly proves the existence of a "softmax bottleneck" from first principles, showing it's not a flaw of optimization but a fundamental property of the normalization itself.
The paper’s second, and perhaps most elegant, contribution is a novel geometric interpretation of attention's limits. It recasts token embeddings as points distributed on a high-dimensional sphere and asks a simple question: how many of the top-N selected tokens can a single attention head truly keep in focus at once? The authors derive a geometric bound that quantifies a hard ceiling on this "separability." It proves that even under idealized spatial assumptions, a single head cannot simultaneously distinguish all the tokens it selects. A fraction of them will inevitably lie outside a distinguishable region, blending with the background. This provides a rigorous, geometric argument for why multi-head attention is not just beneficial but necessary, not merely to capture diverse linguistic patterns, but to overcome the finite resolution of a single geometric viewpoint.
Finally, the work generalizes the well-known trade-off between attention sharpness and gradient stability. While practitioners know that lowering softmax temperature can lead to volatile gradients, the authors extend this into a universal principle. They derive a Jacobian bound for a general class of normalization functions, proving that any function that produces a sharply peaked, selective distribution will inherently suffer from high gradient sensitivity. This elevates a specific softmax-related trick into a fundamental law of attention mechanics, explaining the inescapable compromise between selectivity and trainability. In essence, the paper doesn't just give us a fish; it teaches us how to map the entire ocean, providing the mathematical tools to diagnose the limits of focus in any attention-based architecture.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.17821

points.title: Nurit and Mike’s Daily Paper: 14.10.25
points.preview: Nurit and Mike’s Daily Paper: 14.10.25 Between Underthinking and Overthinking: An Empirical Study of Reasoning Length and Correctness in LLMs In language models, we tend to spend tokens under the assumption that more thinking yields higher accuracy. In practice, in the paper we review here, a consistent pattern emerges: there is an optimal thinking length (in tokens). Too short: the model misses.
points.body:
Nurit and Mike’s Daily Paper: 14.10.25
Between Underthinking and Overthinking: An Empirical Study of Reasoning Length and Correctness in LLMs
In language models, we tend to spend tokens under the assumption that more thinking yields higher accuracy. In practice, in the paper we review here, a consistent pattern emerges: there is an optimal thinking length (in tokens). Too short: the model misses. Too long: the model ties itself in knots. The pattern is hill-shaped: accuracy rises… and then falls.
The empirical paper we review today systematically examines the relationship between the length of thought and the correctness of the answer.
So how did they construct the experiment?
They tested two small reasoning models (~1.5B parameters) on two mathematical datasets (GSM8K and MATH). For each question, they sampled 10 answers under identical configurations (e.g., temperature) and analyzed accuracy as a function of token length. Additionally, they labeled question difficulty by the success rate of the answers and reported their findings.
What were the results of the experiments (as noted, this is an empirical paper)?
A non-monotonic curve: accuracy increases with length up to a point, then decreases (the overthinking phenomenon).
And the opposite also occurs (underthinking): when the chain of thought is too short, the model skips necessary intermediate steps, especially on difficult problems, and produces a confident but incorrect answer because it wasn’t given sufficient “breathing room.” Incorrect answers tend to be longer than correct ones. The correct answer often already appears in the shorter samples.
“The first correct answer” is often short: for a large portion of questions, one of the shortest answers is already correct. Extending the chain beyond this point increases the risk of compounding errors. When measured against difficulty: the models “stretch” the length a bit when they face relative difficulty on easy or medium questions, but on truly hard questions there are no reliable clues as to the “right” length; the model fails to self-calibrate, and accuracy drops.
And what about the model’s confidence? When questions are “easier” for the model, perplexity decreases (greater confidence). On very difficult questions, a consistent decrease is not always observed, another indication that extending the reasoning does not necessarily improve accuracy.
What does work (a practice we adopt when working with the model)?
Short-first: start with a concise answer, and extend only when there is a genuine need.
Length-aware stopping: down-weight or discard chains that are abnormally long during self-consistency.
This paper has a few weaknesses: the first is the very small models that were tested and the limited datasets. The second is the way they “tagged” problem difficulty, which introduces an inherent bias that leans toward the observed outcome. Nevertheless, the main message of the paper is: don’t spend tokens on autopilot. Pay for clean logic, then extend only when it adds value.
arxiv.org/abs/2505.00127
The Art of Markovian Thinking
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.00127

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 17.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 17.10.25 The Markovian Thinker The central challenge in scaling up the reasoning ability of LLMs is a computational one. The standard approach, often called Long-Chain-of-Thought, treats the entire history of a model's reasoning as its current state. For an LLM to generate the next word in its thought process, it must attend to every single word that came before it. This
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 17.10.25
The Markovian Thinker
The central challenge in scaling up the reasoning ability of LLMs is a computational one. The standard approach, often called Long-Chain-of-Thought, treats the entire history of a model's reasoning as its current state. For an LLM to generate the next word in its thought process, it must attend to every single word that came before it. This creates a computational bottleneck where the cost grows quadratically with the length of the thought. Thinking for twice as long doesn't take twice the effort; it takes four times the effort. This paper sidesteps the race to optimize the model architecture and instead proposes a radical novelty: redesigning the very environment in which the model thinks.
The core innovation is a paradigm called Markovian Thinking. The name comes from the Markov property, a mathematical concept stating that the future depends only on the present, not on the entire past. The paper translates this idea into the world of LLM reasoning. Instead of forcing the model to remember its entire thought history, it trains the model to function by only considering a small, constant-size "state" to inform its next step. This fundamentally decouples the total length of the reasoning process from the size of the context the model must process at any given moment, transforming the computational scaling from quadratic to linear.
This paradigm is instantiated through a clever Reinforcement Learning (RL) environment named Delethink. The novelty lies in how it structures the generation process. Instead of producing one continuous, ever-growing stream of text, the model is forced to think in a sequence of fixed-size chunks. The process works as follows:
Chunked Generation: The model begins with the initial query and generates text, but only up to a predefined limit (e.g., 8,000 tokens). This is the first "chunk." Within this chunk, the model's attention mechanism works as usual.
Context Reset: Once the chunk limit is reached, the environment performs a hard reset. The entire generated text is deleted from the model's context.
The Textual State: The only information that survives this reset is a small "carryover"—specifically, the last few tokens from the chunk that was just deleted. This snippet of text becomes the textual, Markovian state.
Re-initialization: A new prompt is constructed for the model by combining the original, unchanging query with this textual carryover. The model then begins generating the next chunk, with its entire "memory" of the previous chunk's work condensed into that small snippet of text.
The true novelty is not just in this mechanical process but in how the model learns to use it. Through reinforcement learning, the policy is trained to write a maximally informative textual state at the end of each chunk. It must learn, under the pressure of maximizing its reward (i.e., solving the problem correctly), to distill the essence of its progress into a compact form that is sufficient to seamlessly continue its line of reasoning after the context is wiped. It is not hand-prompted to summarize; rather, the environment's constraints force it to discover this state-passing behavior organically.
The training itself is mathematically grounded in a policy gradient method adapted for this chunked environment. The learning objective is designed so that the final reward of a trajectory, a binary score for whether the problem was solved correctly, is credited back to every single token generation step across all chunks. This is achieved by calculating a per-chunk loss, which closely follows the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) framework. The core idea is to estimate an "advantage" for the entire multi-chunk reasoning trace. This advantage is a simple, normalized score of the final reward. This single advantage score is then used to update the policy for every step within every chunk, effectively telling the model that the entire sequence of actions, including the creation of intermediate textual states, was either good or bad as a whole. This formulation elegantly solves the credit assignment problem in this fragmented reasoning process, ensuring the model learns to connect its actions within a chunk to the final, distant outcome.
From a computational standpoint, this method is transformative. Since the model's attention is only ever applied to a single, constant-size chunk, the quadratic cost never compounds. The total compute for a thought process of any length becomes a simple linear function: the cost of processing one chunk multiplied by the number of chunks. Similarly, peak memory usage remains constant, as the full reasoning trace never needs to be stored in the KV cache simultaneously. The paper’s novelty is not a new model, but a new way of thinking about thinking itself, one that is inherently more scalable by design.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.06557

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 20.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 20.10.25 Shaping Explanations: Semantic Reward Modeling with Encoder-Only Transformers for GRPO Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce explanations that are not just correct but also pedagogically sound is a significant challenge. The conventional methods for this alignment process are often a trade-off between two extremes. On one hand, using a powerful "LLM-a
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 20.10.25
Shaping Explanations: Semantic Reward Modeling with Encoder-Only Transformers for GRPO
Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) to produce explanations that are not just correct but also pedagogically sound is a significant challenge. The conventional methods for this alignment process are often a trade-off between two extremes. On one hand, using a powerful "LLM-as-a-judge" to score outputs is slow, computationally expensive, and can be prone to biases. On the other, relying on simple, keyword-based metrics like ROUGE is efficient but shallow, as it rewards lexical overlap rather than deep conceptual understanding. This paper carves out a novel third path.
The central innovation is the use of a small, efficient, encoder-only transformer as a specialized semantic reward model. Instead of judging an explanation with natural language or counting keywords, this approach operates in the abstract space of vector embeddings. The methodology is elegantly simple: both the model-generated explanation and a ground-truth reference explanation are passed through the encoder, which converts each piece of text into a dense, numerical vector. The cosine similarity between these two vectors is then calculated. This single numerical score, which captures the conceptual and structural alignment between the two explanations, becomes the primary reward signal. A high similarity indicates that the generated text is semantically close to the expert-written ideal, incentivizing the model to learn the underlying reasoning structure, not just surface-level features.
This semantic reward mechanism is deployed within the Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) framework. GRPO is a reinforcement learning algorithm that, for each prompt, scores a group of generated responses. It then updates the model's policy based on how each generation's score compares to the average score of the group. This provides a stable and efficient way to apply the reward signal.
The paper’s proposed reward function is not monolithic; it’s a multi-component system designed to shape the model's output holistically. The total reward is a sum of four distinct signals:
Semantic Similarity: The core component, calculated as described above using the encoder model. To make the signal more discriminative, the final reward is an adjusted cosine similarity, where a baseline similarity (calculated from an average of random explanation embeddings) is subtracted from the raw score. This is a crucial detail, as it prevents the model from being rewarded for generating text that is generically similar to any explanation.
Factual Accuracy: A simple binary reward for whether the final answer is correct.
Structural Correctness: A rule-based reward that checks if the output is formatted correctly with the required XML tags.
Reasoning Transparency: A reward for including a non-empty "chain-of-thought" within designated tags.
In essence, the novelty of this work lies in its pragmatic and effective solution to reward shaping. It demonstrates that a small, computationally inexpensive encoder model can provide a semantically rich reward signal that is more nuanced than lexical metrics and more efficient and stable than using a large LLM-as-a-judge. By integrating this semantic score into a multi-faceted reward function within the GRPO framework, the paper presents a powerful methodology for guiding LLMs toward producing higher-quality, conceptually-aligned explanations.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.13081

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 25.10.25
points.preview: Paper name: DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression Today’s paper tackles the fundamental bottleneck of modern AI: the quadratic compute cost of processing long text sequences. As context windows grow, attention mechanisms become prohibitively expensive. This paper asks a radical question: why process text as text at all? What if we could compress a 10K-token essay into a 1K-token image and hav
points.body:
Paper name: DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression
Today’s paper tackles the fundamental bottleneck of modern AI: the quadratic compute cost of processing long text sequences. As context windows grow, attention mechanisms become prohibitively expensive. This paper asks a radical question: why process text as text at all? What if we could compress a 10K-token essay into a 1K-token image and have a model read that instead?
This is the core idea of "optical compression." DeepSeek-OCR is a proof-of-concept designed to explore the feasibility and boundaries of this idea. The central mathematical concept is the "compression ratio": the number of ground-truth text tokens divided by the number of vision tokens the model uses. The entire paper is a methodological exploration of how high this ratio can be pushed before the signal is lost.
The novelty lies almost entirely in the DeepEncoder, a new vision encoder architecture purpose-built for this task. It’s not a standard Vision Transformer; it's a clever, three-stage cascade designed to manage computation at high resolutions.
Here’s how it works:
Stage 1: High-Res Perception (Local Attention) First, the high-resolution document image (e.g., 1Kx1K) is fed into a SAM-base model. This initial stage uses only windowed (local) attention. This is a critical choice. It can process the image at its full resolution, generating a large number of initial patch tokens (e.g., 4K tokens), but without the quadratic memory explosion of global attention. Its job is to handle the raw visual perception cheaply.
Stage 2: The Bottleneck (Convolutional Compression) Next, the 4K tokens from the SAM block are passed through a simple 2-layer convolutional module. This acts as a hard, non-learned (in the attention sense) compressor. It performs a 16x spatial downsampling, crushing the token count from 4K down to just 256. This is the "optical compression" step made manifest, a hard bottleneck.
Stage 3: Knowledge Extraction (Global Attention) Only after this aggressive compression are the remaining 256 tokens fed into a CLIP-large model. This component uses dense global attention, allowing it to perform the "deep understanding" and reasoning. Because it only sees 256 tokens, this computationally brutal step becomes perfectly manageable.
This serial, hybrid-attention design is the main architectural takeaway. It intelligently separates the task: cheap local attention handles the high-bandwidth pixel data, a convolutional bottleneck forces compression, and expensive global attention works only on the resulting low-bandwidth latent representation.
The other half of the system is the decoder, a 3B Mixture-of-Experts model (DeepSeek3B-MoE). Its novelty is not in its architecture but in its task. It is trained to be a "decompressor"—to take the 256 compressed vision tokens from the encoder and reconstruct the original, multi-thousand-token text sequence. The paper investigates whether such a non-linear "decompression" mapping can even be learned, especially by a relatively small model.
The final piece of methodological novelty is the multi-resolution training. To explicitly test the limits of the compression ratio, the model is trained on several input modes simultaneously.
Native Modes (Tiny, Small, Base, Large): These are for research. The Tiny (512x512) and Small (640x640) modes resize (as in, rescale) the original image, which degrades the data. This forces the model to learn to decode text from blurry, low-information inputs, directly testing the boundaries of high compression. The Base and Large modes pad the image, preserving its content for more practical, high-fidelity use cases.
Dynamic Modes (Gundam, Gundam-Master): This is the system designed for practical performance. It's a tiling method that combines n local-view tiles with one global-view image. This "secondary windowing" allows the model to process ultra-high-resolution documents (like newspapers) that wouldn't fit in memory even with the DeepEncoder's cascade.
In short, DeepSeek-OCR isn't just another OCR model. It's a structured investigation into the "channel capacity" of vision as a medium for text. The novel encoder architecture is the tool it uses to probe this question, efficiently managing computation while forcing text information through a compressed visual bottleneck.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.18234

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 26.10.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 26.10.25 Imbalanced Gradients in RL Post-Training of Multi-Task LLMs This paper lands a sharp, methodological critique on a practice we all take for granted: multi-task post-training by mixing datasets. The standard approach, even with uniform sampling across tasks, implicitly assumes all tasks are "created equal" in the optimization landscape. This paper's core novelty
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 26.10.25
Imbalanced Gradients in RL Post-Training of Multi-Task LLMs
This paper lands a sharp, methodological critique on a practice we all take for granted: multi-task post-training by mixing datasets. The standard approach, even with uniform sampling across tasks, implicitly assumes all tasks are "created equal" in the optimization landscape. This paper's core novelty is to rigorously prove this assumption is false, demonstrating that Reinforcement Learning (RL) post-training suffers from a severe "imbalanced gradient" problem.
The authors' primary method is diagnostic. Instead of just looking at the final, aggregated gradient, they meticulously isolate the gradient contributions from each task. This isn't trivial. A naive measurement of a batch gradient's norm is a mathematically biased estimator of the true gradient's squared norצ. To get a clean signal, the authors employ an unbiased estimator: they split each task's batch into two independent halves and compute the cross-product of their respective gradients. This gives them a valid, low-noise way to track the true squared gradient norm for each task throughout training.
The finding is stark: gradient magnitudes between tasks are wildly different, with squared norms varying by factors of 15x to 33x. This mathematically demonstrates that the final update vector is completely dominated by the high-gradient tasks. In effect, these tasks get an implicitly massive learning rate, while low-gradient tasks are starved, regardless of the uniform sampling strategy.
The paper's second, and more clever, methodological thrust is to dismantle the most obvious counter-argument: "What if large gradients are good? What if they just signal more room for improvement?" To disprove this, they test the hypothesis in two ways.
First, they devise a direct "learning gain" metric, defined as the smoothed increase in training reward over a window of steps. They then plot this gain against the gradient norms. The result is a total mismatch. Tasks with the largest gradients (e.g., Code, Arithmetic) are repeatedly shown to have the lowest actual learning gains.
Second, they conduct an algorithmic refutation. They design a "gradient-proportional sampling" strategy. Instead of sampling tasks uniformly, they sample them in proportion to their gradient magnitude (using a softmax with temperature). The logic is simple: if large gradients truly signaled high learning potential, this strategy should improve overall average performance by focusing effort where it's most "needed." The fact that this strategy shows no advantage, and can even degrade performance, is the final nail in the coffin. It proves the gradient magnitude signal is not just uncorrelated with learning gain, but actively misleading as a balancing metric.
Finally, the authors methodically eliminate other potential "simple" explanations. They check if the gradient norm correlates with the advantage function, finding it only holds within a single task but fails entirely across tasks. They find no meaningful correlation with training reward (i.e., task difficulty) or with prompt/response lengths.
The paper's novelty isn't a new SOTA algorithm. It's a fundamental, mathematically-grounded diagnosis of a flaw in our standard methodology. It warns that naively mixing datasets for multi-task RL is an optimization trap, biased by gradient mirages that have no bearing on actual learning.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.19178

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 28.10.25 PROVABLE SCALING LAWS OF FEATURE EMERGENCE FROM LEARNING DYNAMICS OF GROKKING
points.preview: Why do networks suddenly "grok," or generalize, long after they've perfectly memorized the training data? Most explanations have been high-level metaphors: "generalization circuits" competing with "memorization circuits," or Bayesian priors taking over. This paper provides something entirely new: a precise, mathematical framework called Li2 (Lazy, independent, interactive) that derives grokking no
points.body:
Why do networks suddenly "grok," or generalize, long after they've perfectly memorized the training data? Most explanations have been high-level metaphors: "generalization circuits" competing with "memorization circuits," or Bayesian priors taking over.
This paper provides something entirely new: a precise, mathematical framework called Li2 (Lazy, independent, interactive) that derives grokking not from analogy, but directly from the gradient dynamics of a 2-layer network. It's not a story about what happens, but a provable mechanism for how it happens. The Li2 framework proposes that feature learning is a three-stage process, with each stage defined by a fundamental shift in the nature of the backpropagated gradient.
Stage I: Lazy Learning (The "Overfitting" Prerequisite)
The novelty here is a complete re-framing of the initial memorization phase. The paper shows that when training begins, the network acts "lazy." The hidden layer remains essentially random, and only the top (output) layer learns. This layer quickly finds a solution to map the random hidden features to the correct labels—it memorizes the data.
In most theories, this is the problem. Here, it is the solution. The paper proves that this initial overfitting is a necessary prerequisite for feature learning. It's only after the top layer has learned this mapping that the gradient flowing back to the hidden layer (let's call it G_F) stops being pure noise.
Crucially, with weight decay, G_F doesn't just go to zero. Instead, it "leaks" a small but highly structured signal that, for the first time, carries information about the target labels. This structured gradient is the fuel for the next stage.
Stage II: Independent Feature Learning (Hunting for Maxima)
This is the paper's core insight. The leaked gradient from Stage I has a special structure: it decouples the learning dynamics of all the hidden neurons. Each neuron now receives a gradient that depends only on its own activation. They all begin learning in parallel, completely independent of each other.
The central mathematical novelty is this: the paper proves that this independent dynamic is exactly equivalent to each neuron performing gradient ascent on a shared, underlying "energy function," which we can call E. This function E measures the nonlinear correlation between the input data and the target labels. The "features" that emerge in the network are nothing more than the local maxima of this energy landscape.
Using group arithmetic tasks (like modular addition) as a testbed, the paper completely characterizes this landscape. It proves that the nonlinearity of the network shatters a simple landscape into a rich set of distinct, generalizable solutions. These local maxima are the irreducible representations (or "irreps") of the task, the fundamental building blocks of the problem, like the Fourier bases for modular arithmetic.
Stage III: Interactive Feature Learning (Refinement and Diversification)
The "independent" stage is an approximation that holds while the hidden layer is still close to random. Once neurons have moved toward the energy maxima, they begin to interact, and the gradient dynamics change again. The paper proves two new mechanisms kick in.
First, a repulsion force. The full gradient now includes terms that actively push neurons away from each other if they are learning the same feature. This encourages diversity and specialization, forcing the network to explore different local maxima.
Second, top-down modulation. As a set of features (irreps) is successfully learned, the gradient G_F is again transformed. This new gradient effectively modifies the energy landscape itself, "zeroing out" the maxima that have already been found. This forces the network to stop wasting time on learned features and focus its gradient ascent only on the missing pieces.
From Dynamics to Scaling Laws
This framework's final novel contribution is connecting the gradient dynamics to the data. The shape of the energy landscap E is dictated by the training samples.
With sufficient data, the landscape is stable, and its local maxima are the generalizable irreps. With insufficient data, the paper shows this landscape changes, the generalizable maxima vanish, and new local maxima corresponding to memorization solutions appear. The grokking "phase transition" is thus explained from first principles: it is the provable data threshold at which the generalizable maxima in the energy landscape become stable.
Li2 reframes grokking not as a mysterious switch, but as a tangible, three-stage mechanical process: overfitting enables an independent hunt for features, which is then refined by interaction and forced diversification, all governed by the provable geometry of an underlying energy function.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.21519

points.title: Mike's Daily Paper Review: 31.10.25
