points.body:
Mike's Daily Paper Review: 31.10.25
Language Models are Injective and Hence Invertible
Finally, a review that features words from my Calc 2 course, Measure Theory (Lebesgue!!), and also a Probability course (almost surely).
Language models are injective and therefore invertible. The common intuition about Transformers is wrong. We assume that components like non-linear activations, attention, and especially layer normalization (like LayerNorm or RMSNorm) are inherently information-losing, necessarily "collapsing" different inputs into the same representation. This paper presents a rigorous mathematical proof that in standard decoder-only models, the exact opposite is true.
The mapping from an input prompt to the final continuous representation of its last token is structurally one-to-one. No information is lost with probability one. This probability lives in the model's parameter space, meaning, a non-invertible transformer can be sampled from this space with probability zero. This is what mathematicians love: throwing all the errors into a set of measure/probability 0. The core of the argument is not statistical but analytical. The authors treat the entire transformer, from the embedding to the final layer, as one single composite function. A very, very complex one, but a function nonetheless.
They prove that this function possesses a very strong property of mathematical 'smoothness' known as real-analyticity: a property shared by simple polynomials, which guarantees the function is infinitely differentiable and behaves in a very predictable and constrained manner. This property is key. The authors define a "collision function" that measures the distance between the representations of two different prompts. A collision, i.e., information loss, occurs only if this function outputs zero.
Because the transformer's mapping is real-analytic, the collision function is as well. A fundamental theorem states that for any non-trivial real-analytic function, the set of inputs that causes it to output zero is infinitesimally small, that is, it has "measure zero." This "bad set" is not an entire region or volume, but rather a tiny collection of isolated points and curves in the model's parameter space.
The authors first prove that the collision function is non-trivial by constructing a simple "witness"  that is, a set of parameters (mostly zeros) where a collision cannot occur. This confirms that the set of "bad" model parameters, those that could ever cause a collision, is a pathological exception of measure zero.
Standard initialization samples parameters from a continuous distribution, so the probability of "falling" into this "bad" set is zero. The model is injective "almost surely" from the moment of initialization. The paper's most significant contribution is the proof that this property is preserved during training. The authors analyze the gradient descent update step itself, showing that it too is a real-analytic mapping. This update mapping can stretch, bend, and warp the "good" parameter space, but it cannot perform the pathological kind of collapse required to map the entire "good" space (which has measure 1) onto the negligible "bad" collision space (which has measure 0). That is, training does not break injectivity.
To make their insights operational, they introduce SIPIT, an algorithm that constructively proves (unlike Shannon's theorem) the theory by inverting the representations. It leverages the transformer's causal structure to work sequentially. To find the first token of the prompt given only its hidden representations, SIPIT feeds every possible item from the vocabulary into the model and checks which one produces the target hidden representation for that position. Because of the proven injectivity, only one token can match. It then uses this known first token as a prefix and repeats the process, iterating through the vocabulary to find the unique second token, and so on. This sequential search, which is brute-force but provably-correct, reconstructs the exact input prompt, token by token.
This paper fundamentally reframes our understanding of transformers, proving mathematically that they are information-preserving and invertible systems. This theoretical insight, practically demonstrated by the SIPIT algorithm, challenges long-held assumptions and opens new avenues for model representation analysis.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.15511

points.title: Can you Steer your Network to Any Direction?: Why Networks are Almost Always SurjectiveMike's daily paper review: 01.11.25
points.preview: Can you Steer your Network to Any Direction?: Why Networks are Almost Always SurjectiveMike's daily paper review: 01.11.25 ON SURJECTIVITY OF NEURAL NETWORKS: CAN YOU ELICIT ANY BEHAVIOR FROM YOUR MODEL? This paper poses a question so fundamental it's shocking it hasn't been the focus of safety research: is a trained neural network surjective? In plain terms, can any arbitrary output be generated
points.body:
Can you Steer your Network to Any Direction?: Why Networks are Almost Always SurjectiveMike's daily paper review: 01.11.25
ON SURJECTIVITY OF NEURAL NETWORKS: CAN YOU ELICIT ANY BEHAVIOR FROM YOUR MODEL?
This paper poses a question so fundamental it's shocking it hasn't been the focus of safety research: is a trained neural network surjective? In plain terms, can any arbitrary output be generated by some input? If the answer is yes, then any safety-critical model-from LLMs to diffusion models-is in principle capable of generating any harmful output, and safety training is just a game of making the required inputs hard to find.
The authors' first and most critical novel contribution is the formalism. Proving surjectivity for all possible parameters is impossible (e.g., zero-weights). Proving it for some parameters is uninformative. The paper's brilliant move is to ask: is surjectivity "almost always" true? That is, does the set of non-surjective parameter configurations have a Lebesgue measure of zero? If so, any training process that involves randomness is "exceedingly unlikely" to land on a non-surjective model.
The paper's second novelty is its toolkit: differential topology. Instead of statistical analysis, it treats networks as smooth maps and applies high-powered mathematical tools to analyze their global input-output properties. This yields two incredibly powerful, purely conceptual proofs for modern architectural blocks.
The first, and most elegant, proof tackles Pre-Layer Normalization. This component, defined as g(x) = f(LN(x)) + x, is the backbone of almost every modern Transformer. The paper proves it is always surjective for any continuous function f. The method is a beautiful application of Brouwer's Fixed Point Theorem. To show g(x) = y has a solution for any target y, they reformulate the problem. They are looking for an input x* such that x* = y - f(LN(x*)). This is a fixed-point problem for a new function F(x) = y - f(LN(x*)). The key insight is that LayerNorm's output is bounded. Because f is continuous, f(LN(x)) is also bounded. This means the F(x) function is just the constant target y plus a bounded "wobble". For any y, one can find a ball in the input space large enough that this function F maps the ball back into itself. Brouwer's theorem guarantees that such a function must have a fixed point. That fixed point is the x* we are looking for. This proof is devastatingly general and identifies the residual connection + x as the structural linchpin of this unavoidable surjectivity.
The second method tackles components like MLPs with LeakyReLU and Linear Attention, which do not have the simple + x structure. For these, the paper uses a more advanced tool: degree theory. The core idea is homotopy, or continuous deformation. The method shows that a complex network (like a LeakyReLU-MLP) can be continuously deformed into a simple function, like an identity map, whose surjectivity is obvious. A topological invariant called the "Brouwer degree"-which conceptually counts the number of pre-images for a point-is shown to be non-zero for the simple function. Because this degree cannot change during a continuous deformation (as long as solutions don't "escape to infinity"), the complex network must have the same non-zero degree. A non-zero degree implies that a pre-image must exist for any output. The paper's technical work here is proving that this "containment" holds, and that solutions do not escape to infinity during the deformation. This method successfully proves surjectivity for Linear Attention by induction, analyzing the map from one token to the next.
Crucially, the analysis is sharp enough to identify non-surjective components. Standard ReLU-MLPs are not surjective (their range is limited to the positive orthant). Standard softmax-based attention is not surjective (the normalization prevents arbitrarily large outputs in certain directions).
The core takeaway is that the vulnerability of modern generative models is not a bug in the training data or optimizer-it is a fundamental, provable mathematical property of the architectures we have chosen.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2508.19445

points.title: Sagiv and Mike's Daily Paper Review: 03.11.25
points.preview: Sagiv and Mike's Daily Paper Review: 03.11.25 Agent Learning via Early Experience The article we will review today deals with the issue of training autonomous agents and improving their capabilities throughout their interaction with the environment in which they operate. The classic way to deal with the problem is using RL algorithms based on positive and negative reinforcements (rewards) to make
points.body:
Sagiv and Mike's Daily Paper Review: 03.11.25
Agent Learning via Early Experience
The article we will review today deals with the issue of training autonomous agents and improving their capabilities throughout their interaction with the environment in which they operate.
The classic way to deal with the problem is using RL algorithms based on positive and negative reinforcements (rewards) to make the agent act in a certain way. In practice, the rewards are very sparse and difficult to collect, thus hindering the learning process of these models. To deal with the rewards challenge, there have been several uses of SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) where experts in the content world (domain experts) provide a desired result that serves as a goal. An expert is a person with a deep understanding of a certain content world who is an authority in the field. For example, a historian who knows how to formulate a proper answer to a question asked by a person in the field of history. Against the goal, proposed by the expert, an error calculation is performed according to which the model's weights are updated, usually this is Entropy Loss or KL divergence. Bringing many such examples by experts is very expensive and difficult to execute, and this is the main challenge.
The way the article's authors deal with the problem is by defining two new loss functions for the model:
Implicit World Modeling: The model tries to predict which state each action will lead to, given a certain state. A loss function that minimizes the error of predicting the next state resulting from an existing state and an action. The goal here is to build a better representation of the world and allow the model to "understand" how it behaves.
Self Reflection: The model receives as input a given state along with an expert's action, providing the desired output. With this input, the model asks a question "Why is the expert's action better than action 'a'?" and thus gets the chain of thought - 'c'. Finally, the loss function maximizes the probability for the explanation 'c' and the expert's action by maximizing likelihood. In effect, the model learns to receive a certain state and provide an expert's answer and the explanation for the answer. In this way, the model changes its action probabilities to match the expert's actions and for the explanation for them to be logical.
In the article, they give a precise prompt to instruct the agents on how to improve and learn:
Provide a detailed self-reflection as an internal monologue that demonstrates your reasoning process for the current situation…
The full prompt contains the way to write the answer, and how the model should analyze the desired text.
The article presents very broad improvements across a considerable range of tasks with a relatively low amount of expert data. The tasks included planning and research, tool use, and web search. The researchers tested the training process on three models: Llama-3.2-3B, Llama-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B and showed improvements over the standard SFT method.
In addition to the improvements relative to SFT, the researchers tested the effect of an additional RL algorithm, GRPO, after the training process with the new losses they proposed. The researchers showed a significant improvement from adding the losses compared to training with RL alone. The researchers show that even with very little expert data, the proposed losses achieve better results than classic SFT. The comparison is nice and presents the trade-off between a large amount of expert data and success.
The article presents an interesting way to fine-tune agents with relatively limited expert data and opens a door to the field of self-supervised learning, which may develop even further. Additional inspiration from the worlds of State Representation Learning could constitute even more dramatic improvements.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.08558

points.title: Adiel, Natan, and Mike's Daily Paper Review: Review 535, 06.11.25
points.preview: Adiel, Natan, and Mike's Daily Paper Review: Review 535, 06.11.25 Statistical Runtime Verification for LLMs via Robustness Estimation In recent years, transformer-based LLMs with many neural network layers have become a central component in modern AI systems. However, they inherit the vulnerability of those same neural networks to adversarial inputs: tiny changes in the input that can cause the mo
points.body:
Adiel, Natan, and Mike's Daily Paper Review: Review 535, 06.11.25
Statistical Runtime Verification for LLMs via Robustness Estimation
In recent years, transformer-based LLMs with many neural network layers have become a central component in modern AI systems. However, they inherit the vulnerability of those same neural networks to adversarial inputs: tiny changes in the input that can cause the model to err severely. When LLMs are embedded in critical systems such as medicine, autonomous vehicles, and law, such a vulnerability can lead to catastrophic results. The central question is: how can we ensure these models are reliable? This question gains added urgency when we expect to receive an answer at runtime when they are deployed in the field.
One option to ensure the reliability of models is formal verification. Tools like Marabou, Alpha-Beta Crown, and PyRat are capable of mathematically proving that for a specific input domain, the network is completely robust to adversarial input, meaning it does not err in its prediction for that domain. There are 2 problems with implementing formal verification in the context of runtime and LLMs. The first is that the verification problem is NP-Hard, and therefore its runtime, even for simple networks, can be exponential, and for an LLM with billions of parameters - impossible. The second is that these methods require access to the internal parameters of the network (white-box access), which is usually not possible in critical systems that operate via API and are protected by intellectual property.
So, what can be done? This is where statistical verification comes into the picture: instead of proving the model is safe for all inputs, statistical verification estimates the probability that the model will not err on a given input. Specifically, the authors were inspired by an existing method for statistical verification called Robustness Measurement and Assessment (RoMA) (paper). In the original paper, RoMA was used to test the reliability of networks trained for the task of image classification. In the current paper, the authors adapted RoMA to the world of LLMs, to measure the robustness of LLM networks with millions of parameters, with the intention that it could be applied to LLMs for monitoring even at runtime.
How does RoMA work? It checks the output stability on a specific input point of the network. Assume the input is the sentence "This movie is really great", and the network outputs that the sentiment of that sentence is positive with 85% confidence. Now, RoMA replaces a word in the sentence, for example, to "this movie is really good", and looks at the score the network gives to the runner-up class (the network's confidence that the sentence is negative). Why does it focus on the runner-up class? Because a high score means the model is close to deciding differently and making a mistake. RoMA performs hundreds of such changes for the same point, collects all the runner-up scores, and checks if their distribution is normal (Anderson-Darling Goodness-of-fit test).
If the scores are normally distributed, one can easily calculate the probability that a random perturbation will cause the model to get confused - this is the PLR score, or Probabilistic Local Robustness. If the perturbations are not normally distributed but are close to normal, RoMA attempts a statistical transformation called Box-Cox in order to "normalize" the distribution. The advantages of RoMA are:
It does not require internal access to the model.
It does not make special statistical assumptions.
It is statistically valid.
It operates at a runtime that allows it to be used as a real-time monitoring tool for LLMs.
But wait, how do we know RoMA's calculation is accurate? To address this question, the authors compared RoMA to a formal verification algorithm named Exact Count, which is capable of calculating the PLR precisely. The authors tested the performance of both methods on synthetic neural networks and on the famous Acas XU benchmark. The results showed that RoMA's accuracy was within a 1% deviation range of the formal algorithm. In this experiment comparing RoMA and Exact Count, the runtime dropped from 24 hours to 16 minutes.
The main challenge the authors faced was adapting RoMA to the text domain. Perturbations for text are not random noise that can be easily added to images; rather, one must replace words in the sentence such that the sentence retains its meaning. To this end, the authors chose to search for words similar to the ones being replaced in the embedding space (specifically word2vec).
The idea is to replace words in the sentence with similar words and estimate the probability of a change in the model's prediction for that sentence. The level of similarity is controlled by an epsilon (\epsilon) parameter that determines the maximum distance between words in the embedding space.
The authors chose to demonstrate the adaptation of RoMA to text on BERT models that were fine-tuned on the GLUE SST-2 dataset for the task of Sentiment Analysis. They used a standardly trained model, and two checkpoints during training were taken:
The best-performing model.
The model at the final training point, which served as a basis for comparison.
Afterward, the robustness of these two models was tested on the evaluation set. They found that the better model demonstrated 97.2% robustness, and the final model demonstrated 96.6% robustness. These results align with previous findings that suggest better-performing models are also more robust. In terms of runtime, RoMA processed 50% of the evaluation set in under 15 minutes, and the entire dataset in under 36 minutes. These times allow RoMA to run in the background and serve as a real-time monitoring tool for LLM models deployed in real-world applications.
Additionally, the authors also checked the robustness of the models in each category, i.e., the robustness of the two models for positive sentences and the robustness for negative sentences. Here, varied results were obtained, showing a difference between the models in per-category robustness. Finally, they tested the models' robustness to random changes at the typo level (character level), and found that the first model was 94.61% robust and the final model was 93.84% robust to character-level changes.
We will mention previous work reviewed here that also examined the nature of LLMs - Chameleon. The similarity between the two approaches is that both modify the sentences but aim to preserve the meaning in order to test the model's robustness. In RoMA, the magnitude of the change is defined by the distance parameter from the original input point, epsilon , and in C-BOD, the magnitude of the change is defined by the distortion parameter mu. The difference between the approaches is their goal: Chameleon is more intended for offline evaluation of benchmarks for testing LLMs, whereas RoMA can also be implemented in real-time for monitoring LLM-based systems.
In conclusion, RoMA enables reliability monitoring of LLM systems at runtime, without access to the model's parameters and at a low computational cost.
Paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2504.17723
Paper's Code: https://github.com/adielashrov/trust-ai-roma-for-llm

points.title: Disentangling Memory from Reason: A Look Inside the Transformer's Loss LandscapeMike's daily paper review: 08.11.25
points.preview: Disentangling Memory from Reason: A Look Inside the Transformer's Loss LandscapeMike's daily paper review: 08.11.25 FROM MEMORIZATION TO REASONING IN THE SPECTRUM OF LOSS CURVATURE We all know transformers are two-faced. One face is the brilliant reasoner that generalizes complex patterns. The other is a dull parrot that recites training data verbatim, a behavior that’s increasingly problematic fo
points.body:
Disentangling Memory from Reason: A Look Inside the Transformer's Loss LandscapeMike's daily paper review: 08.11.25
FROM MEMORIZATION TO REASONING IN THE SPECTRUM OF LOSS CURVATURE
We all know transformers are two-faced. One face is the brilliant reasoner that generalizes complex patterns. The other is a dull parrot that recites training data verbatim, a behavior that’s increasingly problematic for privacy and copyright. The trillion-dollar question is how these two capabilities are represented in the model’s weights. Are they tangled together, or can they be pulled apart?
This paper offers a novel and powerful method for doing just that. The core innovation is to take a classic concept,the curvature of the loss landscape, and apply it in a completely new, inverted way.
The old idea, applied to individual data points, is that memorized examples create extremely sharp, high-curvature spikes in the loss landscape. A tiny nudge to the weights, and the model suddenly fails to recall the exact point, causing the loss to skyrocket. Generalization, by contrast, is associated with flat, low-curvature basins.
This paper's breakthrough is to flip this intuition on its head by looking at the average curvature across a whole population of data.
The central method is a decomposition of the model’s weights using Kronecker-Factored Approximate Curvature (K-FAC). This is a technique for tractably estimating the model's average loss curvature. In simple terms, for any given weight matrix, K-FAC builds a "map" of its curvature by looking at the covariance of activations flowing into the layer and the covariance of gradients flowing back out of it. This process generates a set of "curvature components," which are new directions in weight space, ordered from highest average curvature to lowest.
FAC is a highly efficient technique for approximating the Fisher Information Matrix (FIM), a mathematical object that captures the dataset-average loss curvature. Decomposing this FIM approximation allows the authors to find the primary directions of curvature, which they then sort from highest to lowest. In simple terms, for any given weight matrix, K-FAC.
Here is the paper’s key insight: when you average across thousands of data points, those sharp, individual spikes for memorized data all point in different directions. They are idiosyncratic. In the grand average, they cancel each other out, contributing only to a flat, low-curvature background.
General reasoning mechanisms, however, are different. Because they represent shared structures used by countless different data points, their curvature directions add up coherently. On average, they stand out as the dominant, high-curvature directions.
So, the paper proposes a fundamental inversion:
Population-Level High Curvature = Generalization & Reasoning
Population-Level Low Curvature = Memorization & Idiosyncratic Facts
The authors don't just theorize this; they demonstrate it. They feed a model both memorized text and "clean" text and measure which curvature components "light up" (activate) the most. They find a clean separation: the low-curvature components activate far more strongly for memorized data, while the high-curvature components are preferred by clean, general data. This K-FAC basis provides a natural coordinate system for separating memory from reasoning.
This new understanding leads directly to a novel, unsupervised "unlearning" procedure. If memorization lives in the flatlands, you can just delete them. The method involves re-projecting the model's weights into this K-FAC basis and then "zeroing out" the components with the lowest curvature mass. This effectively silences the parts of the model dedicated to recitation, without needing a "forget set" of data to train against.
Finally, the paper extends this method beyond a simple memory/reasoning binary. It uses the K-FAC decomposition as a new analysis tool to map a whole spectrum of behaviors. By measuring which tasks activate which parts of the curvature spectrum, they find a fascinating continuum:
Verbatim Memorization (lives in low-curvature directions)
Arithmetic & Fact Retrieval (behave like memorization, relying heavily on low-curvature directions)
Open-Book & Logical Reasoning (behave like generalization, relying on high-curvature directions)
The novelty here isn't just a new pruning technique. It's a fundamental shift in perspective. By averaging curvature with K-FAC, the authors have found a "basis" in the model's weights that appears to naturally disentangle shared, reusable mechanisms from the noisy, specific details of the training set. This provides a powerful new lens for understanding, and editing, what our models actually know.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.24256

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 10.11.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 10.11.25 Forget BIT, It is All about TOKEN: Towards Semantic Information Theory for LLMs This paper argues that Shannon’s classical information theory, built on the ‘bit’, is fundamentally insufficient for understanding Large Language Models. Shannon’s theory solves the technical problem of transmitting symbols accurately (Level-A), but it cares nothing for meaning. This
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 10.11.25
Forget BIT, It is All about TOKEN: Towards Semantic Information Theory for LLMs
This paper argues that Shannon’s classical information theory, built on the ‘bit’, is fundamentally insufficient for understanding Large Language Models. Shannon’s theory solves the technical problem of transmitting symbols accurately (Level-A), but it cares nothing for meaning. This paper proposes a new “Semantic Information Theory” built on the ‘token’ to solve the semantic problem (Level-B): how to precisely convey desired meaning. It’s a ground-up reformulation, providing a new mathematical language to describe what LLMs are actually doing.
The novelty starts with a new probabilistic model. The paper recasts an LLM as a "discrete-time channel with feedback and state." In this model, the prompt (as a sequence of semantic vectors) is the input, the generated text is the output, and each newly generated token is "feedback" that alters the channel's state for the next token's generation. This is a powerful conceptual shift.
From this model, the paper introduces three novel, structure-agnostic measures to describe the entire LLM lifecycle:
Directed Rate-Distortion Function (Pre-training): This isn't Shannon's classic function. Here, "rate" is measured using directed information, the information flow from the prompt to the output. "Distortion" is measured as the KL divergence, which quantifies how far the model's next-token prediction is from the "correct" (human-labeled) token. The function, therefore, describes the trade-off between these two: what is the minimum amount of information the model must draw from the prompt to generate an output that is semantically close (low distortion) to the ground truth? This theoretically links the cross-entropy loss objective directly to information theory.
Directed Rate-Reward Function (Post-training): This adapts the first concept for RLHF. It asks: what is the minimum directed information flow (rate) from the prompt needed to generate a sequence that achieves a score above a certain threshold from a human-preference reward model? This elegantly reframes the goal of alignment as a constrained information-theoretic optimization problem.
Semantic Information Flow (Inference): For a single generated sequence, this measure (based on directed information density) tracks the accumulation of information from the prompt at each step of the token-generation process. The paper models this flow as a sub-martingale, a mathematical process that, on average, is expected to increase or stay the same. This implies information from the prompt is non-decaying and cumulative. This concept leads to a "semantic information capacity," a theoretical basis for prompt engineering: a good prompt is one that maximizes this information flow.
The paper also rebuilds the foundation of this theory: the token itself.
Semantic Vector Space: It formally defines the embedding space as a probabilistic inner product space (a high-dimensional unit sphere) where cosine similarity is the fundamental measure of semantic relationships.
Semantic Distance: To compare two entire language embedding spaces (e.g., English vs. Chinese), it proposes using the Gromov-Wasserstein distance. This sophisticated metric doesn't just compare individual words; it measures how much the entire internal geometry (the web of all pairwise cosine similarities) of one space must be distorted to align with the other.
Optimal Embedding: The paper frames embedding as an optimization problem. The goal is to create a vector that maximizes the backward directed information, how much the vector for the current token knows about all future tokens in the sequence, given all past tokens.
The most significant contribution is deriving the Transformer architecture from first principles. The paper first proposes a general mathematical definition for any autoregressive LLM: a Time-Varying Vector Autoregression (TV-VAR) process. This is an abstract model where the next token vector is a complex weighted sum of all previous token vectors, and, critically, the coefficient matrices (the weights) can change at every single time step.
Then, it delivers the punchline: the Transformer architecture is shown to be a specific, simplified case of this general TV-VAR model. The paper derives it by decomposing the impossibly complex, time-varying coefficient matrix into two parts:
A time-invariant parameter matrix (which corresponds to the Value matrix, W_v, in a Transformer).
A time-variant scalar weight (which corresponds to the attention score).
The attention mechanism itself is thus given a new theoretical justification. The attention score is calculated using a bilinear form (the Query and Key matrices, W_q and W_k) that measures the "semantic relevance" between the current context and a past token. Applying softmax to these relevance scores, which the paper justifies using Jaynes' maximum entropy principle as the "most uncertain" valid distribution, yields the final attention weights.
In essence, this work provides a potential answer to why the Transformer works, framing it not as a feat of empirical engineering, but as a principled, special-case solution to a more general, information-theoretic model of semantic prediction.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.01202

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 13.11.25 NESTED LEARNING: THE ILLUSION OF DEEP LEARNING ARCHITECTURES
points.preview: Today’s paper fundamentally challenges the "stack of layers" paradigm that has defined deep learning for decades. The core, novel idea is to re-imagine a model not as a deep architecture, but as a deep stack of nested optimization problems. This new paradigm is called Nested Learning (NL). It proposes that every component of a model, from its recurrent state to its optimizer, is its own learning m
points.body:
Today’s paper fundamentally challenges the "stack of layers" paradigm that has defined deep learning for decades. The core, novel idea is to re-imagine a model not as a deep architecture, but as a deep stack of nested optimization problems.
This new paradigm is called Nested Learning (NL). It proposes that every component of a model, from its recurrent state to its optimizer, is its own learning module, complete with its own objective and, most importantly, its own update frequency.
The authors use the analogy of brain waves to explain this. Think of your model as having different clocks:
Low-Frequency: The "pre-training" step. In a standard Transformer, the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) parameters are updated during this phase and then frozen. Their update frequency drops from 1 (during training) to 0 (at inference).
High-Frequency: The recurrent state of an RNN (or the attention cache in a Transformer). These parameters update at every single token to reflect the immediate context.
The "illusion" of deep learning, they argue, is that we flatten this multi-frequency hierarchy into a single "architecture" and treat "training" as a monolithic, one-time event. NL provides a mathematical framework to deconstruct this and treat learning as a continuous, multi-timescale process.
The first "aha" moment from this framework is the deconstruction of standard optimizers. The paper's novel claim is that an optimizer is a learning module.
Gradient Descent (GD): In NL, this isn't just an update rule. It's a 1-level optimization problem. The network's weight matrix W is reframed as an "associative memory" M. The paper shows that the standard GD update W_t+1 = W_t - ... is mathematically equivalent to taking a single gradient step to solve the optimization problem min L(M(K); V), where K is the input data and V is the "local surprise signal" (the gradient).
GD with Momentum: The authors argue this is a 2-level nested optimization.
Level 1 (Slow): The main network weights, W, which are updated by the momentum term: W_t+1 = W_t + m_t+1. This is the outer optimization loop.
Level 2 (Fast): The momentum term itself, m. The paper reveals that the momentum update rule (m_t+1 = ...m_t...) is also a single gradient step solving its own, inner optimization problem. The momentum buffer m is an associative memory (a simple linear one) whose entire job is to compress the history of past gradients.
This reframing is the paper's first set of novel proposals. If the momentum term is just a simple memory, why not make it more powerful? They suggest:
More Expressive Memory: Replace the simple linear update rule for m with a full-blown MLP. This creates "Deep Momentum Gradient Descent (DMGD)," where the optimizer itself is a deep model that learns to capture complex gradient dynamics, rather than just linearly averaging them.
More Expressive Objectives: Instead of the "momentum" in the optimizer being just a simple memory that accumulates gradients (like a Hebbian rule), the paper proposes changing its internal objective. The idea is to turn momentum into an error-correcting system, so it tries to predict the next gradient and updates based on the prediction error. This change allows the momentum memory to "manage its capacity" more intelligently. It learns to ignore redundant information and focus on new, surprising gradients, making the history storage much more efficient.
The second "aha" moment is applying this same logic to architectures. Take a linear attention model (or RNN). The NL perspective splits this into two distinct optimization problems operating at different frequencies:
Level 1 (Outer Loop): The "pre-training" of the query, key, and value projection matrices (W_q, W_k, W_v). This is a slow-frequency optimization that runs over the entire dataset and then stops.
Level 2 (Inner Loop): The recurrent state update M_t = M_t-1 + v_t * k_t. The paper shows this update is also a single step of a GD algorithm, solving an inner optimization problem (min <M*k, v>) at every single token. This state M is an associative memory learning to map keys to values within the immediate context.
NL provides a clean separation between these two processes. We typically backpropagate through the Level 2 process to update the Level 1 parameters. NL suggests these are two distinct learning systems.
The paper's primary methodological contribution, the Continuum Memory System (CMS), is the direct and elegant application of this entire theory. In a standard Transformer, the FFN (MLP) block is the static, "long-term memory," frozen after pre-training (update frequency = 0). CMS replaces this single, static FFN with a chain of FFNs, each operating at a different update frequency.
This means you can have:
A Low-Frequency FFN that stores the original pre-trained knowledge. Its parameters never get gradient updates after pre-training.
A Mid-Frequency FFN that accumulates gradients for, say, 1000 steps and then updates its weights once.
A High-Frequency FFN that accumulates gradients for only 16 steps and then updates its weights.
This creates a "continuum" of memory, directly inspired by synaptic consolidation in the brain. It allows a model to continually learn and consolidate new information into its parameters at different timescales, without catastrophically forgetting the core knowledge stored in the low-frequency blocks.
Finally, the HOPE model is the flagship architecture built from these principles. It combines a self-modifying recurrent module (which learns its own update rule) with the multi-frequency Continuum Memory System for knowledge storage.
By reframing everything as a nested optimization, this paper doesn't just propose a new model; it suggests a new axis for model design, moving beyond just adding layers to adding levels of learning defined by update frequency.
https://research.google/blog/introducing-nested-learning-a-new-ml-paradigm-for-continual-learning/
Semantic Spies: Slaying String-Matching with Embeddings

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 17.11.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 17.11.25 Extracting alignment data in open models This paper presents a sharp, novel, and slightly terrifying methodology for extracting high-value alignment data from open-weight models. The work’s core novelty is not just in what it does, but in how it re-frames the very concept of data extraction, moving it from the brittle world of text matching to the robust domain
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 17.11.25
Extracting alignment data in open models
This paper presents a sharp, novel, and slightly terrifying methodology for extracting high-value alignment data from open-weight models. The work’s core novelty is not just in what it does, but in how it re-frames the very concept of data extraction, moving it from the brittle world of text matching to the robust domain of semantic meaning.
The authors’ first key insight is to identify the model’s "tell." They observe that chat templates, the special tokens like <|user|> and <|assistant|> used to format conversations, are almost exclusively introduced during the post-training alignment phase. A base model has never seen them. By prompting an aligned model with just the prefix of its chat template (e.g., <|endoftext|><|user|>), the model is aggressively cajoled into the specific data distribution it learned during alignment. It’s less a prompt and more a key that unlocks a specific "mode" of generation, causing the model to output data that strongly resembles its Supervised Finetuning (SFT) or Reinforcement Learning (RL) training sets.
This is where the paper's primary methodological innovation comes into play. The authors argue, quite persuasively, that decades of memorization research focusing on string-matching (like Levenshtein distance) are obsolete for this task. An SFT dataset's value is not in its literal text (e.g., "Emily was 54 inches tall") but in its structure and patterns (e.g., a step-by-step math word problem). A model can regurgitate a sample with trivial changes, like swapping names or numbers, which would completely fool a string-matching metric, resulting in a low similarity score and a false sense of security.
The paper’s method replaces this entirely. It proposes that the only metric that matters is approximate semantic memorisation.
The extraction pipeline is a clean, two-pronged attack. First, on the "attacker" side, they generate thousands of samples by simply feeding the chat template prefix to the target model. Second, on the "victim" side, they take the entire true alignment dataset and use a high-quality embedding model (in this case, gemini-embedding-001) to convert every single sample into a high-dimensional vector. These vectors, which represent semantic meaning, are stored in a vector search database.
The "gotcha" moment is simple: the attacker embeds each of their generated samples and queries the database, asking, "What is the closest semantic match to this sample in the entire alignment dataset?" The "closeness" is not measured by text, but by the dot product of their vectors.
This "embedding-first" approach is what allows the paper to claim that previous string-matching methods undercount this form of leakage by at least an order of magnitude. It finds matches that are semantically identical but textually different, which is precisely the kind of data (e.g., reasoning templates, safety formats) that holds all the competitive value.
Perhaps most surprising is the application of this method to RL. The SFT objective, which maximizes sequence likelihood, is a natural bedfellow for memorization. The PPO objective used in RL is not. Yet, the authors show that prompting an RL-trained model with its chat template causes it to regurgitate training prompts (and even generate correct reasoning traces it was never explicitly trained on). They confirm this by showing that the log-likelihood of the RL training prompts increases significantly in the final RL-tuned model compared to the base model. This suggests the RL process, while optimizing a reward, is also inadvertently "burning in" the very prompts it was trained on.
Finally, the authors demonstrate the potency of their method by taking it full circle. They use this pipeline to extract a large synthetic dataset from one model, then use only this extracted data to fine-tune a new base model. The fact that this new model recovers a meaningful amount of the original's performance is the ultimate proof: the extracted data is not a statistical echo but a high-fidelity, high-utility copy of the original secret sauce.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.18554

points.title: Ruth and Mike’s Daily Paper: 19.11.25 Physics-informed neural networks: A deep learning framework for solving forward and inverse problems involving nonlinear partial differential equations
points.preview: Deep learning is the prevailing method today for the analysis of various forms of data, including speech, images, and audio. It has revolutionized fields such as computer vision (CV) and natural language processing (NLP). However, a significant challenge arises in the scientific realm, where datasets are often limited or sparse. Thus, it appears that the scientific community has been somewhat side
points.body:
Deep learning is the prevailing method today for the analysis of various forms of data, including speech, images, and audio. It has revolutionized fields such as computer vision (CV) and natural language processing (NLP). However, a significant challenge arises in the scientific realm, where datasets are often limited or sparse. Thus, it appears that the scientific community has been somewhat sidelined in the broader narrative of deep learning's evolution.Interestingly, the intersection of deep learning and scientific inquiry is not a recent development. As early as 1998, researchers proposed the use of artificial neural networks (ANN) with a single hidden layer to solve ordinary and partial differential equations, mathematical constructs that underpin a multitude of scientific disciplines.
The authors of the paper we review today introduce a physics-informed neural network (PINN). The main idea is to utilize the well-known automatic differentiation paradigm to differentiate the network with respect to its input coordinates and model parameters. These derivatives are then used to incorporate the constraints containing the prior knowledge, the physical laws governing the system. This integration of the physics into the network allows generalization even with limited data.
They divide the use cases of this method into two classes of problems: data-driven solutions and data-driven discovery of partial differential equations. The former focuses on inferring hidden states given fixed parameters, while the latter aims to identify the optimal parameters that best describe observed data.
Data-driven solutions
In the paper they separate algorithms to solve PDEs for Continuous and Discrete Time Models. As an example for a continuous time model the one-dimensional Schrödinger equation is given. The authors used classical conventional simulating tools to generate data (which is smaller in learning terms, few hundred to few thousand points), which they then fed into the network. They introduced the boundary condition, the initialization and the equation itself into the loss function; MSEu for initial/boundary data and MSEf for enforcing the PDE structure. So, even though the training data was limited, they managed to get the network to accurately model the solution for the nonlinear Schrödinger equation.
It is worth noting that in scenarios with a small amount of training data, the authors chose L-BFGS, a quasi-Newton, full-batch gradient-based optimization algorithm, instead of the usually used stochastic gradient descent,  Although it requires more memory, it usually converges faster due to the second-order derivative inclusion (inverse Hessian). However, it can be less robust and more susceptible to noise than SGD.
One drawback of the continuous time neural network models considered so far stems from the need to use a larger number of collocation points for the model loss in order to enforce physics-informed constraints. Consequently, they introduce discrete time models. Discrete time models allow utilizing fewer collocation points while still incorporating the physical constraints.
There, they embed the classical Runge-Kutta solver (Iserles 2009), enforcing its equations into the network loss, and allowing for a chosen higher order of the solver at a small cost. The number of Runge-Kutta stages and the time-step size are chosen parameters. This implicit Runge-Kutta approach allows solving the PDEs at larger time intervals while still preserving accuracy, leveraging the advantages of both time-stepping methods and neural network architectures without compromise.
They demonstrate this by solving the Allen-Cahn equation  (Allen and Cahn 1972) along with periodic boundary conditions, sampling points at a particular time, and predicting the solution at a later time step, achieving a high accuracy prediction.
Data-driven discovery
In these sections the goal is to learn the parameters rather than the solution of the model. They demonstrated this using the Navier-Stokes operator solving the equations in two dimensions (2D). They inserted the equations to the loss as well as the continuity equation for incompressible fluids that describes the conservation of mass of the fluid.
The authors again prepared data set via classical methods, and this time, they also examined a case with 1% uncorrelated gaussian noise.
Not only was the network able to accurately predict the parameters, it also provided a qualitatively accurate prediction of the entire pressure field in space-time in absence of any training data on the pressure itself. The outcome of deducing a continuous quantity of interest from measurements by harnessing the principles of physics is an illustration of the possibilities that physics-informed neural networks are capable of.
To demonstrate the discrete time model solver here again, the authors show an example of the Kortewegde Vries Equation. They use a similar Runge-Kutta based method, this time incorporating the unknown parameters to be learned, and changing the equation a bit to take as input two distinct temporal snapshots.
Despite these differences, and the large temporal gap between the two training snapshots, the method is able to correctly identify the unknown parameters regardless of whether the training data is corrupted with noise or not. Showcasting again the ability of the network to encompass the underlying physics.
Final note
As a final note, the authors emphasize that the proposed methods should not be seen as mere substitutes for traditional numerical techniques used in solving partial differential equations (such as finite elements, spectral methods, etc.), but instead as significant advancements that can elevate their capabilities further and it is a pity to overlook them.
https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0021999118307125
References
Lagaris, I. E., Likas, A., & Fotiadis, D. I. (1997). Artificial Neural Networks for Solving Ordinary and Partial Differential Equations. arXiv:Physics/9705023v1.
Raissi, M., Perdikaris, P., & Karniadakis, G. E. (2018). Physics-Informed Neural Networks: A Deep Learning Framework for Solving Forward and Inverse Problems Involving Nonlinear Partial Differential Equations. Journal of Computational Physics.
Iserles, A. (2009). A First Course in the Numerical Analysis of Differential Equations. Cambridge University Press.
Allen, S. M., & Cahn, J. W. (1972). Ground state structures in ordered binary alloys with second neighbor interactions. Acta Metallurgica, 20(3), 423–433.
