points.title: Aviad and Mike’s Daily Paper: 21.11.25 LiteAttention: A Temporal Sparse Attention for Diffusion Transformers
points.preview: This paper tackles a critical bottleneck in video generation: Diffusion Transformers(DiT) produce stunning videos but are painfully slow, with attention mechanisms consuming up to 80% of inference time in some models. A single 5-second video can take 30 minutes to generate, even on state-of-the-art GPUs. The core innovation here is recognizing that attention sparsity patterns exhibit temporal cohe
points.body:
This paper tackles a critical bottleneck in video generation: Diffusion Transformers(DiT) produce stunning videos but are painfully slow, with attention mechanisms consuming up to 80% of inference time in some models. A single 5-second video can take 30 minutes to generate, even on state-of-the-art GPUs. The core innovation here is recognizing that attention sparsity patterns exhibit temporal coherence across the denoising sequence - tiles deemed unimportant at timestep t remain unimportant at timestep t+δ.
The existing approaches fall into two camps, each with fundamental limitations:
Dynamic Methods: These recompute sparse patterns at every denoising step, profiling which attention computations to skip. The overhead is substantial - you're constantly re-evaluating what to skip, introducing both computational cost and estimation errors.
Static/Caching Methods: These exploit redundancy across steps by caching features or using fixed sparsity patterns. But static patterns can't adapt to content, and caching still requires storing and approximating intermediate values with significant memory overhead.
LiteAttention introduces a third paradigm: evolutionary computation skips. The breakthrough insight is deceptively simple - identify skippable tiles once during early denoising, then propagate these skip decisions forward through the entire trajectory. This gives you the content-adaptivity of dynamic methods (patterns derive from actual attention statistics) with the efficiency of static methods (no per-step re-evaluation), plus complete elimination of computation for marked tiles.
The method builds on FlashAttention's tiling mechanism and the sparse online softmax from SpargeAttention. In standard sparse attention, when a tile's local maximum is much smaller than the cumulative maximum (specifically, when max(m_local - m_ij) ≤ -ε), that tile's contribution is exponentially suppressed and can be skipped.
LiteAttention's QK-Skip algorithm maintains a persistent Skip-Mask that updates at each timestep. Initially, no tiles are marked. As denoising progresses, tiles that meet the skipping condition get flagged, and this decision persists. The key observation: once a tile is marked skippable at step t, it typically remains skippable at step t+1, t+2, and beyond. The Skip-Mask gradually fills up, and by later timesteps, you're skipping 40-70% of attention computations without any repeated profiling overhead.
The paper also introduces an Accumulated-Error Calibration mechanism. Not all timesteps are equal - errors at earlier steps propagate more strongly to final outputs. LiteAttention divides timesteps into segments and assigns different error thresholds (ξ-τ, ξ, ξ+τ) to each, ensuring tighter bounds on early steps where accuracy matters most. These thresholds can be set through a quick calibration process.
The implementation is production-ready, built atop FlashAttention3 for NVIDIA H100 GPUs. The Skip-Mask uses run-length encoding for efficiency under high sparsity. The evaluation on Wan2.1 and Wan2.2 video diffusion models shows that LiteAttention achieves 40-42% sparsity while maintaining video quality indistinguishable from full attention (FA3). Critically, it delivers better runtime than competing methods (SparseVideoGen, RadialAttention) despite their higher nominal sparsity, because LiteAttention achieves complete iteration elimination - no softmax, no memory transfers - while other methods only skip partial computations.
The empirical result that runtime reduction scales nearly one-to-one with skipped computation confirms the method's efficiency. With proper calibration and optimized tile ordering, the authors suggest reaching 70% sparsity is achievable.
This work doesn't just accelerate diffusion transformers - it introduces a new optimization axis for sequential generative models based on exploiting temporal structure in sparsity itself.
Try it yourself: https://github.com/moonmath-ai/LiteAttention
Read the paper: https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.11062

points.title: CAT: A Test-Time Adaptive TransformerMike's daily paper review: 22.11.25 ATTENTION AND COMPRESSION IS ALL YOU NEED FOR CONTROLLABLY EFFICIENT LANGUAGE MODELS
points.preview: The central problem in efficient transformers is not just the quadratic cost of attention. It is the inflexible, apriori nature of the solution. Architectures built on sparse, sliding, or linear attention lock in a specific quality-compute trade-off at training time. This paper introduces the Compress & Attend Transformer (CAT), a novel architecture whose primary innovation is test-time controllab
points.body:
The central problem in efficient transformers is not just the quadratic cost of attention. It is the inflexible, apriori nature of the solution. Architectures built on sparse, sliding, or linear attention lock in a specific quality-compute trade-off at training time. This paper introduces the Compress & Attend Transformer (CAT), a novel architecture whose primary innovation is test-time controllability. It allows a single trained model to dynamically adjust its efficiency, interpolating between near-dense quality and high-speed, low-memory operation.
The architecture's novelty lies in its two-component design: a compressor and a decoder, both of which are standard transformers. The core method is simple. The input sequence is first divided into chunks of a fixed size C. The compressor, a bidirectional transformer, processes each chunk in parallel and independently, collapsing each C-token chunk into a single, learned vector representation.
The decoder, a standard causal transformer, then models the sequence. Its attention mechanism is the crucial part. To predict a token within a given chunk i, the decoder attends to two distinct sets of information:
The preceding tokens within the local chunk i.
The list of compressed vector representations from all previous chunks (chunk 1 to i-1).
This design is a significant departure from two common efficiency paradigms. Unlike linear attention (or RNNs), CAT does not suffer from a fixed-size state bottleneck. The "state" is the growing list of past compressed chunks, which scales gracefully at a rate proportional to the sequence length divided by the chunk size, not the full sequence length. Unlike architectures that compress the entire past into a single representation, CAT provides the decoder with granular, chunk-by-chunk representations, avoiding a single point of information loss.
The second, and perhaps most significant, novelty is the mechanism for controllable efficiency. CAT is not just one model. It is a single model trained to be multiple models. During training, a chunk size C is randomly sampled from a predefined set (e.g., 4, 8, 16, 32) for each batch. A learnable indicator token is prepended to the sequence to inform the model of the current operational chunk size. This forces the single set of weights to learn how to effectively compress and decode at multiple levels of granularity.
At inference time, the user can simply select a chunk size and provide the corresponding indicator token to activate the desired quality-compute profile. A small chunk size (like 4) means more compressed representations are kept, leading to higher recall and quality, but more memory usage. A large chunk size (like 32) drastically reduces the sequence length for the decoder, offering significant speed and memory savings.
The final piece of novelty is the scalable training implementation. A naive, sequential calculation for each chunk would be computationally prohibitive, scaling cubically with the number of chunks. CAT avoids this with a clever parallelization trick. First, all chunk representations are computed in parallel using the compressor. Second, a modified sequence is created by interleaving the raw token chunks with their corresponding compressed representations. A single, standard transformer decoder is then applied to this entire interleaved sequence. The magic is in a custom attention mask. This mask ensures that tokens within a chunk i can only "see" their local predecessors in chunk i and the compressed representations of past chunks. They are explicitly masked from seeing the raw tokens of past chunks. This design allows the entire training process to be parallelized, reusing KV-cache for the compressed tokens and achieving a scalable training complexity that is a factor of C times better than a dense transformer.
During generation, this architecture is highly efficient. The KV cache only needs to store the compressed representations of past chunks and the local tokens of the current chunk. Once a chunk is fully generated, its raw tokens are evicted from the cache, it is compressed into a single vector, and that vector is appended to the KV cache, ready for the next chunk.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.05313

points.title: Omri and Mike's daily paper review: 23.11.25 Tiny Model, Big Logic: Diversity-Driven Optimization Elicits Large-Model Reasoning Ability in VibeThinker-1.5B
points.preview: In recent years, reasoning has become a central frontier in improving LLM capabilities—especially following the breakthrough of Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Models like o1, R1, and o3 represent a growing shift toward building systems intended not just to answer, but to “think.” Recently, we’ve seen more and more research groups attempting to enhance reasoning by encouraging greater diversity in generat
points.body:
In recent years, reasoning has become a central frontier in improving LLM capabilities—especially following the breakthrough of Chain-of-Thought (CoT). Models like o1, R1, and o3 represent a growing shift toward building systems intended not just to answer, but to “think.” Recently, we’ve seen more and more research groups attempting to enhance reasoning by encouraging greater diversity in generated solutions or higher levels of creativity in the model’s thought process, hoping this ultimately leads to more correct answers.
The authors propose a new framework called Spectrum-to-Signal. The idea is to divide training into two separate stages: first, during the SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) phase, the model is encouraged to produce a wide “spectrum” of possible solutions by optimizing for Pass@K rather than Pass@1. The motivation is that unlike Pass@1—which only checks the model’s most likely answer—Pass@K rewards situations where at least one of several sampled responses is correct. This means the model is not penalized for alternative or unconventional attempts; instead, it is encouraged to generate a broader set of potential solutions. This pushes the model toward a richer, more diverse, and sometimes more creative reasoning space, rather than converging too quickly on a single conservative path.
Next comes the RL (Reinforcement Learning) stage, whose role is to extract the “signal” from that spectrum generated during SFT—that is, to identify and reinforce the reasoning trajectories that actually lead to correct answers. To achieve this, the authors use a modified version of GRPO, which prioritizes questions for which the model exhibits uncertainty, based on a statistical measurement of uncertainty (entropy). According to the authors, separating diversity generation in SFT from signal amplification in RL enables a relatively small model to achieve significantly improved reasoning performance.
During the SFT phase, the authors divided the mathematical domain into four subdomains—algebra, geometry, calculus, and statistics—and trained a separate model on each. Throughout training, they monitored performance using Pass@K and selected the checkpoint that produced the highest diversity per domain. Afterward, they merged the four specialist models into a single one using weight averaging—a linear combination of all model parameters, with each domain contributing equally. The result is a unified SFT model that incorporates reasoning strengths from all subdomains.
In the RL phase, the authors introduce MaxEnt-Guided Policy Optimization (MGPO)—an extension of GRPO designed to focus learning on questions where the model is not confident in its answers. For each question, they generate G samples and compute the empirical success rate:
p_c = (1/G) * Σ I(r_i = 1)
where i indexes the sample out of the G generated responses, and r_i is the reward for that response (1 for correct, 0 for incorrect).
A value of p_c close to 0.5 indicates maximal uncertainty. To quantify how far the model is from that uncertainty state, they compute the deviation using KL Divergence against a 0.5 distribution:
D_ME(p_c || 0.5) = p_c * log(p_c / 0.5) + (1 - p_c) * log((1 - p_c) / 0.5)
They then define a learning weight:
w_ME = exp(-λ * D_ME)
This weight is high when p_c ≈ 0.5, and low when the model almost always fails (p_c ≈ 0) or almost always succeeds (p_c ≈ 1). The weight is multiplied into the GRPO advantage, meaning gradient updates focus primarily on mid-difficulty questions—not too easy and not too hard—the region where the model has the greatest potential to learn and improve efficiently.
The less convincing part of the paper lies in the evaluation. Although the authors report Pass@1, they actually compute it using 64 samples for math, 8 for coding, and 16 for knowledge. This means that even if only one out of dozens of sampled attempts succeeds, it still counts as a Pass@1 success. Meanwhile, performance numbers for comparison models were taken from their original papers—where evaluation was typically done using greedy decoding or a single sample. As a result, the proposed model receives a substantial and unfair advantage.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.06221

points.title: Yaniv and Mike's daily paper review: 24.11.25 LeJEPA: Provable and Scalable Self-Supervised Learning Without the Heuristics
points.preview: A new paper by Randall Balestriero and (the one and only) Yann LeCun makes a bold claim: many of the tricks we rely on for training large self-supervised foundation models might be unnecessary. Instead, they show that one humble mathematical object, the Gaussian distribution, is all we need. A quick reminder - foundation models are colossal neural networks pretrained on internet-scale data, able t
points.body:
A new paper by Randall Balestriero and (the one and only) Yann LeCun makes a bold claim: many of the tricks we rely on for training large self-supervised foundation models might be unnecessary. Instead, they show that one humble mathematical object, the Gaussian distribution, is all we need.
A quick reminder - foundation models are colossal neural networks pretrained on internet-scale data, able to find powerful representations that can solve virtually any downstream task in their domain. Such models are widely available for text, images, robotics and recently even geospatial analysis. But training such beasts is messy, requiring a full toolbox of heuristics just to keep training from collapsing: stop-gradients, teacher–student setups, complex schedulers, and many more.
In LeJEPA (Latent-Euclidean Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture), the authors propose a theoretically grounded and empirically validated framework for self-supervised learning that eliminates the need for heuristics. They prove that for both linear and nonlinear probes, the optimal embedding distribution for minimizing downstream prediction risk is the isotropic Gaussian. An intuitive visualization can be found in the image below, linear separation is much easier on an isotropic gaussian. To enforce this in practice, they introduce Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg), a simple, scalable regularizer that constrains embeddings to match that ideal distribution. Since matching multi dimensional distribution is computationally expensive, they do this in practice by projecting the embedding into random 1D vectors (image below). The insight is that such projections are matching if and only if the original multi dimensional distribution is matching.
Combining SIGReg with the standard JEPA predictive loss yields LeJEPA, a “lean” training recipe with:
A single trade-off hyperparameter (λ),
Linear time and memory complexity,
Stable training across model types (ResNets, ViTs, ConvNeXts),
And no heuristics or architectural asymmetries.
Despite this simplicity, the results are striking. When trained on small, domain-specific datasets like Galaxy10, LeJEPA trained on 11k samples outperforms DINOv3 trained on billions of natural images. The framework also scales seamlessly to billion-parameter models while maintaining stable optimization.
In essence, LeJEPA revives an idea long championed by LeCun: that theoretical insight, not just scaling, can drive deep learning forward. By showing that self-supervised representations converge to a provably optimal Gaussian structure, this paper bridges mathematical rigor and practical efficiency, reasserting that deep learning’s future may still lie in theory-informed simplicity rather than brute-force scale. Head over to the paper for the full proofs and experiments!
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.08544

points.title: Yonatan and Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 26.11.25
points.preview: Yonatan and Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 26.11.25 Whisper Leak: a side-channel attack on Large Language Models In this paper, two researchers from Microsoft (the Israeli Jonathan Bar Or and the Canadian Geoff McDonald) describe a side-channel attack against Large Language Model (LLM) systems. The motivation is that cloud-based language models communicating with users, even if the communication is en
points.body:
Yonatan and Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 26.11.25
Whisper Leak: a side-channel attack on Large Language Models
In this paper, two researchers from Microsoft (the Israeli Jonathan Bar Or and the Canadian Geoff McDonald) describe a side-channel attack against Large Language Model (LLM) systems.
The motivation is that cloud-based language models communicating with users, even if the communication is encrypted, may expose sensitive information through traffic patterns, specifically packet sizes and inter-packet arrival times. This information allows an attacker to obtain details about the prompt that was sent.
The paper defines and describes an attack called "Whisper Leak." It demonstrates that it is possible to identify "sensitive topics" and "regular queries" from encrypted LLM traffic. Meaning: even without seeing the text of the prompt or the model's response, significant information can be inferred.
In the paper, the researchers describe how LLMs use the TLS protocol for end-to-end encryption. The TLS protocol performs a cryptographic key exchange and then switches to symmetric encryption, where the encryption key and the decryption key are identical. The symmetric encryption in all LLMs tested is a Stream Cipher, where the size of the encrypted message equals the size of the plaintext message. Therefore, a strong correlation is possible between the size of an encrypted token and the size of a visible (plaintext) token. Additionally, LLMs tend to return tokens to the user as quickly as possible, creating a correlation between the time differences of encrypted data packets and the time differences of token generation.
The paper reports on experiments conducted with 28 different LLMs, showing that an attack exploiting the aforementioned patterns exists and succeeds at significant rates. The attack relies on collecting features from the communication, such as packet sizes, duration between sending a prompt and receiving a response, etc. After collecting the data, statistical analysis or machine learning models are used to determine if the prompt belongs to a "sensitive" or "regular" topic. The paper tested three types of models:
BERT Model: Implementing a large language model trained to handle sequential data.
LSTM Model: Implementing a neural network adapted for sequential data.
LightGBM Model: Based on decision trees.
These models were trained on sequential data based on packet sizes and the timing between packets. This data was collected from 10,000 different benign prompts sent to the attacked language model, as well as 1,000 different prompts containing a sensitive topic. In the paper, all three models achieved impressive results of approximately 98% success in classifying a "sensitive prompt" versus a "non-sensitive prompt," given only the sequential data based on packet sizes and inter-packet timing.
The implication of such an attack is relevant to an attacker with the ability to eavesdrop on network traffic (such as governments or a hacker running code on a router), for example, the ability to identify regime opponents, terrorism, and various other activities.
Finally, the paper suggests several ways to mitigate the problem. Microsoft worked with numerous industry leaders in a process lasting nearly a year, during which several companies (OpenAI, Mistral) added an option to their APIs to include obfuscation, random data generated between tokens that prevents the attack.
The paper emphasizes the need to design LLMs and their surrounding systems to successfully minimize data leakage through such side channels.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.03675

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 28.11.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 28.11.25 How to Correctly Report LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluations Using LLMs to evaluate other LLMs has become the default standard for scaling benchmarks. It is cheap, fast, and seemingly effective. It is also, as this paper strictly demonstrates, statistically broken. Most practitioners treat an LLM judge’s output as a proxy for ground truth, perhaps acknowledging some "nois
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 28.11.25
How to Correctly Report LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluations
Using LLMs to evaluate other LLMs has become the default standard for scaling benchmarks. It is cheap, fast, and seemingly effective. It is also, as this paper strictly demonstrates, statistically broken.
Most practitioners treat an LLM judge’s output as a proxy for ground truth, perhaps acknowledging some "noise". They calculate the pass rate (accuracy) on a test set and report it. This paper argues that this naive approach yields not just noisy estimates, but systematically biased ones that do not converge to the true accuracy even with infinite test data.
The authors provide a rigorous statistical framework to correct this. They treat LLM-as-a-Judge not as a black-box oracle, but as a noisy diagnostic test with imperfect specificity and sensitivity. Their contribution is a complete pipeline for unbiased estimation, a novel confidence interval that captures propagated uncertainty, and an adaptive algorithm for allocating human annotation budgets.
The Mathematics of Judgment Bias
The core insight is that an LLM judge acts exactly like a medical screening test. It has two specific failure modes:
False Positives: Judging an incorrect answer as correct (failure of specificity).
False Negatives: Judging a correct answer as incorrect (failure of sensitivity).
If these two error rates were perfectly symmetric, they might cancel out. In reality, they never are. An LLM might be sycophantic (prone to marking answers correct), leading to inflated scores, or overly pedantic, leading to deflated ones.
The authors demonstrate that the "naive accuracy" (what everyone reports on Leaderboards) is a mathematical function of the true accuracy mixed with the judge’s sensitivity and specificity. Crucially, this relationship is linear but skewed. If the judge’s specificity (ability to spot wrong answers) is low, it introduces a positive bias that inflates low-performing models. Conversely, if sensitivity is low, it penalizes high-performing models.
To fix this, the paper adapts the Rogan-Gladen estimator from epidemiology. Instead of taking the LLM’s word for it, we invert the probability matrix. We take the observed naive accuracy and essentially "subtract" the false positives and "add back" the false negatives, scaling the result by the judge’s total discriminatory power (the sum of its sensitivity and specificity minus one). This transformation recovers a statistically consistent estimator of the true accuracy
The Novelty: Dual-Source Uncertainty
Correcting the point estimate is standard epidemiology. The paper’s deeper novelty lies in how it handles confidence intervals (CIs).
In a standard benchmark, we calculate error bars based solely on the size of the test set (using a binomial confidence interval). This assumes the judge is perfect. The authors point out that in the bias-corrected framework, uncertainty comes from two distinct sources:
Test Set Variance: The randomness of the questions asked.
Calibration Set Variance: The uncertainty in our estimation of the judge’s own sensitivity and specificity.
To correct the bias, you need a "calibration set", a small batch of data labeled by humans to measure the LLM judge's performance. Because this human-labeled set is finite, our knowledge of the judge's parameters (q_0 and q_1) is imperfect.
Existing methods often ignore the second source of error. This paper derives a Wald-type confidence interval that explicitly combines the variances from both the test set and the calibration set. They use a "add-two-successes-and-two-failures" adjustment (similar to the Agresti-Coull interval) to ensure the interval remains robust even when calibration sample sizes are small or probabilities are near the boundaries (0 or 1).
This is critical: if you have a massive test set (vanishing test variance) but a tiny calibration set, your confidence interval should remain wide, reflecting your ignorance about the judge's reliability. Standard metrics fail to capture this.
Method: Adaptive Calibration Allocation
The final contribution is an operational one: Adaptive Allocation.
If you have a budget for 500 human annotations to calibrate your LLM judge, how should you spend them? The intuitive answer is 50/50: show humans 250 model-generated answers the LLM thought were "correct" and 250 it thought were "incorrect.
The paper proves this is suboptimal. The variance of the final estimator depends heavily on the specific values of the judge’s sensitivity and specificity, and the current estimated accuracy of the model.
If the LLM is very good at spotting correct answers but struggles with incorrect ones (high sensitivity, low specificity), the variance in the specificity term dominates the total uncertainty.
To minimize the width of the final confidence interval, you should greedily allocate more human labels to the class where the judge is "noisier" or where the impact on the final formula is mathematically weighted higher.
They propose a two-stage algorithm. First, run a tiny "pilot" calibration (e.g., 10 samples) to get a rough estimate of the judge’s error profile. Then, plug these rough estimates into their derived variance formula to calculate the optimal ratio of samples. Finally, spend the remaining budget according to this optimal ratio. This results in significantly tighter confidence intervals for the same cost, effectively squeezing more statistical power out of expensive human annotators.
Validation and Results
Empirical validation using Monte Carlo simulations confirmed the statistical soundness of the proposed framework. When using a judge with asymmetric errors (q_0=0.7, q_1=0.9), the naive estimator consistently failed, overestimating low-performing models and underestimating high-performing ones. In contrast, the bias-adjusted estimator tracked the true accuracy almost perfectly across the entire range. Crucially, the proposed confidence intervals maintained the nominal 95% coverage rate, whereas naive intervals frequently collapsed to near zero coverage, creating a dangerously false sense of certainty. Furthermore, the adaptive allocation strategy successfully reduced the confidence interval width compared to a standard equal split, particularly when the true accuracy deviated from the equilibrium point determined by the judge's error ratio.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.21140

points.title: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 07.12.25
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 07.12.25 Gated Attention for Large Language Models: Non-linearity, Sparsity, and Attention-Sink-Free The standard multihead attention mechanism in the Transformer is, at its core, a "nearly linear sandwich" wrapped in a probability distribution. This sounds a bit unclear, but let's see what is inside: First, we project to V, mix them in a convex manner (with a vector nor
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 07.12.25
Gated Attention for Large Language Models: Non-linearity, Sparsity, and Attention-Sink-Free
The standard multihead attention mechanism in the Transformer is, at its core, a "nearly linear sandwich" wrapped in a probability distribution. This sounds a bit unclear, but let's see what is inside: First, we project to V, mix them in a convex manner (with a vector normalized via Softmax), and project them once more with matrix W_O (after concatenating the outputs of all heads).
The authors of the paper argue that this architecture suffers from a low-rank bottleneck. Since the V projection and the W_O projection are essentially consecutive linear transformations operating on the weighted sum of tokens, the mechanism lacks inherent non-linearity between these stages. This structural limitation restricts the model's expressiveness and forces it to rely heavily on the Softmax bottleneck to filter information.
The innovation here is the introduction of Gated Attention, implemented specifically at the output of the Scaled Dot-Product Attention (or SDPA for short). While gating is common in FFNs and RNNs (such as LSTMs), its application directly onto the attention head output is the main novelty.
As mentioned, the method works by splitting the computation path after the attention operation but before the final aggregation. One path follows the standard Q-K-V generation and Softmax aggregation. The parallel path computes a "gating score." This score is derived via a learned linear projection of Q, which is then passed through a Sigmoid (which ensures it falls between 0 and 1).
The gating score is calculated with the Q representation and does not consider the value of V; it looks at the representation of the current token to decide how much of the retrieved content should actually pass to the residual stream (remember that in Transformers, we compute an addition to the representation from the previous layer, not the representation itself). The operation is element-wise multiplication (or Hadamard product in mathematical parlance) between the standard SDPA output and the gate vector which underwent sigmoid activation.
The authors investigate different variants of this gate computation: head-shared vs. head-specific, and element-wise vs. a single coefficient per attention head. The most effective method identified is Head-Specific Element-wise Gating. This means that every attention head has its own unique gating parameters that modulate each dimension of that head's output vector independently. This resolution allows the model to suppress specific token dimensions within a specific head while allowing others to pass.
Mathematically, this introduces two essential properties into the attention block.
First, it injects non-linearity directly into the (potentially) low-rank mapping between the V and W_O matrices. The composition of the V projection, the attention aggregation, and the O projection is no longer a simple sequence of linear transformations; it is now modulated by a data-dependent non-linear mask. This increases the rank and expressiveness of the transformation without significantly increasing the number of parameters.
Second, and perhaps more importantly, the sigmoid activation induces sparsity. Sigmoid functions naturally push values toward zero or one. This creates a situation where the model can actively zero out the output of an attention head if the retrieved information is deemed irrelevant to the current query context.
This gating mechanism fundamentally changes the dynamics of "Attention Sinks." In standard Transformers, the Softmax function must sum to one. If a token does not find relevant information in the context, it is forced to dump its probability mass somewhere, usually on the first token or a delimiter. This creates massive activation spikes and "sinks" that distort the hidden states.
The Gated Attention mechanism decouples information selection (Softmax) from information intensity (Gating). Even if the Softmax forces a high probability on a sink token due to normalization constraints, the sigmoid can recognize that the retrieval is irrelevant and push the output intensity to zero. This action effectively filters the noise generated by the Softmax normalization constraint before it contaminates the residual stream. The result is cleaner signal propagation, where the model is not forced to process massive and irrelevant activations from sink tokens, thereby stabilizing the internal feature distribution without the need for additional engineering of sink tokens.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2412.15115

points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 13.12.25 ToolOrchestra: Elevating Intelligence via Efficient Model and Tool Orchestration The prevailing "Agentic AI" architecture is economically flawed. It relies on monolithic generalist models to handle every step of a reasoning chain, regardless of complexity. The authors of ToolOrchestra dismantle this inefficient structure, proposing a composite system where a lig
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 13.12.25
ToolOrchestra: Elevating Intelligence via Efficient Model and Tool Orchestration
The prevailing "Agentic AI" architecture is economically flawed. It relies on monolithic generalist models to handle every step of a reasoning chain, regardless of complexity. The authors of ToolOrchestra dismantle this inefficient structure, proposing a composite system where a lightweight 8B-parameter Orchestrator manages a heterogeneous swarm of specialized tools and frontier models. This is not merely advanced prompting; it is a rigorous Reinforcement Learning (RL) framework that reframes model orchestration as a resource-allocation problem, balancing intelligence against cost and latency.
The Agentic Decision Process
The authors formalize the tool-use problem as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) where the cost of intelligence is an intrinsic variable. Unlike standard RLHF, which typically optimizes for a single scalar reward like "helpfulness," this framework operates in a multi-dimensional state-action space.
The Orchestrator's policy does not simply generate text; it selects an action, specifically a tool call, based on the entire interaction history. Crucially, every action carries specific attributes: a monetary cost and an operational latency. The environment's transition function represents the response from these tools (e.g., a database query result or a generated answer from GPT-5), creating a closed feedback loop where the Orchestrator must learn the utility-per-dollar of every available resource.
Multi-Objective Reward Engineering
The most significant technical contribution is the construction of a dynamic, multi-objective reward function. The system does not optimize a static goal; it optimizes adherence to a user's specific preference profile.
For every reasoning trajectory, the system constructs a detailed feature vector that tracks four distinct metrics:
Invocation Counts: How many times each specific tool was called.
Outcome: A binary correctness score verified by a strong judge model (GPT-5.
Cost: The total monetary expense of the tools used.
Latency: The total wall-clock time consumed.
To enable stable learning, these raw metrics are normalized across the generated batch. This step is critical because it reconciles the vast magnitude differences between dollar costs (often fractions of a cent) and token counts or binary scores.
The final reward signal is computed by taking the dot product of this normalized feature vector and a user-defined "preference vector". This effectively makes the reward function modular. By altering the preference vector, the system can be trained to prioritize "lowest latency" for one user and "highest accuracy" for another, without changing the underlying model architecture.
Policy Optimization without a Critic
The training employs Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a policy gradient method that removes the need for a separate value network (or "critic").
Instead of predicting the expected value of a state, the model generates a group of multiple trajectories for the same query. The "advantage" of any single trajectory is calculated by comparing its reward against the average reward of that specific group. If a trajectory performs better than its peers, it is reinforced; if worse, it is discouraged. This reduces memory overhead and stabilizes training by using the group mean as a dynamic baseline.
To further prevent collapse, the authors implement homogeneity filtering during the backward pass. If the rewards within a batch are too similar (low standard deviation), the batch is discarded. This prevents the model from training on data where it has already converged to a single behavior, ensuring gradients are only updated when there is meaningful differentiation to learn from.
ToolScale: Synthetic Environment Generation
Deep reasoning requires verifiable training data, which is scarce for complex tool use. The authors introduce ToolScale, a synthesis pipeline that reverses the standard generation order.
Environment Simulation: Instead of starting with a question, the system first creates the world. An LLM generates a domain-specific database schema, populates it with consistent entries, and defines the API specifications (tools) relevant to that schema.
Intent-to-Task Evolution: A separate model proposes user "intents" that are feasible within that specific environment. These intents are converted into ground-truth trajectories (gold actions).
Adversarial Complexity: To prevent the Orchestrator from learning trivial shortcuts, a third stage "evolves" the tasks, adding constraints and logical twists to force multi-step reasoning.
Quality is rigorously enforced: a generated task is discarded if the gold actions fail to execute cleanly or if the task is solvable without any tool use at all, ensuring the dataset only contains problems that genuinely require orchestration.
Unified Tool Abstraction
Finally, the paper proposes a unified interface where specialized LLMs are wrapped as standard tools. A "Math Model" or a "Coding Model" is presented to the Orchestrator via a JSON schema that is indistinguishable from a web search API.
The description for each model-tool is synthetically generated based on its actual performance on sample tasks. This grounded description allows the Orchestrator to learn a general routing policy: it delegates sub-problems to the cheapest tool that satisfies the difficulty constraint, effectively solving the efficiency-intelligence trade-off through learned delegation rather than brute force.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.21689

points.title: MOETUNER: Orchestrating the Chaos of Expert ParallelismMike's daily paper review: 16.12.25
points.preview: MOETUNER: OPTIMIZED MIXTURE OF EXPERT SERVING WITH BALANCED EXPERT PLACEMENT AND TOKEN ROUTING Today we will review a paper on inference optimization for LLMs in multi-GPU systems. You have surely heard that these models have become so large that they do not fit into the memory of a single GPU, so we start splitting model weight matrices across different GPUs. This is called parallelism, and in th
points.body:
MOETUNER: OPTIMIZED MIXTURE OF EXPERT SERVING WITH BALANCED EXPERT PLACEMENT AND TOKEN ROUTING
Today we will review a paper on inference optimization for LLMs in multi-GPU systems. You have surely heard that these models have become so large that they do not fit into the memory of a single GPU, so we start splitting model weight matrices across different GPUs. This is called parallelism, and in this review, we will discuss how to do this optimally (sort of).
The standard narrative in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) scaling is that we have solved the memory problem but hit a communication wall. We split experts across GPUs (Expert Parallelism) to fit the model parameters into VRAM, yet we pay a heavy tax in the form of "all-to-all" communication latencies as tokens bounce between devices. However, the authors of MOETUNER argue that the current approach to Expert Parallelism is fundamentally flawed because it treats experts as static blocks of memory rather than dynamic units of computation.
Standard frameworks like DeepSpeed or Megatron-LM assign experts to GPUs sequentially (e.g., Experts 0-1 to GPU0, Experts 2-3 to GPU1). This assumes uniform workload distribution. This paper dismantles that assumption by proving that token routing is highly skewed; some experts effectively do all the heavy lifting while others idle. Consequently, the system is only as fast as its slowest (most overloaded) GPU.
The Primer: MoE, Expert Parallelism, and All-to-All
To understand the optimization, one must first grasp the underlying mechanics. In a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model, layers are replaced by a "router" and a set of "experts" (small feed-forward networks). For every input token, the router selects only a few experts (e.g., top-2) to process it, keeping computation costs low despite a massive total parameter count. Expert Parallelism (EP) scales this by distributing these experts across different GPUs. This introduces a necessary evil: if a token on GPU A needs an expert located on GPU B, it must be transferred. This shuffling happens via an "all-to-all" collective communication operation, where every GPU simultaneously sends tokens to and receives tokens from every other GPU. If not managed perfectly, this all-to-all step becomes a bottleneck, forcing GPUs to stall while waiting for incoming data.
The novelty of MOETUNER lies in its rejection of heuristic-based load balancing in favor of a rigorous, two-stage Integer Linear Programming (ILP) formulation. The authors propose a global expert placement framework that optimizes for two competing objectives simultaneously: balancing the compute load on every GPU and minimizing the cross-device traffic that clogs the interconnect.
The Core Intuition: Deterministic Affinity
The methodological backbone of this work is the observation of "Inter-layer Token Routing Dependency". Token paths through an MoE are not random walks. If a token is processed by a specific expert in layer L, there is a high statistical probability it will be routed to a specific subset of experts in layer L+1. The authors exploit this "affinity" to physically place frequently interacting experts on the same device, or at least on devices with high-bandwidth connections.
The method operates in 2 distinct phases: Profiling and Optimization (via ILP).
Phase 1: Profiling via Sampling
Optimization requires data. The authors first conduct a profiling step using a small subset of the dataset. They validate that routing patterns are invariant across batches; the "hot" experts for a specific task remain consistent over time. This allows them to build a "routing history table" that quantifies two things: the compute load (token count) per expert and the transition frequency (token volume) between every pair of experts across adjacent layers.
Phase 2: The ILP Optimization Engine
This is where the paper diverges from locality-aware heuristics. They formulate the placement problem as two sequential Integer Linear Programs.
ILP 1: Load-Balanced Expert Clustering
The first ILP tackles the computational imbalance. The objective function minimizes the absolute deviation between the load of a specific cluster and the average load across the layer. The solver groups experts into clusters, not based on their numerical index, but based on their profiled token demand. If Expert0 is extremely heavy and Expert1 is light, they might be clustered together to offset a medium-load pair. The constraint here is purely computational balance; communication cost is ignored at this stage to ensure no GPU becomes a straggler during the compute phase.
ILP 2: Communication-Aware Cluster Placement
Once experts are clustered to ensure equal compute time, the second ILP assigns these clusters to physical GPUs27. The inputs here are the inter-cluster traffic volumes (derived from the routing history) and the physical bandwidth matrix of the hardware interconnect.
The objective function specifically targets the "tail latency" of the all-to-all operation. It minimizes the maximum communication cost across any GPU pair. The formulation mathematically penalizes the assignment of two highly correlated clusters (where Cluster A feeds many tokens to Cluster B) to GPUs that have a slow connection or reside on different nodes. By dividing the traffic volume by the available bandwidth, the solver finds a topology-aware placement that keeps heavy traffic local or on high-speed NVLink lanes.
In summary, MOETUNER replaces the static, memory-centric view of Expert Parallelism with a dynamic, flow-centric view. By treating expert placement as a resource allocation problem solvable via ILP, it provides a deterministic method to shave down tail latencies that traditionally plague large-scale sparse models.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.06643

points.title: HybridEP: The Great Dilemma—Should We Move Tokens or Move Experts?Mike's Daily Paper Review: 17.12.25
points.preview: HYBRIDEP: SCALING EXPERT PARALLELISM TO CROSS-DATACENTER SCENARIO VIA HYBRID EXPERT/DATA TRANSMISSION We all know the superpower of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. By replacing standard feed-forward layers with specialized "experts" and using a gate to pick just the top-1 or top-2 for every token, we can scale models to trillions of parameters while keeping compute costs sane. But there is
points.body:
HYBRIDEP: SCALING EXPERT PARALLELISM TO CROSS-DATACENTER SCENARIO VIA HYBRID EXPERT/DATA TRANSMISSION
We all know the superpower of Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. By replacing standard feed-forward layers with specialized "experts" and using a gate to pick just the top-1 or top-2 for every token, we can scale models to trillions of parameters while keeping compute costs sane.
But there is a catch. In standard training, experts are sharded across GPUs, and tokens have to travel across the network (via All-to-All) to find their assigned expert. Inside a single, high-speed Datacenter (DC), this is fine. But try to train a model across multiple bandwidth-starved Datacenters? That token traffic becomes a disaster.
Enter HybridEP. The core insight is simple but revolutionary: Why are we only moving tokens? Why don't we make the experts movable too?
The Gate and the Traffic Jam
The MoE Gate is the traffic controller. It takes a token and decides which experts handle it. In standard Expert Parallelism (EP), the experts are stationary. If a token on GPU A needs an expert on GPU B, the token must travel. This creates an All-to-All communication pattern—a massive shuffle of data that chokes cross-DC connections.
HybridEP keeps the gate logic unchanged but fundamentally rethinks the physics of the system. It introduces a flexible strategy to reduce that expensive cross-DC token movement.
The Magic Knob: Parameter p
The heart of the paper is a single tunable parameter, p (ranging from 0 to 1), which controls the mix of movement:
Fraction p of tokens follow the classic path: they travel via All-to-All to the static experts.
Fraction 1-p of tokens stay local. Instead of the tokens moving, the necessary experts are fetched to them via All-Gather.
Why not just always move experts?
It’s a trade-off game. Token traffic per GPU is roughly constant (reshuffling a fixed batch size). However, fetching expert weights grows linearly with the number of GPUs (G). You have to find the sweet spot.
The Math: Two Regimes
The model minimizes latency by comparing the cost of moving data (D) vs. moving expert weights (PE). It identifies two distinct regimes:
The Mixed Regime (2D > G * PE):If tokens are "heavy" relative to the cost of broadcasting experts, the optimal strategy is 0 < p < 1. You migrate some experts to cut down the massive token traffic, but you don't migrate all of them to avoid exploding the All-Gather costs.
The "Mobile Experts" Regime (2D <= G * PE):If experts are "cheap" to move relative to the token volume, the optimal setting is p=0. You fetch all necessary experts, keeping tokens entirely local and eliminating the expensive All-to-All token shuffle.
Standard EP is simply the edge case where p=1.
Under the Hood: Implementation Details
The paper backs this theory up with some clever engineering to make it practical:
1. Expert Domains (Topology Awareness)
p gives us the proportions, but "Expert Domains" decide who talks to whom. The system groups workers (DCs/Nodes) into domains. Within a domain, they share experts via All-Gather. Between domains, they use All-to-All. This creates a communication schedule that perfectly matches the hardware hierarchy.
2. Shrinking the Payload (Shared Expert + Sparse Residual)
To make moving experts cheaper, HybridEP uses a compression trick. Since experts often share structure, the authors compute a "mean" expert (Shared) and subtract it from specific experts to get "residuals."
They only transmit the sparse residuals (top-k values) over the network. The receiver reconstructs the full expert on the fly (Shared + Residual). This drastically cuts the bandwidth required for moving experts, pushing the optimal p lower.
3. Async Overlap
Finally, nothing waits. While the GPU is crunching the pre-expert layers (Attention), the system is already asynchronously All-Gathering the expert residuals for the next step. By the time the compute is ready, the experts are there.
The Takeaway
HybridEP transforms Expert Parallelism from a rigid, "tokens-must-move" protocol into a tunable family of strategies. By using closed-form math to pick the optimal p and employing smart compression, it solves the bandwidth bottleneck of Cross-DC training. For the era of trillion-parameter models, the rule is no longer "always route the data." Sometimes, you have to bring the compute to the data.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.19470

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 19.12.25
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 19.12.25 Let's (not) just put things in Context: Test-Time Training for Long-Context LLMs We have been conditioned to believe that the solution to long-context reasoning is architectural: better rotary embeddings, larger context windows, or linearized attention. We also assume that if a model struggles, we should let it "think" longer by generating Chain-of-Thought token
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 19.12.25
Let's (not) just put things in Context: Test-Time Training for Long-Context LLMs
We have been conditioned to believe that the solution to long-context reasoning is architectural: better rotary embeddings, larger context windows, or linearized attention. We also assume that if a model struggles, we should let it "think" longer by generating Chain-of-Thought tokens. This paper dismantles both assumptions with a precise, theoretical diagnosis of why Transformers fail at retrieval, and proposes a surgical intervention that fundamentally changes how inference compute is allocated.
The authors identify a structural pathology in the Softmax mechanism termed Score Dilution. The proposed cure, Query-only Test-Time Training (qTTT), is not a new architecture but a dynamic inference strategy. It trades the compute usually spent on generating "thinking" tokens for a transient, context-specific gradient update that rewires the model's attention mechanism on the fly.
The Mathematical Diagnosis: Score Dilution
To understand the novelty of qTTT, one must first understand the fatality of static attention. In a standard Transformer, the attention weight for a relevant "needle" token against a backdrop of irrelevant "haystack" tokens (distractors) is determined by the Softmax function.
The paper formalizes a Logarithmic Margin Requirement. As the context length T grows, the number of distractors increases. Even if the distractor logits are slightly lower than the target logit, their sheer quantity inflates the denominator of the Softmax function. To prevent the probability mass of the needle from vanishing (approaching 0), the gap (margin) between the target logit and the distractor logits must grow logarithmically with T.
Static weights, learned during pre-training, cannot guarantee this margin dynamically for every arbitrary context. They produce fixed projection geometries. Consequently, as context expands, the signal drowns in the noise of the denominator. The authors prove that "thinking" tokens cannot recover this signal. Since generated tokens utilize the same frozen, diluted attention mechanism, they are subject to a Needle-Signal Bound: the amount of relevant information a generated token can retrieve is capped by the very attention mass that has already been diluted. You cannot think your way out of a deficit of attention if the mechanism of thought is the one failing to attend.
The Method: Query-Only Test-Time Training (qTTT)
The authors propose shifting the paradigm from generating more tokens to adapting the model. Test-Time Training (TTT) fundamentally reimagines inference not as a static pass, but as a dynamic learning event where the model updates its parameters on the test input itself. By optimizing a self-supervised objective, such as next-token prediction, on the specific prompt at hand, the model adapts to the instance's unique distribution before generating a final response.
The method, qTTT, effectively repurposes the inference-time compute budget usually reserved for generation and invests it in optimization. The procedure is elegant in its constraints. Full test-time training (updating all weights) is infeasible because updating Key (K) and Value (V) projections would invalidate the KV cache, necessitating an expensive re-computation of the entire context for every gradient step.
qTTT circumvents this by strictly freezing the KV cache after a single prefill pass. The "evidence" (Keys and Values) remains static. The method only updates the Query projections (W_Q).
The Process:
Single Prefill: The model processes the long context x_{1:T} once. The resulting Key and Value tensors are cached and locked.
Span Sampling: The model samples a short, contiguous span of tokens from the context (e.g., 128 tokens).
Local Objective: It attempts to predict the next tokens within this span using the standard cross-entropy loss.
Targeted Update: The gradients are backpropagated, but updates are applied exclusively to the Query projection matrices (W_Q).
Iteration: This cycle repeats for a few dozen steps.
Inference: The adapted model, now possessing a modified W_Q tailored specifically to the current context, generates the final answer.
Why It Works: Geometric Steering
The intuition behind updating only the Queries is geometric steering. The paper provides a theoretical proof that the gradient update on the query projection specifically increases the margin between the target and the distractors.
When the model calculates the loss on a text span, the gradient descent step moves the query vector q_i closer to the key vector of the correct token k_{j*} (the needle) and pushes it away from the average attention centroid (the distractors).
While static attention suffers from a fixed geometry that creates "near-ties" among millions of tokens, qTTT dynamically warps the query space. It forces the query projections to become more discriminative for the specific patterns found in the current document. It is not learning new knowledge; it is sharpening the retrieval instrument. By modifying the query to align better with the cached keys, the method artificially manufactures the logarithmic margin required to overcome score dilution.
FLOP Equivalence
The brilliance of the method lies in its resource management. The authors establish an equivalence between the FLOPs required for qTTT and the FLOPs required for generating thinking tokens. They show that a few dozen steps of gradient updates on short spans consume roughly the same computational budget as generating a few thousand intermediate tokens.
However, the utility is vastly different. Thinking tokens add depth to the reasoning trace but rely on the existing, diluted attention. qTTT keeps the sequence length constant but increases the quality of the attention mechanism itself. It is an argument for quality of weights over quantity of tokens.
In summary, qTTT posits that for long-context tasks, the bottleneck is not reasoning capability but retrieval precision. By freezing the evidence (KV cache) and fluidly adapting the inquiry mechanism (Query weights) via gradient descent at test time, the method mathematically guarantees a reversal of the score dilution that plagues static LLMs.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.13898v1

points.title: Omri and Mike's daily paper review: 22.12.25
points.preview: Omri and Mike's daily paper review: 22.12.25 Beyond Final Answers: DeepSeekMath-V2 and the Rise of Self-Verifiable Reasoning In recent years, most work on solving math problems with LLMs has relied on reinforcement learning (RL) methods where the reward is based almost entirely on one thing: whether the final answer is correct or not. This approach has already led to impressive breakthroughs on co
points.body:
Omri and Mike's daily paper review: 22.12.25
Beyond Final Answers: DeepSeekMath-V2 and the Rise of Self-Verifiable Reasoning
In recent years, most work on solving math problems with LLMs has relied on reinforcement learning (RL) methods where the reward is based almost entirely on one thing: whether the final answer is correct or not. This approach has already led to impressive breakthroughs on competitions like AIME, but it leaves an uncomfortable question in the air: do these models actually understand the proofs they write, or do they simply learn to game the scoring system (reward hacking) and sometimes land on the correct answer by chance?
In this DeepSeek paper, the authors argue that we can and should aim much higher: not just a “correct answer,” but self-verifiable reasoning. To do this, they build a fairly complex system: three different models (a proof generator, a proof verifier, and a meta-verifier that checks the verifier), trained through six staged training steps. The end result is not just a model that can “solve” problems, but a system that generates proofs, critiques itself, and attempts to ensure the reasoning actually holds mathematically.
They start from a dataset of about 17.5K proof-required math problems from AoPS. This is the problem pool, and the paper does not specify exactly how many of the 17.5K were manually labeled in practice. For these problems, they generate initial proofs using a strong base model, DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp-Base, and then manually label each proof with a score s_i according to the following rules: 1 for a complete and rigorous proof, 0.5 for a partially correct proof with small gaps, and 0 for a fundamentally incorrect proof.
Using this data, they train the Verifier model with GRPO: it takes a problem and a proof as input, and produces a natural-language critique of the proof, ending with a numeric score. Choosing a language model (rather than a classic discriminative encoder) enables not only producing a score, but also explaining why the proof is good or flawed, which is critical because later they want to measure the quality of the critique itself, not just the final number. To make the score usable as a reward signal, they also enforce a fixed output format (including a final score in a predefined template), so the model not only “understands” but communicates its decision consistently and clearly. From there, they define the scoring-accuracy reward component: they compare the score predicted by the Verifier ′s'_i′​ to the human label s_i, and reward it more as the gap between them shrinks, according to:
Rscore(s′i, si) = 1 − |s′i − s_i|.
Next, they observe that the Verifier only sometimes “hits” the correct score, and along the way it may invent issues that are not actually present in the text. To address this, they train a second model, the Meta-Verifier, which receives the problem, the proof, and the Verifier’s critique, and then rates whether the critique is reasonable and faithful to what is actually written in the proof. This training also uses GRPO, but now the objective is the quality of the critique rather than the quality of the proof. Once they have the Meta-Verifier, they retrain the Verifier with a combined reward:
RV = R_format · R_score · R_meta,
where R_format checks that the output matches the required template (including a final score in a fixed format), R_score rewards score accuracy relative to the human label, and R_meta is the score the Meta-Verifier assigns to the quality of the explanation/critique itself (i.e., whether the issues the Verifier pointed out truly exist and are reasonably stated).
In the next stage, they introduce the proof generator model, the Generator. It is given only the problem and produces a proof. The Verifier reads the proof and returns a score, which serves as the Generator’s reward during training. This is the moment the system transitions, for the first time, into a setup where one model provides reward to another without humans in the loop.
Then comes the critical self-verification stage: the Generator no longer produces only a proof, but also writes a “Self Evaluation”, its own critique of the proof and the score it believes it deserves. The Verifier and Meta-Verifier evaluate both the proof and the self-evaluation. The Generator’s reward combines proof quality with how honest, accurate, and aligned its self-critique is with what an external verifier would say, according to:
R = R_format(Y, Z) · (α · R_Y + β · R_Z), and R_Z = R_score(s′, s) · R_meta(Z).
Here, Y is the proof produced by the Generator and Z is the Self Evaluation it writes; R_format checks that the output matches the required format, R_Y is the score the Verifier assigns to the proof, and α, β are weights balancing proof quality versus self-critique quality. In addition, R_Z is the reward for the self-critique: s′ is the score the Generator assigns to itself, s is the score the Verifier assigns to the proof, R_score measures how close the scores are, and R_meta is the Meta-Verifier’s score for the quality/faithfulness of the explanation in Z (i.e., whether it points to real issues and argues them reasonably).
In the final stage, the system becomes a fully closed loop with no humans: the Generator produces large volumes of new proofs, the Verifier runs multiple parallel critiques on them, and the Meta-Verifier filters which critiques truly reflect issues that exist in the proof versus “invented” claims or unsupported assertions. Then, via voting, they decide a final score for each proof. This automatically labeled data is used to retrain the Verifier again and now fully automatically. The result is a bootstrap process where the generator and verifier improve together, each pushing the other forward, without needing human annotators in the later phase.
In terms of results, DeepSeekMath-V2 is compared directly to today’s strongest models, such as GPT-5-Thinking, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and DeepThink and achieves particularly impressive outcomes: solving most of the IMO 2025 and CMO 2024 problems, and achieving a near-perfect score on Putnam 2024 (118 out of 120). But beyond the numbers, the strongest conceptual claim of the paper is much deeper: the authors argue that LLMs can be trained to develop genuine self-evaluation ability for complex reasoning tasks. Not only to generate solutions, but to meaningfully judge themselves, detect mistakes, and know when a proof is truly valid. That claim goes far beyond “we got a high score” but proposes a new direction in which models do not merely imitate solution patterns, but develop an internal control mechanism over the quality of their own thinking.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.22570=

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 25.12.25 LEARNING DYNAMICS OF LLM FINETUNING
points.preview: This paper departs from the static analysis of loss landscapes and instead formalizes the learning dynamics of LLMs. Rather than asking where the model ends up, the authors ask how a single gradient descent step on a specific training example alters the probability landscape for all other potential responses. By rigorously decomposing the update rule of finetuning algorithms, they uncover a struct
points.body:
This paper departs from the static analysis of loss landscapes and instead formalizes the learning dynamics of LLMs. Rather than asking where the model ends up, the authors ask how a single gradient descent step on a specific training example alters the probability landscape for all other potential responses. By rigorously decomposing the update rule of finetuning algorithms, they uncover a structural pathology in Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) termed the "squeezing effect." This theoretical mechanism explains why off-policy alignment can inadvertently degrade model diversity and confidence, and it motivates a counter-intuitive modification to the supervised finetuning stage.
The Influence Decomposition Framework
The core novelty lies in adapting the theory of Neural Tangent Kernels (NTK) to the auto-regressive nature of LLMs. The authors propose a framework where the change in the log-probability of a specific response sequence is decomposed into the product of three distinct matrices.
First is the Architecture term, which depends solely on the model’s current predictive distribution (specifically, the deviation of probabilities from the identity matrix). This term dictates the stiffness of the probability mass; a highly confident model resists change differently than an uncertain one.
Second is the Similarity term, represented by the empirical Neural Tangent Kernel (eNTK). This is the engine of generalization. It measures the inner product of the gradients for the training example and the observing example. If the gradient vectors of two different prompts or responses align in the high-dimensional parameter space, updating on one essentially "pulls up" the other. This explains the inductive bias of the model: learning a specific response to a prompt simultaneously increases the likelihood of semantically or stylistically similar responses, even if they were never seen.
Third is the Residual term, or the error signal. This vector represents the gap between the current prediction and the target supervision. In Supervised Finetuning (SFT), this vector simply points toward the target token. In preference optimization, however, this term becomes a composite force, pushing toward the chosen response and pulling away from the rejected one.
The Squeezing Effect
The most profound contribution of this dynamics analysis is the identification of the "squeezing effect." This phenomenon emerges when the model is subjected to a negative gradient, specifically, when DPO tries to decrease the probability of a rejected response.
The authors analyze the derivative of the Softmax function under gradient ascent (unlearning). They mathematically demonstrate that when a negative gradient is applied to a token that is already in a low-probability "valley" of the distribution, the probability mass is not redistributed evenly among the remaining options. Instead, the mechanics of the Softmax gradient dictate that the displaced mass is disproportionately squeezed into the single token with the highest existing confidence (the argmax).
In off-policy DPO, the rejected response often comes from a distribution the model has already moved away from, or is simply distinct from the model's current preferences. Consequently, the model is punished for a response it already considers unlikely. This triggers the squeezing effect: the probability of the rejected token drops further, and the probability of the current top-choice token skyrockets.
This creates a "rich-get-richer" dynamic where the model becomes pathologically peaky around its argmax predictions. The authors argue this is the dynamical root of the "repeater" phenomenon and the degradation of generation quality often observed in long DPO runs. The negative pressure doesn't teach the model nuance; it simply forces it to retreat into its most confident, often repetitive, patterns.
Mitigation via SFT Extension
The analytical framework implies a direct, if paradoxical, solution. The squeezing effect is contingent on the rejected response being in a probability valley. To mitigate it, one must alter the initial conditions of the preference tuning phase.
The authors propose augmenting the Supervised Finetuning (SFT) stage to include not just the chosen responses, but also the rejected responses. By training on the rejected responses using a standard maximum likelihood objective during SFT, the model "pulls up" these sequences out of the valley and into a region of higher probability.
When DPO begins, the negative gradient is then applied to a target that has significant probability mass (a "peak" rather than a "valley"). Under these conditions, the mathematical derivation shows that the probability redistribution is far more balanced. The negative signal provides a constructive contrast rather than triggering the degenerative squeezing into the argmax. This method effectively primes the geometry of the probability distribution to receive the alignment signal without structural collapse.
 https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.10490

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 28.12.25
points.preview: Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond) The trajectory of modern AI research has largely been defined by a pursuit of convergence. We train models to converge on the "correct" answer, the "optimal" policy, or the "ground truth" distribution. But what happens when the task demands divergence? The paper Artificial Hivemind tackles the uncomfortable reality tha
points.body:
Artificial Hivemind: The Open-Ended Homogeneity of Language Models (and Beyond)
The trajectory of modern AI research has largely been defined by a pursuit of convergence. We train models to converge on the "correct" answer, the "optimal" policy, or the "ground truth" distribution. But what happens when the task demands divergence? The paper Artificial Hivemind tackles the uncomfortable reality that our pursuit of alignment and correctness may be engineering a collapse in creative variance. This is not merely a study of repetitive text; it is a methodological blueprint for quantifying the homogenization of machine thought in high-dimensional embedding spaces.
This research distinguishes itself through a rigorous, mathematically grounded framework for evaluating open-endedness. It moves beyond the anecdotal observation that "LLMs sound the same" and constructs a robust statistical apparatus to measure exactly how much they sound the same, even across different architectures and providers. The novelty here lies not just in the diagnosis but in the instrumentation built to detect it.
The INFINITY-CHAT Protocol and Taxonomy Construction
The authors reject standard synthetic benchmarks in favor of an empirically derived dataset, INFINITY-CHAT. The methodology for its creation is a study in filtration and semantic rigorousness. Starting with the WildChat corpus, they apply a multi-stage sieve to isolate true open-endedness. This is not simple keyword matching. They deploy a classifier (specifically GPT-4o) to evaluate queries against three distinct topological criteria: meaningful information seeking, the absence of greeting-like phatic expressions, and, crucially, the potential for "pluralistic" responses.
This last criterion is the mathematical heart of the dataset. A query is only included if it maps to a non-deterministic output space, a question where the "correct" answer is a distribution, not a point. To structure this unstructured space, they employ a semi-automatic taxonomy generation. They bootstrap a hierarchy from a seed set of 100 queries, iteratively refining categories like "Counterfactual Reasoning" or "Abstract Ideation" before scaling the classification across the full 26,000-item corpus. This ensures the dataset covers the full manifold of user intent rather than overfitting to narrow creative writing prompts.
Geometric Quantification of Homogeneity
The paper’s primary methodological contribution is its translation of "creativity" into vector geometry. To measure the "Hivemind" effect, the authors utilize high-dimensiona embeddings. They treat every model response as a vector in an embedding space.
For Intra-Model Repetition, they do not rely on n-gram overlap or surface-level lexical statistics, which are brittle and miss semantic paraphrasing. Instead, they compute the pairwise cosine similarity between embeddings of 50 independent generations for the same prompt. By analyzing the density of these clusters, they quantify how tightly a model’s "imagination" is bounded. If the hypersphere of generated responses has a negligible radius, the model has suffered mode collapse.
For Inter-Model Homogeneity, the methodology expands to compare the centroids and distributions of response vectors across different models. They introduce a "Unique Source Model" metric. For a given query, they pool responses from all models and identify the N nearest neighbors in vector space. If diversity existed, the nearest neighbors to a specific response would be distributed across various source models. If the neighbors are dominated by the same model or a tight cluster of aligned models, it indicates a cross-model convergence—a homogenization of the alignment prior itself.
To visualize this high-dimensional collapse, they employ Principal Component Analysis (PCA). By projecting the 1536-dimensional embedding vectors onto a 2D plane defined by the two axes of maximum variance, they demonstrate how responses from nominally different models (e.g., Llama vs. Claude vs. GPT) collapse into identical clusters. This geometric approach provides a formula-free yet mathematically rigorous proof that distinct models are converging on the same local minima of expression.
Calibrating for Pluralism: Entropy and Tukey’s Fences
Perhaps the most sophisticated aspect of their methodology is how they evaluate the evaluation of these models. Recognizing that a single scalar "reward" is insufficient for open-ended tasks, they collected dense human annotations, 25 separate human ratings per response.
This density allows them to treat human preference not as a ground truth label but as a probability distribution. They calculate the Shannon entropy of these 25 ratings to quantify "disagreement." High entropy implies that the quality of the response is subjective and idiosyncratic; low entropy implies consensus.
To test if Reward Models and LLM-as-a-Judge systems are calibrated to this variance, they employ robust statistical filtering techniques. They isolate subsets of responses that are "statistically indistinguishable" in quality. They achieve this using Tukey’s Fences, a method for outlier detection based on the interquartile range (IQR). By filtering out responses that lie outside the standard statistical dispersion, they create a "hard" evaluation set where one response is not obviously better than another.
They then compute correlation coefficients (Spearman and Pearson) between the model’s predicted scores and the human average. The methodology here is designed to stress-test the models: can a Reward Model distinguish between two high-quality but distinct poems? Or does it fail when the signal-to-noise ratio decreases? By focusing on these high-entropy and low-variance subsets, the authors expose the inability of current reward architectures to model pluralism. They prove that our current alignment algorithms are optimized for the mean of the human preference distribution, aggressively pruning the tails where creativity actually lives.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.22954

points.title: Eden and Mike’s Daily Paper, 28.12.25
points.preview: Eden and Mike’s Daily Paper, 28.12.25 SEAL: Self-Adapting Language Models Introduction LLMs are trained on massive amounts of data and, as a result, exhibit excellent capabilities across a wide range of tasks and domains. However, despite this, these models show poor performance on tasks that require new information. Consequently, the authors of the paper pose the following question: Is there a wa
points.body:
Eden and Mike’s Daily Paper, 28.12.25
SEAL: Self-Adapting Language Models
Introduction
LLMs are trained on massive amounts of data and, as a result, exhibit excellent capabilities across a wide range of tasks and domains. However, despite this, these models show poor performance on tasks that require new information. Consequently, the authors of the paper pose the following question:
Is there a way for the models themselves to generate the training data they need for a task and decide how to train/teach themselves using that information? The researchers investigate the answer to this question in this paper. They argue that just as humans studying for an exam know how to organize information in the way most convenient for them to learn (e.g., in a diagram, table, etc.), language models should be given the same capability. The researchers frame this ability as a Reinforcement Learning (RL) problem, where an RL algorithm learns—given new information—the best way to teach the language model. In this context, "learning" refers to updating the model's weights. They call this method SEAL.
The Method
The method operates given a task context and a method used for the evaluation of the task solution. An example of this is the ARC benchmark, where the context consists of the few-shot examples provided for each question.
At the heart of the method lies the concept of Self-edits (SE). This term describes the action the model can perform: editing the context itself to improve its own solution to the task. For example, given a context of a paragraph about which questions are asked, the model can edit the paragraph into a different format (based on a specific instruction from the researcher) and use this form (SE) if it leads to improved performance.
The researchers also experimented with creating SEs in other ways, such as rewriting the paragraph or writing questions and answers based on the paragraph.
Technical Implementation:
Parallel Processing: The authors perform several actions simultaneously, creating numerous SEs for a given context.
LoRA: they use LoRA (a fast and memory-efficient fine-tuning algorithm) to perform a single training step (including both SFT and RL) for each SE separately. This creates a large number of different versions of the same model (each corresponding to one training step for its specific SE).
