Evaluation: Each version undergoes an evaluation phase and receives a score based on its defined task.
The Reward System:
The training reward was defined as binary:
1: If using the SE led to a model update (via Fine-Tuning) that improved results (based on the evaluation performed).
0: If the SE did not lead to improvement.
This reward is used by the RL algorithm to learn the optimal way to write an SE so that it serves the model best during the Fine-Tuning (FT) stage.
RL Algorithm:
The authors chose the ReST (Reinforcement Learning from Self-Play) method as their RL algorithm. In this approach, the model generates several examples (candidates) in its current version and then uses only the examples that yielded a positive reward for the learning process.
Self-Improving Capabilities
The researchers demonstrate how the method can also be used when the context consists of several solved examples for the task (i.e., few-shot). They use the ARC benchmark as an example, where the model must demonstrate reasoning and generalization to answer various tasks using a small set of solved examples.
In this environment, the SEs the model could create included:
Tool Use: Applying tools to augment the context examples to create additional examples (e.g., inversion, grouping, etc.).
Training Parameters: Optimizing parameters for its own training, such as the number of epochs, learning rate, and more.
This creates a scenario where the model can learn how to optimize its own training process for a task to improve its performance. In other words, we obtain a type of self-improving model.
Results
To test their method on ARC, the researchers used the Llama-3.2-1B model. They successfully showed that even though the model encounters a task (ARC) it has never seen before, it learns and improves using the proposed method. The paper claims that no ARC data was used for the initial training.
They compared their method against the following approaches:
In-context learning (ICL)
SEAL without RL (i.e., including only the SFT stage)
The results achieved using SEAL were superior to the other methods.
For the first example involving paragraphs, they performed a comparison using the SQuAD dataset. Here, they used Qwen-2.5-7B models and tested their method against others, including one that uses GPT-4o (referred to in the text as 4.1) as a "teacher model" to create the SE (without using RL). In some cases, SEAL yielded better results, while in others, the teacher model approach performed better.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.10943

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 02.01.26
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 02.01.26 Agent-R1: Training Powerful LLM Agents with End-to-End Reinforcement Learning Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models has traditionally been treated as a static optimization problem. In standard RLHF, the model generates a complete response, receives a reward, and updates its policy. This assumes a deterministic transition: the next state is simply
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 02.01.26
Agent-R1: Training Powerful LLM Agents with End-to-End Reinforcement Learning
Reinforcement Learning (RL) for Large Language Models has traditionally been treated as a static optimization problem. In standard RLHF, the model generates a complete response, receives a reward, and updates its policy. This assumes a deterministic transition: the next state is simply the current text plus the new token. However, this collapses when we move to Agents. Agents do not just generate text; they interact with stochastic environments, use tools, and process external feedback.
Agent-R1 proposes a fundamental restructuring of the RL framework to accommodate this shift. The authors argue that training agents requires a mathematically distinct formulation of the Markov Decision Process (MDP) compared to static LLMs.
The Agent-MDP Formulation
The paper's primary conceptual novelty lies in redefining the tuple (S, A, P, R) for agentic workflows.
1. The Comprehensive State Space S:
In static LLMs, the state is merely the sequence of tokens generated so far. In Agent-R1, the state S_t is expanded to include the entire history of interaction turns. A "turn" is no longer just text; it is a composite object T containing the agent's reasoning, the specific tool call, and the raw environmental feedback. This distinction is critical because the state now encapsulates external world states, not just internal linguistic context.
2. The Hybrid State Transition P:
This is the most mathematically significant deviation from standard LLM training. The authors split the state transition probability P into two distinct modes:
Generative Transition (P_G): Deterministic. When the agent is reasoning or formatting an answer, s_{t+1} is just s_t appended with action a_t (the token).
Environmental Transition (P_E): Stochastic. When the agent emits a "tool call" sequence, the next state depends on the environment's response (e.g., a search engine result or API return).Standard RL pipelines assume P_G everywhere. Agent-R1 explicitly models P_E, acknowledging that the agent's action (calling a tool) leads to a non-deterministic next state determined by the ToolEnv.
3. Dense Reward Structure (R):
Instead of a sparse reward at the end of a trajectory (terminal state), Agent-R1 introduces Process Rewards (r_p). These are intermediate signals triggered by specific atomic actions, such as successfully executing a valid tool call. The total reward function becomes a summation of the final outcome reward (r_f) and these intermediate process rewards, allowing the model to learn correct tool usage syntax and logic even if the final answer is incorrect.
The Agent-R1 Training Framework
To operationalize this MDP, the authors introduce a modular framework split into Tool and ToolEnv.
Tool: This is the atomic executor. It performs the action (e.g., Python execution) and returns raw factual output. It is stateless and purely functional.
ToolEnv: This is the RL environment wrapper. It interprets the raw output from the Tool, manages the state transition logic, and calculates the reward. It converts the "fact" of the tool output into a "state" for the agent.
Optimization: Masking and Alignment
The implementation of the learning phase addresses the "credit assignment" problem in multi-turn trajectories. A raw trajectory contains mixed tokens: user instructions, agent reasoning, tool calls, tool outputs (environment), and final answers.
Standard policy gradient methods (like PPO and GRPO) would naively try to optimize the probability of all tokens in the sequence. Agent-R1 implements a strict Action Masking mechanism. The loss function is applied only to tokens generated by the agent (thoughts and tool calls). The tokens representing environmental feedback (the tool outputs) are masked out. You cannot update the policy to "generate" the environment's response better; you can only update the policy to react to it.
Crucially, the paper introduces Advantage Alignment. When calculating Advantages (using Generalized Advantage Estimation), the system must align the reward signal (which comes after the environment acts) with the agent's specific action (the tool call) that triggered it. The framework calculates advantages based on the full trajectory (including environment rewards) but filters the backpropagation using the Action Mask. This ensures that the credit for a positive environmental outcome is assigned strictly to the decision to call the tool, not the tool's output itself.
This end-to-end approach moves beyond "prompt engineering" or static RAG by treating the tool-use loop as a differentiable, optimizable decision process where the agent learns to navigate stochastic transitions to maximize long-term rewards.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.14460

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 04.01.26
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 04.01.26 RECURSIVE LANGUAGE MODELS I don't say this often, but I hope I've understood this paper correctly. If not, correct me.... I'll start with a slightly controversial claim (or perhaps not). The main bottleneck of the Transformer architecture is arguably not its quadratic complexity, but its implicit reliance on a monolithic context window. Even with architectural a
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 04.01.26
RECURSIVE LANGUAGE MODELS
I don't say this often, but I hope I've understood this paper correctly. If not, correct me....
I'll start with a slightly controversial claim (or perhaps not). The main bottleneck of the Transformer architecture is arguably not its quadratic complexity, but its implicit reliance on a monolithic context window. Even with architectural approximations like linear attention or Ring Attention, the model is forced to hold the entire state in activation memory. Moreover, empirical evidence points to a phenomenon of "context rot," where reasoning capabilities degrade non-linearly as the prompt length increases, regardless of the theoretical window size.
In Recursive Language Models (RLMs), Zhang et al. propose a radical shift in how we conceptualize the input prompt. Instead of treating the prompt as a sequence of tokens to be fed directly into the neural network's embedding layer, RLMs treat the prompt as external data—specifically, as a string variable residing in an external computational environment. This is a move from in-context learning to out-of-core inference.
The methodology effectively decouples the "problem size" (prompt length) from the "working memory" (context window). The authors implement this by embedding the LLM within a Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) environment, typically Python. When a user submits a query Q and a massive context P, the RLM does not ingest P. Instead, it initializes a session where P is loaded into memory as a variable. The LLM is then presented with a system prompt that describes the environment, the length of P, and the available tools.
The primary novelty lies in the set of operations available to the model. The LLM acts as a controller, interacting with the prompt P strictly through code execution. It can inspect P by printing slices (e.g., print(P[:1000])), but more importantly, it can programmatically decompose P using standard string manipulation or regular expressions.
This decomposition enables the second, and most critical, component of the methodology: recursive self-invocation. The REPL environment provides a specific function, denoted as llm_query, which allows the model to call itself (or a smaller, faster sub-model) on a specific subset of data.
This structure transforms the inference process from a single forward pass over a long sequence into a recursive tree of function calls. The root model might analyze the metadata of P and decide to split the text by chapter or semantic delimiters. It then writes a loop in Python to iterate through these chunks, passing each one to a sub-instance of the LLM via llm_query along with a specific sub-task (e.g., "Summarize the key entities in this chunk").
The results of these recursive calls are returned to the root model's environment as variables. The root model can then aggregate these intermediate outputs—effectively performing a "reduce" operation after the "map" phase—to synthesize a final answer. This allows the model to perform complex reasoning over contexts that are orders of magnitude larger than its training window, limited only by the memory available to the Python runtime and the recursion depth allowed by the system.
Methodologically, this mimics the structure of classic divide-and-conquer algorithms. By offloading the "storage" of the context to the RAM of the interpreter and using the LLM only for "processing" manageable chunks, the approach enforces a hard limit on the noise introduced by irrelevant tokens. The model never sees the haystack; it only sees the needles it explicitly mined via code.
Furthermore, the authors introduce a mechanism for state persistence across the recursion. Because the interaction happens within a REPL, the model can store findings in lists, dictionaries, or custom data structures. This explicit state management contrasts sharply with the implicit state management of attention heads, offering a form of interpretable, symbolic working memory. The trajectory of reasoning becomes a trace of code executions, making the logic transparent and debuggable.
The approach also handles output scaling. Standard models are limited by max output tokens. Because the RLM constructs its answer within the REPL environment, it can build outputs that far exceed the single-pass generation limit by stitching together strings stored in variables over multiple turns, finally releasing the result only when the symbolic construction is complete.
In summary, the RLM framework redefines the LLM as a kernel for a recursive operating system. It substitutes the probabilistic retrieval of attention mechanisms with the deterministic, verifiable retrieval of code execution, allowing inference-time compute to scale dynamically with the complexity of the task rather than the length of the input.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.24601

points.title: Netanel & Mike’s Daily Paper - 05.01.26 Paraformer-v2: An improved non-autoregressive transformer for noise-robust speech recognition
points.preview: In recent years, most leading work in Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has relied on autoregressive architectures (such as Conformer). In these models, the system generates its output token by token, each token depending on everything generated before it. This approach has already delivered impressive results and completely dominated Leaderboard tables - but it leaves an uncomfortable question h
points.body:
In recent years, most leading work in Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) has relied on autoregressive architectures (such as Conformer). In these models, the system generates its output token by token, each token depending on everything generated before it. This approach has already delivered impressive results and completely dominated Leaderboard tables - but it leaves an uncomfortable question hanging in the air: do we really have to wait for the model to recognize the first syllable before it can understand the tenth? Or is this simply a limitation of an architecture we’ve grown used to, one that prevents us from fully leveraging the GPU’s parallel computation power?
In the work from Alibaba’s Speech Lab, the authors claim we can and should aim much higher: not just “high accuracy,” but fully parallel (that is, non-autoregressive) decoding, without sacrificing the performance of today’s computationally intensive autoregressive models. To achieve that, the authors build a fairly elaborate system: the Paraformer architecture, which breaks the classical structure into four different modules (encoder, predictor, sampler, and decoder), all working together to crack the biggest challenge of parallel speech recognition: how do you know how many words to generate, and how they relate to each other, without relying on chronological order?
They start from the strongest foundation available today: the encoder. Here they don’t reinvent the wheel - they use a Conformer, a network capable of extracting rich audio representations. But unlike standard models that just throw these representations straight into the decoder, Paraformer recognizes a critical missing piece in the middle: the predictor.
In version one (v1), they introduced the CIF mechanism (Continuous Integrate-and-Fire). The model assigns a numeric value (weight) to every tiny slice of audio. These weights are determined by a dedicated network trained to distinguish syllables from silence - for instance, a clear syllable fragment might get a weight of 0.8, while silence might get something negligible like 0.01. The model sweeps through the audio and sums these weights (0.01 + 0.8 + …), under the idea that the final total should equal the number of tokens in the utterance (for example, a sentence with 5 words should sum to 5.0). This allows the model to learn how many words to generate without knowing the length in advance. Once the cumulative sum crosses a threshold (meaning enough information has accumulated), the model recognizes that a pronunciation unit has completed and “fires out” a textual token.
This worked beautifully for Mandarin - but when moving to languages like English or to noisy environments, they found CIF tended to “drift” and produce tokens simply due to background noise. To fix this, Paraformer-v2 makes a dramatic move and replaces the heart of the system: instead of CIF, they switch to CTC as the predictor. The model performs a fast prediction pass, identifies “strong” tokens, and compresses the sequence in a way that filters out silence and noise. The decoder now receives a far more refined and accurate input.
Then comes the critical stage of “understanding without order.” First, the predictor makes its guesses based on the graphical shape of the sound (the spectrogram) at each tiny time slice, almost “blind” to broader linguistic context. It runs once and outputs all tokens at once (say, 100 tokens) as an initial guess. The decoder receives all 100 simultaneously and performs one massive computation to refine them all together. But how does the decoder build a meaningful sentence in a single computation if it doesn’t have previous-word context? In a regular Transformer, “apple” is predicted because the model first generated “the boy ate.” Here, to compensate, they introduce the Sampler with the GLM technique (Glancing Language Model). This trick happens only during training: the model “peeks” at the correct answer and plants some of the real words inside the decoder’s input. The decoder must then fill in the missing words using the partially revealed truth plus the audio. This forces it to learn deep semantic dependencies - it learns that “ate” and “apple” belong together, even without generating them sequentially.
In terms of results, Paraformer-v2 is compared directly with today’s strongest models and delivers numbers that genuinely shift the landscape: on LibriSpeech and AISHELL, it matches the accuracy of state-of-the-art autoregressive Conformer models - but does so at 20× the speed. Moreover, switching to CTC in v2 boosted noise robustness from 54.5% to 77.7%, making it viable for real-world environments. But beyond the numbers, the paper’s most powerful conceptual claim runs much deeper: the authors demonstrate that the deep connection between “strong language understanding” and “sequential generation” can be broken. Neural networks are capable of “grasping” entire sentences in one shot - just like humans read a sign - not only word-by-word like a typewriter.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2409.17746
Are Small Language Models the Future of Agentic AI?

points.title: Michael & Mike’s Daily Paper, 07.01.26
points.preview: Michael & Mike’s Daily Paper, 07.01.26 Small Language Models are the Future of Agentic AI The past year has undoubtedly been the year of Agentic AI. It is therefore natural that the question resurfaces: should agentic applications increasingly rely on Small Language Models (SLMs), models with on the order of ~10B parameters that can be executed on edge devices? The paper surveys the relevant desig
points.body:
Michael & Mike’s Daily Paper, 07.01.26
Small Language Models are the Future of Agentic AI
The past year has undoubtedly been the year of Agentic AI. It is therefore natural that the question resurfaces: should agentic applications increasingly rely on Small Language Models (SLMs), models with on the order of ~10B parameters that can be executed on edge devices? The paper surveys the relevant design considerations for this direction and frames it as a trend likely to shape the next steps in the field.
Key Claims
SLMs are sufficiently capable for the repetitive, well-defined tasks that characterize many agent-based applications.
They represent a more suitable operational solution due to their predictability and efficiency. For cases that require complex dialogue or advanced reasoning, an LLM can still be invoked selectively, on demand.
Cost and energy efficiency: SLMs are significantly more economical than large, general-purpose models, both in monetary cost and energy consumption (with an explicit ethical consideration raised around sustainability).
Performance Considerations
The paper provides examples of modern small models that demonstrate performance on par with much larger models across various applications. Examples include Microsoft Phi, NVIDIA Nemotron-H, and Hugging Face’s SmolLM2, among others. These models are built on classical Transformer architectures, hybrid Transformer-MAMBAdesigns, and RETRO-style architectures (Transformers augmented with retrieval from large external databases).
Efficiency and Modularity
Beyond the obvious gains in inference latency, memory footprint, and hardware requirements, fine-tuning SLMs is substantially faster, often requiring only a few hours. This enables high flexibility and supports modular system design, where independent components can be optimized in isolation, aligning well with modern agentic architectures. An additional source of efficiency is the use of a single, fixed output format, in contrast to LLMs that are required to generate across a wide variety of output schemas.
Counterarguments and Limitations
The paper also presents alternative viewpoints emphasizing the inherent advantages of LLMs in language understanding, semantic richness, and their ability to generalize across domains and modalities. These arguments are largely countered by pointing to the relative simplicity and task-specific nature of agentic systems, where such generality is often unnecessary. Another counterpoint is that LLM costs are expected to decline with scale, potentially narrowing the gap.
Identified barriers to broader SLM adoption include:
Existing organizational and infrastructural investment in LLM-based systems.
A focus on broad, generalized benchmarks, rather than application-specific metrics more relevant to agentic use cases.
Lack of awareness and marketing around small models, resulting in limited mindshare.
Migration Blueprint
Finally, the paper proposes a blueprint for migrating an agent from an LLM-based design to an SLM-based one. This includes systematic logging and data collection for fine-tuning, along with removal and anonymization of personal information for safety. A particularly compelling idea is the use of unsupervised clustering of prompts to better understand which types of SLMs are needed for different task categories. From there, standard fine-tuning or distillation pipelines are applied to mimic the behavior of the original LLM.
Our Perspective
We tend to agree with most, if not all, of the paper’s arguments. The strongest insight is the recognition that agentic tasks are typically simple and low-context, making small models especially well-suited due to their repeatability and predictability. Equally important, from practical experience, debugging AI workflows composed of multiple small model calls is often easier, a clear application of the classic divide-and-conquer principle. It would be particularly valuable to see future work in this direction supported by large-scale empirical evaluations, systematically quantifying the performance, cost, and reliability trade-offs between SLM- and LLM-based agentic systems.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.02153

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 09.01.26
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 09.01.26 From Entropy to Epiplexity: Rethinking Information for Computationally Bounded Intelligence This is the most complex paper I have reviewed for some time (and this “some” is quite long). Let’s start with a loud claim: we have a crack in the foundations of learning theory. Standard information theory, built on Shannon entropy and Kolmogorov complexity, assumes obs
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 09.01.26
From Entropy to Epiplexity: Rethinking Information for Computationally Bounded Intelligence
This is the most complex paper I have reviewed for some time (and this “some” is quite long). Let’s start with a loud claim: we have a crack in the foundations of learning theory.
Standard information theory, built on Shannon entropy and Kolmogorov complexity, assumes observers with unlimited computational power. To Shannon, a pseudorandom number generator is merely a deterministic function with zero entropy; to Kolmogorov, a fractally complex image generated by a short script is simple. Yet, for a neural network with a finite FLOPs budget, these distinctions are meaningless. A cryptographically secure sequence appears maximally random, and a complex game engine like Chess requires massive informational capacity to model.
This paper proposes a paradigm shift: measuring information not by what exists in the abstract limit, but by what a computationally bounded observer can actually learn. The authors introduce Epiplexity (epistemic complexity), a metric that decouples "randomness" (unpredictable noise) from "structure" (learnable patterns) based strictly on the observer’s processing constraints.
The Core Formalism: Time-Bounded MDL
The authors ground their framework in a resource-constrained version of Minimum Description Length (MDL). In classical MDL, we seek the shortest program that outputs the data. Here, the authors restrict the search space to Time-Bounded Probabilistic Models: programs that can evaluate probabilities and sample data within a fixed time budget T.
They decompose the total description length of a dataset into two distinct components:
Epiplexity (S_T): The length of the shortest program that minimizes the total code length within time T. This represents the structural info, the rules, patterns, and logic the model has successfully encoded into its weights.
Time-Bounded Entropy (H_T): The expected code length of the data given that optimal program. This represents the residual unpredictability or "apparent randomness" relative to the observer's constraints.
The novelty here is the shift from objective complexity to subjective complexity. A ciphertext has low Kolmogorov complexity (it's just a key plus a message) but maximal Time-Bounded Entropy because no efficient program can decrypt it. Conversely, "structural" data is defined as data where investing more compute allows for a shorter description length.
Measuring the Unmeasurable
Since we cannot search the space of all Turing machines, the authors propose two distinct, highly technical methods to estimate epiplexity using neural networks as the function class.
1. Prequential Coding (The Heuristic Approach)
This method leverages the concept of "predictive coding" to estimate the information stored in model weights. Rather than encoding the weights explicitly (which is inefficient for large nets), the authors utilize a "prequential" transmission protocol: the sender and receiver train a model in sync on the data stream..
The insight is mathematical: the description length of the model is approximated by the accumulated generalization gain during training. Specifically, it is the integral of the difference between the "online" loss (loss at step t before training on step t) and the "offline" loss (loss of the final converged model). Visually, this is the area under the loss curve above the convergence baseline. If a model rapidly drops its loss, it has "absorbed" a massive amount of structural information (high epiplexity). If the loss curve is flat (like on random noise), no structure is learned, and epiplexity is zero.
2. Requential Coding (The Rigorous Approach)
To address theoretical looseness in the prequential approach, the authors introduce Requential Coding. This is a novel encoding scheme where the "student" model is explicitly coded by transmitting its training trajectory relative to a "teacher" model.
Using Relative Entropy Coding (REC), the authors treat the weights not as static floats, but as the result of an optimization process on synthetic data generated by a teacher. The cost to transmit the student model becomes the cumulative Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between the teacher and the student at every step of training. This effectively measures the "informational distance" the student travels to learn the structure. This method provides a constructive, computable upper bound on the complexity of the learned program without relying on asymptotic assumptions.
The Compute-Optimal Frontier
A critical methodological contribution is how they define the "optimal" observer. They don't just train one model. Instead, they sweep across hyperparameters (model size N and dataset size D) to find the Pareto frontier of compression vs. compute.
They explicitly couple the computational cost of finding the model (training FLOPs) with the cost of using the model (inference FLOPs). The "Time-Bounded" constraint T is defined as a linear combination of training compute and inference compute on the test set. The epiplexity is not just the size of any model; it is the size of the specific model that minimizes the total description length for that specific budget T.
Why This Matters
This framework resolves deep paradoxes in modern AI. It mathematically explains how deterministic transformations (like running a game engine or a cellular automaton) can create new information: they convert computational work into structural epiplexity that was implicit (but inaccessible) in the initial state. It also provides a formal basis for why data order matters: factorizing a joint distribution P(X,Y) one way might be computationally easy, while the reverse requires solving a cryptographic-hard inverse problem, necessitating a model with vastly higher epiplexity.
By formalizing the "value" of data as the computational work required to compress it, Finzi et al. provide a rigorous language for what scaling laws are actually measuring: the conversion of FLOPs into structural information.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.03220v1

points.title: Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26
points.preview: Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26 WAIT, WAIT, WAIT... WHY DO REASONING MODELS LOOP? Reasoning models like DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen have opened a new scaling axis: inference-time compute, by creating long Chain of Thought (CoT) sequences. However, this capability comes with a frustrating pathology: thought (or reasoning) loops. At low temperatures or under greedy decoding (sampling the token with the highest
points.body:
Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26
WAIT, WAIT, WAIT... WHY DO REASONING MODELS LOOP?
Reasoning models like DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen have opened a new scaling axis: inference-time compute, by creating long Chain of Thought (CoT) sequences. However, this capability comes with a frustrating pathology: thought (or reasoning) loops. At low temperatures or under greedy decoding (sampling the token with the highest "probability"), these models often get stuck in thought cycles, endlessly repeating facts or retracing incorrect logical steps. While raising the temperature is the standard "fix," this paper argues that it is merely a stopgap that masks the underlying problem without solving it.
The authors propose a novel theoretical approach to explain the phenomenon, identifying imperfect learning as the root cause. They argue that reasoning loops can stem from a misalignment between the training distribution (the teacher's reasoning) and what the student-model actually learns in a reasoning model scenario. To analyze this, they introduce a synthetic "graph reasoning" task that isolates different mechanisms driving these loops: Risk Aversion due to Hardness of Learning and Inductive Bias for Temporally Correlated Errors.
Unexpectedly, the authors train Transformers on a (synthetic) navigation task over star graphs to investigate reasoning loops in a controlled environment (drawing inspiration from a 2024 paper that failed to train a Transformer to perform this task). The graph consists of a central node (also called the root) connected to n disjoint "spoke" paths, each ending in a leaf. A "start" node connects only to the root. The goal is to generate a random walk path from the start node to a randomly selected target leaf.
The training data does not consist only of the shortest path. It also mimics (sub-optimal) reasoning chains that include backtracking. At each node, the walk advances toward the target with probability 1-p or performs a reset back to the start node with probability p (a cyclic action). This setup creates an environment where "progressing" actions (choosing the correct edge) compete with "cyclic" actions..
Mechanism 1: Risk Aversion due to Hardness of Learning
The first mechanism explains why models prefer loops over progress. The central insight is that "difficulty" causes a diffusion of probability mass. According to the authors, in the star graph, the transition from the root to the correct spoke is "hard" because the model needs to distinguish between n indistinguishable outgoing edges. In contrast, the "reset" action is "easy"; it is the same action regardless of the target.
The authors explain this using the following proposition (which sounds logical overall, though still empirical): when a learner cannot distinguish between n competing "hard" actions, Maximum Likelihood Estimation forces it to distribute the probability mass uniformly among them (roughly 1/n for each). However, the "easy" cyclic action retains its full probability mass.
Consequently, even if the teacher-model performs the progressing action more often than the reset action (e.g., 0.7 vs. 0.3), the student-model sees the progress probability diluted (e.g., 0.7/n). If n is large enough, the probability of a single progress action drops below that of the cyclic action (0.3 > 0.7/n). Under greedy sampling (temperature 0), the model consistently chooses the cyclic action, effectively "playing it safe" by entering a loop instead of risking a low-confidence progress step. This is a model for "risk aversion": the model enters a loop because it is the "safest" move (having the highest probability) that it learned.
I'd say the comparison here is a bit far-fetched, but at least the line of thinking is quite interesting...
Mechanism 2: Inductive Bias for Temporally Correlated Errors
The second mechanism reveals why models enter a loop even without an aversion to excessive risk-taking. Here, the authors modify the task so that there is no "hard" choice at the root; the random walk simply chooses a child uniformly and randomly. Ideally, a perfect learner would assign uniform probability 1/n to all outgoing edges. However, Transformers develop temporally correlated errors. Small, random deviations in the learned probabilities (e.g., a slight preference for edge A over edge B) are not independent across time steps.
Since the Transformer conditions on the full history, if it preferred edge A during the first visit to the root, it tends to prefer edge A again when returning to the root later in the sequence. This correlation amplifies small initial errors into continuous loops. Under greedy sampling, the model "locks onto" a specific sub-optimal path, tries it repeatedly, fails, performs a reset, and then tries the exact same path again. This mirrors reasoning models where the system commits a logical error, backtracks, and then immediately repeats the exact same error.
Temperature: A Temporary Fix, Not a Complete Solution
The paper critically evaluates the use of temperature. While high temperature breaks loops by forcing exploration, it does not fix the distorted "probability surface." The authors show that while temperature reduces loop counts, the resulting lengths of reasoning chains remain significantly longer than those of a perfect learner. This confirms that temperature is a "band-aid" that alleviates the situation but leaves the inefficient and confused underlying model structure in place.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.12895

points.title: Niv and Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26
points.preview: Niv and Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26 Joint-Embedding vs Reconstruction: Provable Benefits of Latent Space Prediction for Self-Supervised Learning The reviewed paper compares the two central paradigms in the field of Self-Supervised Learning (or SSL for short) in recent years: the Reconstruction model and the Joint Embedding latent representation. Comparisons of this type are particularly interestin
points.body:
Niv and Mike's Daily Paper 11.01.26
Joint-Embedding vs Reconstruction: Provable Benefits of Latent Space Prediction for Self-Supervised Learning
The reviewed paper compares the two central paradigms in the field of Self-Supervised Learning (or SSL for short) in recent years: the Reconstruction model and the Joint Embedding latent representation.
Comparisons of this type are particularly interesting in light of the heating race towards AGI in recent years and the general hype surrounding generative models. At the same time, in the past year, we are seeing a slowdown in the rate of improvement of language models; consequently, there is a concern that the current paradigm (i.e., architecture, training method, inference methods, etc.) of LLMs has reached a saturation point in capabilities, and we need to start looking for the next thing that will provide a leap in capabilities.
A particularly prominent criticism of the paradigm of using LLMs comes from Yann LeCun, considered one of the founding fathers of DL (Deep Learning) and until recently the Chief AI Scientist at Meta. When asked about AGI (which happens quite often), Yann claims that "LLMs are a dead end," and generally argues that no generative model will bring us to AGI (in the recent podcast with Ravid Ziv Schwartz, LeCun claimed that there is no such thing as AGI, only HLI, meaning Human-Level Intelligence). The alternative he presents to LLMs is World Models and learning under the Joint Embedding paradigm. (Clarification: The main training stage of LLMs is pre-training using next-token-prediction; therefore, LLMs fall under the category of "reconstruction tasks" and are, of course, generative models). For an expansion on the subject, you are invited to view the paper A Path Towards Autonomous Machine Intelligence (2022) by LeCun.
Returning to the current paper, which deals with neither AGI nor HLI, but attempts to answer a much more modest question regarding SSL: Can one show an advantage for one SSL method over the other, or under what conditions is it preferable to use each one?
We will start with the conclusion reached in the paper, and then detail more for those interested in diving deeper. Interestingly, the analysis results divide into two main cases: in cases where the input is relatively "clean" (i.e., does not contain much noise), there is a preference for unsupervised learning using a reconstruction model; however, in cases where the input is "noisy," there is a preference for Joint Embedding methods. Regarding what input is considered "clean" and what input is considered "noisy," the authors offer a rough but likely not bad division: input emerging from an artificial simulation may be considered "clean," but any input related to the real world is likely "noisy" (the authors propose a relatively inclusive definition for the term "noise." Simply put, they define noise as components or features that appear in the input but are irrelevant. Under this definition, background in an image may also be considered noise).
Now for those interested in diving deeper, we will start by giving a short detail on each paradigm, and then detail the mathematical analysis performed in the paper.
Reconstruction Task: The well-known generative models fall under the category of models trained to perform reconstruction (GPT, Diffusion models, VAE), but there are additional model types (Denoising AutoEncoder, Masked AutoEncoder, etc.) trained to build an efficient vector representation (for a variety of data tasks). Briefly, these approaches can be described as methods attempting to reconstruct examples from partial information or from an example that underwent a certain transformation (noising, compression, partial masking, or other augmentation). Generative models are particularly useful in the case where the reconstruction problem is not single-valued, meaning there are multiple (or a sequence of) possible solutions (for example, completing a missing part of an image, or completing the next word in a text).
Joint Latent Representation Task: Unlike reconstruction models, these models focus on the latent (hidden) space rather than the input space. Their goal is to learn similar latent representations for different versions of sufficiently "similar" input, and simultaneously encourage high variance in the latent space when the inputs are of "different" examples (the precise definition of "similar" or "different" is actually distinct for each model; this is essentially where much of the "wisdom" in developing such models lies). Examples of well-known models under this paradigm are: BYOL, DINO, CLIP, MOCO.
Let us now approach mathematical analysis. The central assumption underlying the analysis is that the input can be represented in a linear basis composed of 2 orthogonal components defined as "signal" and "noise" (this assumption is equivalent to the assumption that there exists a linear transformation that will clean the "noise" components from the input). The great advantage of this assumption is that now a network with a single linear layer has sufficient representation power to completely clean the input (in such a case, the network would essentially be equivalent to a diagonalization transformation involving multiplying all "noise" components by zero). This assumption simplifies the problem so that closed analytic solutions can be reached, which can then be investigated and analyzed.
 In total, 3 types of models were analyzed:
Reconstruction Based SSL (RC-SSL)
Supervised Learning (SL)
Joint-Embedding Based SSL (JE-SSL)
Where in the case of SL and JE-SSL the network consists of an encoder only, which is a single linear layer, and in the case of RC-SSL the network consists of two layers, a linear encoder layer and a linear decoder layer.
Let’s start with an interesting comparison between SL and SSL. The mathematical analysis showed that for a sufficiently large dataset, the solution of a linear SL model yields a transformation that provides a clean representation of the input (i.e., completely zeros out the noise components). Whereas for a linear SSL model, even in the limit of an infinitely large dataset, a clean representation of the input is obtained only under certain conditions. In the case where these conditions are not met, noise components will manage to leak into the encoder's output layer.
Now we arrive at the central point emphasized by the researchers in the paper. The condition for obtaining a clean representation of the input for linear SSL models is augmentations that sufficiently simulate the noise. Before we continue, we want to clarify the role of augmentations in the models examined here. Since for SSL, training is performed without labels, the SSL training method examined here essentially treats every single input as having its own unique label. This is exactly where augmentations come into the picture; they are essentially transformations aimed at generating a variety of training examples for each input in the dataset (after all, one cannot learn from a single example for each label). Now, back to the paper’s results. Quite interestingly, the condition obtained from the mathematical analysis for the augmentations is not identical for the two SSL models. Specifically, in the case where noise intensity is low, the condition for augmentations was more lenient for the linear RC-SSL model compared to JE-SSL. And in the case where noise intensity is high, the result was reversed. That is, the condition for augmentations was more lenient for the linear JE-SSL model compared to RC-SSL. The conclusion is that when the input noise intensity is high, we expect better performance from a JE-SSL model.
Okay, so the mathematical analysis showed interesting conclusions, but the analysis took into account only linear networks. Are these conclusions of any relevance to deep networks? To examine exactly this question, the researchers performed simulations on standard deep network architectures of types ViT and ResNet. The simulations were performed on the well-known dataset ImageNet-100. Additionally, use was made of the ImageNet-C dataset, which is a noisy version of ImageNet-100 and even includes a classification of the images into different noise levels. First, the results showed that models trained using the JE-SSL method presented better results on ImageNet-100 than models trained using the RC-SSL method (no surprise here yet, identical results were observed in the past). But in addition to this, for the noisy dataset ImageNet-C, the results showed that as images with higher noise levels were used, there was a sharp drop in the performance of RC-SSL models. In contrast, JE-SSL models managed to maintain good performance and did not experience a sharp drop in performance even at particularly high noise levels! These results support the conclusion that RC-SSL models are less robust under conditions of high-intensity noise input.
Summary: In this paper, the ability of SL and SSL models to deal with noisy input was examined, an ability the researchers define as critical for the success of models in the real world. The analysis focuses on a particularly ideal and simple case, where the "noise" components are orthogonal to the "signal" components, a significantly lenient assumption relative to the complexity of input in the real world. Nevertheless, even in this lenient scenario, it was found that SSL-type learning is limited and fails to yield clean representations of the input, not even in the theoretical limit of an infinite dataset.
Among the 2 sub-families of models included under the definition of SSL, models based on Joint Embedding proved superior performance. If one accepts the assumption that the real world provides inherently noisy input, the paper's conclusions can be interpreted as a claim that the currently prevailing training method for LLMs is not ideal. From this arises the possibility that LLMs, in their current format, constitute a dead end on the path to achieving HLI. Conversely, approaches based on Joint Embedding, such as those proposed by LeCun and others, appear to be a more promising research direction.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.12477
The Transformer Goes on (Latent) Leave

points.title: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 14.01.26
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 14.01.26 The Free Transformer Despite its almost absolute dominance in the GenAI world, the standard decoder-only Transformer model (i.e., autoregressive) has several limitations. First of all, these models are pure autoregressive density (probability) estimators. They generate the next token based solely on the previous tokens. According to the paper's authors, this arc
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 14.01.26
The Free Transformer
Despite its almost absolute dominance in the GenAI world, the standard decoder-only Transformer model (i.e., autoregressive) has several limitations. First of all, these models are pure autoregressive density (probability) estimators. They generate the next token based solely on the previous tokens. According to the paper's authors, this architecture lacks an explicit mechanism for "planning" or making high-level latent decisions, such as determining the sentiment of a review or the topic of syntax, before the first token is generated. Instead, the model must maintain these decisions implicitly within the latent representations of the tokens themselves, a process that is computationally inefficient and prone to "drifting" off course if the first tokens are ambiguous.
This paper proposes the Free Transformer, an architecture that injects a Conditional Variational Autoencoder (or CVAE) directly into the LLM backbone. For those who don't remember, a VAE is a generative approach consisting of an encoder and a decoder, which are trained together to reconstruct data from the training set. The encoder maps the data to the latent space, and the decoder reconstructs it, where the distribution of the latent representations is induced by a given distribution, for example, a Gaussian. In data generation, one samples from this distribution and feeds the sampled representation into the decoder to generate data.
The authors propose an efficient implementation that offloads almost all the computational burden of the variational encoder to the generative LLM decoder (the standard causal one), leading to a modest overhead of only about 3% in parameters in the transformer. The key innovation is the integration of the VAE's latent variable Z within the LLM. In a standard VAE, the encoder and decoder are usually separate and heavy networks. The Free Transformer, however, splits the standard decoder into two halves.
The First Half, Causal: The input sequence is processed by the first half of the LLM decoder layers. This produces an intermediate representation of the sequence tokens.
Between Halves, Encoder, Training: The intermediate output from layer L/2 serves as the K and V vectors for the VAE encoder block. This encoder block is non-causal (takes into account all tokens of the sequence, which is important), allowing it to compress everything "that was in the sequence" into the intermediate representations. The encoder uses several (hyperparameter) learned vectors as Qs to extract an intermediate representation of the entire sequence.
Between Halves (Encoder), Inference: The encoder is not activated. The latent variable is sampled from a fixed uniform prior distribution (with which the VAE is trained).
The Completion (Layers L/2 + 1 to L): The sampled latent Z representation passes through a linear layer and is added directly to the K and V vectors of the first layer in the second half of the decoder. The remaining layers then continue with standard autoregressive generation, now conditioned on the "plan" that was injected.
The paper does not use Gaussian latent variables typical of VAEs in the image domain, for instance. Instead, Z is constructed as discrete 1-hot vectors. To enable end-to-end training through this discrete bottleneck, the authors introduce a Binary Mapper..
The encoder outputs parameters of a probability distribution corresponding to individual bits. These are sampled to produce a discrete integer index, which is then mapped to a multi-dimensional latent 1-hot vector (I couldn't quite get to the bottom of the authors' “reasoning chain” here 100%). In training, the authors use a gradient estimator combining the sampled value with probability gradients (similar to straight-through estimators) to allow the gradient to flow through the discrete decision (sampling) steps, similar to classic VAE.
A known failure in VAEs for text is "posterior collapse," where the decoder completely ignores the latent variable because the autoregressive prior is already too strong (essentially the reconstruction term in the loss). The authors mitigate its effect using the Free Bits method (specifically, hinge loss on the KL term). This forces the model to encode a minimal amount of information (defined by a threshold k) within the latent variable Z, ensuring that the decoder indeed uses the latent plan instead of bypassing it.
The Free Transformer is a kind of "split brain." It recognizes that while the autoregressive model is sufficient for probabilistic modeling, it is inefficient for inference or planning. By breaking the decoder in two and inserting a non-causal variational sampling step in the middle, it grants the model "freedom" to sample a high-level state Z independently of the immediate token history. This allows the model to lock onto a global decision (sort of), like the sign of the answer or the tone of a response, before generating a single word, thereby theoretically shifting part of the decision burden from long context to a latent variable.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.17558

points.title: Andrey and Mike’s Daily Paper, 17.01.26
points.preview: Andrey and Mike’s Daily Paper, 17.01.26 RAFT: Retrieval-Augmented Time Series Forecasting Introduction Traditional time series forecasting models typically rely on a parametric memorization approach; they attempt to encode and compress global temporal patterns into the model’s weights during the training phase. However, this paradigm faces two critical limitations: Overfitting: The model may "memo
points.body:
Andrey and Mike’s Daily Paper, 17.01.26
RAFT: Retrieval-Augmented Time Series Forecasting
Introduction
Traditional time series forecasting models typically rely on a parametric memorization approach; they attempt to encode and compress global temporal patterns into the model’s weights during the training phase. However, this paradigm faces two critical limitations:
Overfitting: The model may "memorize" stochastic noise that does not generalize to the inference phase.
Long-tail/Rare Events: The model often fails to adequately capture infrequent events that are underrepresented in the training distribution.
The authors of this paper propose a shift in paradigm: rather than forcing the model to internalize all data points, they implement an "open-book" architecture. In this framework, the model is granted access to an external historical database, allowing it to retrieve analogous past states to inform its future predictions.
This methodology synthesizes concepts from k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). By integrating an external memory, the system learns to leverage historical data in inference time. This approach enables lightweight, computationally efficient models to achieve superior performance compared to massive, high-parameter architectures that attempt to compress all information into their internal latent space.
Methodology
The RAFT (Retrieval-Augmented Time Series Forecasting) framework combines classical signal processing techniques with neural networks. A core architectural principle utilized is Channel Independence, where each variable (e.g., temperature, stock price) is processed independently. This allows the model to learn generalized temporal dynamics without the need to deal with noisy inter-channel correlations.
The pipeline consists of four primary stages:
Multi-Resolution Downsampling: Data is sampled at multiple temporal granularities (e.g., hourly and daily averages). This enriches the database and allows the model to capture structural similarities across different time scales.
Retrieval: This is the core mechanism, formulated as a Query-Key-Value (QKV) operation. The model treats the current lookback window as a Query, scans the historical training corpus (Keys) to identify similar temporal segments, and retrieves the subsequent actual realizations (Values) associated with those keys.
To quantify similarity, the researchers employ Pearson Correlation. By focusing on morphological similarity (the "shape" of the signal) rather than absolute magnitudes, the model achieves scale invariance—recognizing patterns even if the absolute values differ from historical instances.
Retrieval Aggregation: Analogous to the "Generation" phase in RAG, the model aggregates the retrieved trajectories (Values) to produce a "Reference Forecast." This serves as a non-parametric baseline representing the outcome of similar historical signals.
Prediction: A shallow Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) receives a concatenated input consisting of the current observed sequence and the synthesized reference forecast. The network’s objective is not to memorize the data, but rather to learn a function determining how to balance current observations against historical precedents to produce the final, high-precision forecast.
Results and Evaluation
The researchers benchmarked RAFT across 10 prominent datasets against current State-of-the-Art (SOTA) models such as PatchTST and DLinear.
The empirical results were highly significant:
RAFT achieved a Win Ratio of 86% against competing SOTA models.
The authors demonstrated that a simple MLP, when augmented with the RAFT retrieval mechanism, consistently outperforms significantly heavier and more complex architectures.
The retrieval-based approach proved particularly robust in scenarios involving noisy data or distributional shifts (rare patterns), as the model can rely on specific historical anchors"
Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that in the domain of time series forecasting, the capacity to retrieve relevant historical context is fundamentally more powerful than the attempt to compress all data into a monolithic model.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.04163v1

points.title: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 19.01.26
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 19.01.26 Deep Delta Learning The paper we will review today proposes an architectural improvement in ResNet areas for deep learning models (truthfully, there have been quite a few of these in the last two months, such as MHC, which will receive an in-depth review soon). Deep Residual Networks (ResNets) work because they allow information to flow easily through what is ca
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 19.01.26
Deep Delta Learning
The paper we will review today proposes an architectural improvement in ResNet areas for deep learning models (truthfully, there have been quite a few of these in the last two months, such as MHC, which will receive an in-depth review soon).
Deep Residual Networks (ResNets) work because they allow information to flow easily through what is called an identity shortcut, constituting a "cable" that adds the input directly to the output. While this additive structure prevents gradients from vanishing, it, according to the authors, causes the network to be "shift-biased." Simply put, standard layers shift features from place to place but struggle to rotate them or flip their direction. This rigidity makes it difficult to model complex changes, especially those requiring "negative eigenvalues," such as oscillations or oppositional dynamics.
The Deep Delta Learning (DDL) method solves this by upgrading the skip connection into a programmable geometric operator (possessing learnable features). Instead of simply adding F(x) to x, this architecture weights the identity path (x) itself via a learnable transformation derived from a Householder reflection (quite surprisingly, I learned this for the first time 20 years ago when I started working at a company developing chips for wireless communication). This allows the layer to switch (dynamically) between passing the signal unchanged, projecting it onto a subspace, or fully reflecting it.
The central mechanism is the "Delta Operator," a learnable matrix acting on the hidden state (representation). Mathematically, this is a transformation (in my opinion affine, but I might be missing something here) with a rank of 1 relative to the identity matrix I, defined by two key learnable parts: a reflection direction vector k and a scalar gate beta. Both direction k and beta are computed with shallow networks with learnable weights.
In a standard ResNet, the update is x + F(x). In DDL, the operator first modifies x by projecting it onto direction k and scaling with beta, and finally subtracting them from the original representation. The authors provide a spectral analysis showing that the operator has eigenvalues equal to 1, except for one. This single eigenvalue is determined by the gate beta, allowing the layer to manipulate the length of the representation space along exactly one axis, while leaving the orthogonal subspace unchanged.
The scalar gate beta serves as a continuous factor controlling the layer's geometry. By constraining this gate to the range between 0 and 2, the authors essentially define three distinct linear algebra operations within a single differentiable block:
Identity (beta close to 0): The operator becomes the identity matrix. The layer acts like a standard skip connection as in ResNet, preserving the signal. This is critical for maintaining the stability of deep networks.
Projection (beta close to 1): The operator becomes an orthogonal projector. It completely erases the input component (=the original representation from the previous layes) aligned with direction k. This allows the network to "forget" specific features before adding new ones.
Reflection (beta close to 2): [Note: The source text repeats "Projection" here, but contextually refers to Reflection]. The operator becomes a Householder matrix. The eigenvalue along k becomes -1, flipping the sign of features along this direction. This provides the ability to reverse direction, a geometric capability lacking in additive ResNets.
The update rule ensures that the gate (scalar) beta controls both the removal of old information and the injection of new values. This effectively implements the Delta Rule, a mechanism famous in fast associative memory models, throughout the network's depth. If the layer decides to delete a feature (via projection), it simultaneously writes a new value in its place. If it decides to reflect the feature, it adjusts the new value to match that direction. This synchronization prevents the "residual accumulation" problem, where new features simply pile up on top of old, noisy features.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.00417v1

points.title: The Asaf and Mike’s Daily Paper 23.01.26, SFT Memorizes, RL Generalizes: A Comparative Study of Foundation Model Post-training
points.preview: Background The paper examines a fundamental question in training foundation models: How do common post-training techniques, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), impact a model's ability to generalize instructions and logic to unseen situations (Out of Distribution). The central conclusion is definitive: while SFT tends to cause the model to "memorize" the data and instruct
points.body:
Background
The paper examines a fundamental question in training foundation models: How do common post-training techniques, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL), impact a model's ability to generalize instructions and logic to unseen situations (Out of Distribution). The central conclusion is definitive: while SFT tends to cause the model to "memorize" the data and instructions it was trained on, RL-based training encourages significantly better generalization in both textual and visual tasks.
Methodology and Experimental Environments
The researchers utilized a multimodal model (Llama-3.2-Vision-11B) and evaluated it on two tasks designed to test reasoning and generalization:
GeneralPoints: An arithmetic card game (a variation of "24"), requiring the model to reach a target number using arithmetic operations on four cards. Input was provided via text or image (to test the VLM).
V-IRL: A real-world navigation simulator (based on maps of cities like New York), requiring spatial reasoning and identification of visual landmarks to execute complex navigation instructions.
The experiments were designed to isolate In-Distribution data from Out-of-Distribution (OOD) examples by varying instructions and visual elements. For example, the model might be trained on instructions where Jack, King, and Queen are all worth 10, but tested on instructions where they are worth 11–13; or trained on navigation in New York and tested in other cities worldwide.
Findings
The study reveals a dramatic performance gap between the two approaches:
SFT: The model shows high performance on the data it was trained on but collapses when faced with OOD examples. For instance, in the navigation task (V-IRL-L), performance plummeted from 80.8% to a mere 1.3% when transitioning to a new set of instructions. This implies that SFT causes the model to memorize rather than generalize.
RL: The model remains stable and even improves when transitioning to OOD examples. In the same navigation task, the RL-trained model improved from 80.8% to 91.8% upon switching to the new instruction set. This finding indicates that RL, guided by an outcome-based reward function, learns generalizable problem-solving strategies.
Improving Visual Perception: One of the surprising insights concerns Multimodal Models (VLMs). It was found that RL does not only improve reasoning but also actively enhances the model's low-level capabilities in identifying visual objects. In experiments, increasing training resources (compute) for RL led to improved accuracy in identifying cards or landmarks. Conversely, increasing SFT compute actually led to a decline in visual capabilities. The hypothesis is that SFT causes overfitting on textual reasoning tokens at the expense of vision capabilities.
The Critical Role of SFT as a "Format Teacher": Despite RL’s success in generalization, the paper emphasizes that SFT cannot be discarded entirely. Experiments attempting to train RL directly on the base model failed to follow instructions effectively. SFT is required to stabilize the model's output format and enable it to follow basic instructions, allowing the RL phase to begin learning effectively. In this sense, SFT acts as a "Format Teacher" that prepares the ground for the actual learning that occurs during RL.
Training Scaling: Increasing compute resources in RL training improves visual recognition accuracy (unlike SFT scaling, which degrades it), while increasing the number of Verification steps under the same budget consistently and significantly improves the model's ability to generalize.
Summary and Implications
The paper provides empirical evidence that while classical SFT training may provide excellent performance on In-Distribution data, this is a result of memorization and produces models that fail when faced with slight distributional shifts. The paper suggests that to build robust systems capable of true reasoning and generalization, RL must be integrated with outcome-based reward functions, using SFT only as an initialization phase.
However, RL training is not a universal "magic bullet." It requires an objective and unambiguous "Verifier" function, which exists in logical tasks (such as calculating if an equation reaches 24 or reaching a destination in navigation) but is absent in "soft" tasks (such as creative writing or document summarization). In those cases, expensive and noisier Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is required. Furthermore, due to high computational costs and the need for high-quality SFT initialization, RL is not justified for simple tasks where pattern memorization provides an efficient solution.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2501.17161

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 23.01.26
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 23.01.26 REPO: LANGUAGE MODELS WITH CONTEXT RE-POSITIONING For nearly a decade, the Transformer architecture has operated under a rigid assumption: the position of a token is an immutable integer index, strictly increasing and linear. Whether using absolute embeddings or relative encodings like Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), models force a fixed geometric structure on
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 23.01.26
REPO: LANGUAGE MODELS WITH CONTEXT RE-POSITIONING
For nearly a decade, the Transformer architecture has operated under a rigid assumption: the position of a token is an immutable integer index, strictly increasing and linear. Whether using absolute embeddings or relative encodings like Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE), models force a fixed geometric structure onto the context. Token n is always exactly one unit of distance away from token n-1. This paper argues that such rigidity imposes "extraneous cognitive load" on the model, effectively wasting working memory on organizing information that should be dynamically structured by relevance rather than mere arrival order.
The authors introduce REPO (Context Re-Positioning), a method that fundamentally decouples a token's physical location in the prompt from its "positional" coordinate used in attention calculations. Instead of relying on pre-defined integer ranges, REPO learns to assign continuous, real-valued positions to tokens based on their semantic content. This allows the model to warp the geometry of the context window, effectively folding space to bring distant, relevant information closer together while pushing noise into the periphery.
The methodology centers on a differentiable module, denoted as f_ϕ, which is injected into the architecture prior to the standard positional encoding step. Unlike standard approaches that map a hard-coded integer index to a vector, f_ϕ operates directly on the token's hidden state. This implies that a token's "position" becomes a function of what the token is, not just where it sits in the sequence.
The process operates in two distinct mathematical stages. First, the module extracts a "position representation" from the hidden state using a SwiGLU sub-layer. This gate-and-content mapping mechanism allows the model to filter and transform semantic features into a latent representation specifically optimized for determining spatial relationships. Second, a linear transformation projects this representation into a scalar, real-valued position coordinate. Because these coordinates are continuous, the model is not constrained to monotonic or integer assignments; it can place multiple tokens at nearly identical positions to simulate a "constant" encoding, or spread them out to enforce strong locality.
The genius of this approach lies in its seamless integration with existing differentiable position encoding schemes, particularly RoPE. In a standard RoPE implementation, the attention score is modulated by a rotation matrix derived from the fixed integer difference between a query index and a key index. REPO hijacks this process. It substitutes the fixed integer difference with the difference between the learned, continuous position scalars generated by f_ϕ..
Crucially, this modification preserves end-to-end differentiability. During backpropagation, the gradients flow through the attention scores, through the rotation function, and back into the position-assignment module. The model effectively learns to "slide" tokens along the positional axis to maximize attention utility. If a distant "needle" token is critical for a specific query, the gradient descent process drives the module to assign it a position coordinate numerically close to the query, neutralizing the "long-distance" penalty typically imposed by RoPE.
The authors apply this mechanism independently to each attention head. This is a vital architectural detail: it means different heads can learn different topologies for the same sequence. One head might maintain a linear structure to track local syntax, while another collapses the entire context into a dense cluster to perform global aggregation, and yet another inverts the sequence or segments it by topic. This mirrors the flexibility of the "NoPE" (No Positional Encoding) approach but makes it a learnable subset of a broader geometric framework rather than a hard architectural choice.
By treating position as a learned latent variable rather than a fixed metadata tag, REPO transforms the context window from a rigid timeline into a malleable manifold. It offers a mathematically elegant path toward models that organize their own working memory, dynamically reducing the cognitive load required to parse noisy or massive contexts.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.14391

points.title: Mike's daily paper review: 28.01.26
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 28.01.26 DOPE: Denoising Rotary Position Embedding Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) has effectively won the positional encoding wars, becoming the default standard for LLMs like LLaMA and Qwen. We implicitly trust it to inject relative order into the attention mechanism. However, DOPE (Denoising Rotary Position Embedding) challenges this trust, arguing that RoPE is not m
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 28.01.26
DOPE: Denoising Rotary Position Embedding
Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) has effectively won the positional encoding wars, becoming the default standard for LLMs like LLaMA and Qwen. We implicitly trust it to inject relative order into the attention mechanism. However, DOPE (Denoising Rotary Position Embedding) challenges this trust, arguing that RoPE is not merely a passive ruler but an active amplifier that can destabilize long-context performance.
This paper offers a rigorous spectral analysis of why models "break" on long sequences. The authors identify a structural flaw in RoPE's low-frequency bands that creates "massive activations", attention heads that essentially scream static noise rather than attending to context.
The Physics of the Scream: Cone Constraints and Spectral Amplification
The core novelty here lies in the "physical picture" the authors construct regarding RoPE’s behavior in the frequency domain. RoPE works by rotating query and key vectors. In high-frequency bands, these vectors spin rapidly, creating a diverse distribution of angles. However, in low-frequency bands, the rotation is glacial.
The authors formalize this as the Cone Constraint. For standard sequence lengths, the rotation phases of low-frequency bands are so small that the vectors never wrap around the unit circle. Instead, they remain trapped within a narrow angular "cone." Because the query and key vectors in these bands are all pointing in roughly the same direction, their dot products do not cancel out; they accumulate constructively.
This leads to Spectral Amplification. The authors prove that the dominant eigenvalue (or singular value) of the attention matrix in these bands scales linearly with the sequence length. As the context grows, the energy in these low-frequency components explodes, creating an "attention sink." The model is forced to dump this excess attention mass onto specific tokens (often the start of the sequence), creating a low-rank, over-aligned attention pattern that drowns out the actual semantic signal.
The Diagnostic: Truncated Matrix Entropy
To fix this, one must first identify the infected heads. The authors propose Truncated Matrix Entropy (TME) as a surgical diagnostic tool. Standard matrix entropy (calculated over all singular values) is too blunt because it gets washed out by the "long tail" of small singular values.
TME focuses exclusively on the top-r singular values.
Low TME: Indicates that the head’s energy is aggressively concentrated in just one or two dominant directions (the "scream"). These are the heads suffering from spectral amplification and attention sinks.
High TME: Indicates a healthy, high-rank distribution where attention is spread across multiple latent dimensions.
The novelty here is the inversion of standard intuition: typically, we prize low entropy (certainty) in predictions, but in the internal attention manifold, low entropy signals a collapse of representational capacity, a head stuck in a "cone" loop.
The Intervention: Selective Denoising
Once the "noisy" (low TME) heads are identified, DOPE applies a training-free intervention to silence them. The authors propose a few variants, but the two most notable are:
DoPE-by-Parts (Frequency Masking): This is the most precise cut. For the identified heads, the method masks out only the specific low-frequency bands that violate the cone constraint. It leaves the high-frequency bands (which capture local texture and precise position) intact, effectively acting as a high-pass filter for attention.
DoPE-by-Gaussian (Reparameterization or factoring q and k vectors by a standard gaussian noise): This is the more aggressive, "soft" lobotomy. For heads that are entirely dominated by massive activations, DOPE suppresses their positional encoding entirely and reparameterizes their attention maps with an isotropic Gaussian distribution. This forces the attention mechanism back into a smooth, high-entropy state, preventing the spiky, low-rank behavior that causes instability during extrapolation.
Summary
DOPE fundamentally changes how we view RoPE. It suggests that "positional awareness" is not always a virtue; for certain heads, explicit position encoding acts as a structured noise generator that must be suppressed. By dynamically identifying and denoising these low-rank heads, DOPE allows the model to extrapolate to lengths far beyond training (e.g., 64k tokens) without the need for expensive fine-tuning, simply by turning down the volume on the frequencies that hurt the most.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.09146

points.title: Daily paper review by Nimrod and Mike, 30.01.26: mHC: Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections
points.preview: In January 2025, an unknown Chinese company called DeepSeek shook the AI world with models that beat all the major benchmarks. Exactly one year later, at the start of January 2026, after a long time without truly new architectural innovations in deep learning, the Chinese team brought a small, elegant idea that directly affects one of the most painful issues in ML: training stability. One of the b
points.body:
In January 2025, an unknown Chinese company called DeepSeek shook the AI world with models that beat all the major benchmarks. Exactly one year later, at the start of January 2026, after a long time without truly new architectural innovations in deep learning, the Chinese team brought a small, elegant idea that directly affects one of the most painful issues in ML: training stability.
One of the biggest problems in training deep models in the previous decade was called exploding/vanishing gradients. Essentially, as more layers were added, the model became harder to train, information was “lost” along the way, and training simply broke. The solution was simple: instead of each layer learning something completely new, it would only learn a small correction: a residual.
A residual connection in neural networks is implemented so that the input of a layer is added directly to its output:
Res(x) = f(x) + x.
This creates a “shortcut” (skip path) that helps gradients flow backward during training. The critical property here is identity mapping: the function F can learn to return zero, in which case the layer simply passes the input forward unchanged. This is one of the main reasons deep networks are trainable at all.
Simply put, adding residuals changed training so that layers have the option to stay close to the representation coming from the previous layer, which dramatically improved stability. However, residual connections implicitly assume that the connected representations live on the same geometric surface (manifold). When representations from different layers are combined without an explicit constraint, this assumption does not necessarily hold. What happens when it doesn’t? Noisy representations. Training still “works,” but stability and generalization suffer.
After years where no one really looked at this area, DeepSeek came up with the idea of Manifold Hyper-Connections (mHC). If a standard residual connection includes a single skip path, Hyper-Connections expand this to n parallel paths. Each of the n paths is connected with a weight matrix, but without any constraint. As a result, in HC the combined representations do not necessarily preserve the identity-mapping property. That is, composing the paths can cause uncontrolled amplification or suppression of signals across layers, leading to unstable gradient norms and exploding/vanishing signals as the network gets deeper.
