The authors use these values to calculate a weighted average of the heads, prioritizing those with slower convergence rates to capture more distinct features.
The method relies on the assumption of square attention matrices, which limits its direct application to self-attention or cases where the sequence lengths of Q and K are identical. Cross-attention matrices represent a failure case, as they are typically rectangular and contain inaccessible states, preventing the construction of a standard Markov chain. The paper argues that this could be resolved by introducing dummy states with uniform transition probabilities; however, it does not detail the exact parameters for such an implementation. Regarding complexity, while calculating the second eigenvalue is noted as resource-intensive, the iterations for TokenRank are efficient, typically converging within 10 to 20 steps.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.17657

points.title: Review 585: Beyond the Conceptual Graph: The Differential Dynamics of Reasoning Flow in Language Models
points.preview: Mike's Daily Paper Review: 02.03.26 THE GEOMETRY OF REASONING: FLOWING LOGICS IN REPRESENTATION SPACE Continuing the reviews on the geometry of the (latent) representation space of language models. We have already covered Lie algebras, statistical and Riemannian manifolds, and Fisher metrics; today, it is the turn of flows describing the geometry of reasoning. Papers in the field of model interpre
points.body:
Mike's Daily Paper Review: 02.03.26
THE GEOMETRY OF REASONING: FLOWING LOGICS IN REPRESENTATION SPACE
Continuing the reviews on the geometry of the (latent) representation space of language models. We have already covered Lie algebras, statistical and Riemannian manifolds, and Fisher metrics; today, it is the turn of flows describing the geometry of reasoning.
Papers in the field of model interpretability often focus on probes of static properties, but understanding complex reasoning processes requires a dynamic view of how models move through their internal representations. This paper presents a geometric framework that treats reasoning as a smooth flow within the model's representation space. By modeling the reasoning process as a trajectory along a "concept manifold," the researchers apply tools from differential geometry to measure the velocity and direction of thought. Here, the concept manifold is a low-dimensional surface within the representation space where related concepts cluster. The central innovation is the conceptualization of logic as a local controller that imposes differential constraints on the flow velocity in the representation space, thereby disentangling the logical structure from specific semantic carriers. The proposed method enables a formal analysis of how the model's internal representations evolve as a continuous process rather than a series of discrete representation vectors.
As stated, reasoning flow is a geometric model that interprets the internal computations of LLMs as continuous trajectories within the representation space. The authors construct these flows using a context-cumulative algorithm. Here, the input consists of a sequence of reasoning steps, and the output is a series of vectors where the embedding of each step is calculated by processing the full cumulative prefix of the input up to the last token of that reasoning step. This methodology differs from the analysis of individual tokens, as it explicitly accounts for how the model's internal state evolves and changes as new information is added to the context.
The paper formulates the "Smooth Trajectory Hypothesis". The insight is that although language models produce discrete tokens, their internal representations are actually samples from a continuous and differentiable curve, that is, a trajectory on which an instantaneous rate of change can be calculated. To establish this theoretically, the researchers introduce the "Relaxed Prefix-Mask mechanism". This is a conceptual tool where tokens are not included as discrete inputs but are integrated via a smooth function that gradually increases their influence (meaning the token's influence rises progressively). This smoothing of the input justifies the use of differential geometry tools, such as the calculation of flow velocity (the instantaneous rate of change of the representation) and its curvature.
To quantify the geometric properties of these paths, the framework uses "Menger curvature". Menger curvature is a metric defined for three distinct points in space as the reciprocal of the radius of the unique circle passing through them. This is a non-trivial choice compared to standard "cosine similarity" (a similarity measure between two vectors). While cosine similarity depends only on the angle, Menger curvature combines information about the angle and the distance between embedding points, allowing it to serve as a geometric proxy for the intensity of the logical step.
The methodology is designed to disentangle logical structure from semantic carriers (the topical or linguistic details of the text). The researchers use a natural deduction system, a formal logical framework where conclusions are derived from premises using fixed inference rules. By maintaining an identical logical skeleton while varying the topic and language, the method examines whether the flow's geometric properties are consistent (invariant) across changes in surface content. Logic is modeled as a differential controller that dictates the embedding trajectory's velocity regardless of the specific words used.
The approach relies on the "Linear Representation Hypothesis," for language models the assumption that concepts are represented as directions within the embedding space. However, it extends this by allowing these paths to exist on curved manifolds and not just in linear subspaces. The computational complexity of tracking these trajectories scales linearly with the number of reasoning steps, as curvature and velocity are calculated using a sliding window of successive reasoning steps.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.09782

points.title: Review 586: Symmetry as the Structural Engine of Deep Representations
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 04.03.26, Review 586 INTRINSIC TASK SYMMETRY DRIVES GENERALIZATION IN ALGORITHMIC TASKS I’ve fallen a bit in love with the internal geometry of representations in deep models, and Review #586 is dedicated to this topic. In my opinion, it’s actually less "tough" than the previous ones. The paper models the emergence of "structural" representations in neural networks durin
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 04.03.26, Review 586
INTRINSIC TASK SYMMETRY DRIVES GENERALIZATION IN ALGORITHMIC TASKS
I’ve fallen a bit in love with the internal geometry of representations in deep models, and Review #586 is dedicated to this topic. In my opinion, it’s actually less "tough" than the previous ones.
The paper models the emergence of "structural" representations in neural networks during the learning of algorithmic tasks, focusing specifically on the transition from memorization to true generalization (i.e., the ability to solve algorithmic problems of the type they were trained on). As mentioned, the central object of the study is the geometry of the representation/embedding space, the "spatial arrangement" of vector representations of tokens, such as numbers or nodes in a graph (some algorithmic questions are in the graph domain). The researchers examined how these representations evolve when the model is required to predict outputs for operations such as modular addition, geodesic distance on graphs, and comparisons between pairs of numbers.
The authors propose a training process characterized by three essential stages:
Memorization: The model reduces training loss by fitting specific input-output pairs without grasping the underlying rule.
Symmetry Acquisition: The model internalizes the task's internal symmetries. These are the formal invariants of the data, such as commutativity (the order of inputs does not affect the output) or transitivity (if A > B and B > C, then A must be > C).
Geometric Arrangement: The representations collapse into low-dimensional manifolds.
The Loss Function and Training
The training loss is the sum of cross-entropy and an auxiliary loss called Symmetry Prompting Loss, which penalizes the model when its outputs for symmetry-equivalent inputs are asymmetrical. To measure this divergence, the authors use Symmetric KL (quite similar to Jensen-Shannon divergence). For example, in modular addition, the loss penalizes the difference between the model's output for x + y and its prediction for y + x.
To further guide the model, the authors use priors that "enforce geometric properties." These include:
Nuclear Norm Regularization: The sum of the singular values of a matrix, used to encourage a low-rank representation, a state where the data can be described by a small number of independent factors (directions).
Entropy Regularization: Used to prevent scattered, high-energy embeddings.
Lipschitz Regularization: A constraint on how much the output can change relative to changes in the input, thereby enforcing local smoothness in the representation space.
Comparison to Grokking
The main difference between this method and standard explanations for the Grokking phenomenon is the focus on data-centric invariance rather than optimization-centric compression. Standard baselines often attribute the transition to generalization to weight decay (a regularization technique that penalizes large parameter values to prevent overfitting).
This paper, however, argues that while weight decay stabilizes representations, the task's internal symmetry is what determines the specific shape of the learned geometry. For example, the authors provide a theoretical explanation using Pontryagin Duality—a mathematical framework linking locally compact Abelian groups to their dual structures—to explain why modular addition necessarily induces a closed helical geometry in the embedding space. (I admit I’m no expert on these matters and had to use Sonnet to understand what was being discussed here).
Assumptions and Complexity
The proposed method assumes that the algorithmic task has identifiable fundamental symmetries that can be sampled during training:
For graph tasks, it relies on the triangle inequality (the distance between two points on the shortest path equals the sum of distances through an intermediate point).
For comparison tasks, it assumes a global linear order.
In terms of complexity, the computational cost of the symmetry prompting loss increases linearly with the number of symmetries sampled from the data. This is a significant improvement over a brute-force search for invariants, as it allows for targeted regularization during the training loop. Inference remains standard, as the auxiliary loss and structural constraints are only active during the optimization of the model parameters. The result is a representation that, once organized, allows the model to "react" to previously unseen samples by projecting them onto the structured manifold established during the symmetry-learning phase.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.01968

points.title: Review 587: Generative Modeling via Drift Fields
points.preview: Daily paper review by Niv and Mike, 08.03.26, Review 587 Generative Modeling via Drifting We are already accustomed to hearing about physical processes in the context of generative modeling. After diffusion models and flow models, is the next thing Drifting models? The reviewed paper presents a new approach to generative modeling, which allows it to reach state-of-the-art performance in single-ste
points.body:
Daily paper review by Niv and Mike, 08.03.26, Review 587
Generative Modeling via Drifting
We are already accustomed to hearing about physical processes in the context of generative modeling. After diffusion models and flow models, is the next thing Drifting models? The reviewed paper presents a new approach to generative modeling, which allows it to reach state-of-the-art performance in single-step image generation.
Single-step generation (one-step-generation) has been a hot research field in recent years, and particularly in the past year, where the primary motivation is reducing the high operational costs of models that generate images and video. The cost reduction potential is at least an order of magnitude relative to currently leading methods that require a large number of steps, around 20 and even up to 50.
Beyond efficiency, drifting models have another interesting property. They allow the integration of knowledge distillation from foundational models into the training process, so the model receives guidance to improve not only the visual quality of the images it produces but also the semantic meaning they carry.
Background on Diffusion and Flow Models
Generally, generative modeling can be formulated as learning a mapping/transformation from an input distribution that is simple to sample (usually Gaussian noise) to a desired output distribution (data). Diffusion models and flow models are the leading approaches today for continuous and multi-dimensional distributions, for example, for image, video, and audio generation tasks. Diffusion and flow models model the mapping from input to output as a multi-stage and autoregressive process i.e., as a series of states, where the first state is a noise sample and the last state is a piece of data (e.g., an image). Usually, each state in the series contains less noise than the previous state.
In the training phase, the model learns the dynamics between one state and the next in the sequence, so that in inference, we can progress step-by-step from noise to data. The difference between diffusion and flow models lies in the type of dynamics learned. Diffusion models learn dynamics inspired by the physical process of "diffusion," similar to the movement of a particle affected by random collisions. In flow models, at least in modern versions like Flow Matching, the learned dynamics are simpler, simulating the movement of a particle moving in a straight line.
Single-step Generation is Not a Simple Problem
If we try to train a diffusion or flow model to perform single-step generation naively, we will encounter a classic problem: from noise alone, it is impossible to infer what the "correct image" is, and therefore under standard loss functions (e.g., regression to a target point), the model will converge to an average solution, meaning we will get a collapse of the generative model.
Single-step methods exist in the literature that succeed in addressing the collapse problem: shortcut models, consistency models, and the like. They are interesting in their own right, and some even present impressive results, but generally speaking, it can be said that their performance is currently at a significant gap compared to multi-step methods.
Drifting Models Reformulate the Task in a Way That Prevents Collapse
This is where the paper's twist comes in. Instead of formulating the task as "given noise, give me a specific image," the paper redefines the training task so that it does not refer to a single sample but to a population of samples. Accordingly, the loss function does not define a target for each image individually, but describes a drift field determined from the interaction between all samples.
How the drift field calculation looks in practice:
Sample a batch of generated samples from the model. We emphasize that the model produces an image (sample) from noise in a single step.
In parallel, sample a batch of real images from the dataset.
Define a drift field composed of two opposing forces:
An attractive force towards the data (attraction)
A repulsive force between the generated samples (repulsion)
The intuition here is simple: the "attractive" force tries to bring the generated samples closer to the data cloud, and the "repulsive" force scatters the generated samples from one another, thereby preventing collapse.
After defining the drift field for the generated samples, the model's training loop can be completed relatively simply:
Update the generated samples by a small step in the direction dictated by the drift field. This step can be thought of as a local "correction" of the samples: the drift field pushes each sample to be closer to the data cloud, but simultaneously to remain scattered relative to the other generated samples.
The new samples serve as target points: freeze them using a stop-grad operation, so that the gradient does not flow through the calculation of the drift field itself.
Calculate the network's loss function as the distance between the samples the model produced and the target points obtained after the drift field update. Training pushes the network so that in the next iteration, it will produce samples closer to the target points. That is, samples that are already located closer to a state that balances attraction towards the data and repulsion between the samples themselves.
After a large number of iterations, the network learns to produce a population of samples in which the drift field becomes zero (stable equilibrium point). Under reasonable mathematical assumptions, this state indicates that the set of generated images "covers" the set of real images from the dataset well.
Knowledge Distillation from Foundation Models and Semantic Consistency
One of the interesting properties of foundation models is their ability to learn strong semantic representations. This is in contrast to generative models, which are often characterized by semantically weaker representations. As a result, many generation methods rely on an external foundation model to guide the image creation process and maintain semantic coherence. However, this reliance is not perfect: sometimes it is unstable during the training of the generative model, and generally, it adds additional complexity to the method and even harms its reliability.
A central research topic today in diffusion and flow-type generative models is how to train a generative model with stronger semantic representations, so that it can operate independently and without dependence on an external foundation model. This is where another interesting property of drifting models comes in: the possibility of projecting the drift field onto semantic spaces as well. The practical meaning is that the attraction of the samples towards the data is not defined only by distance in pixel space, but can also be defined by distance in the representation spaces of foundation models, such as CLIP or DINO.
In other words, instead of relying on a foundation model during generation, the semantic knowledge of such a model can be distilled already during training. The ability to define the drift field in different spaces allows the generative model to match the data not only visually but also in terms of the semantic meaning of the samples it produces. The result is an induction of semantic knowledge into the generative model itself. This may enable future generative models that operate independently of an external foundation model, thereby also creating simpler and more reliable systems.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.04770

points.title: Review 588: Fourier Geometry of Semantic Symmetries
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 11.03.26, Review 588 SYMMETRY IN LANGUAGE STATISTICS SHAPES THE GEOMETRY OF MODEL REPRESENTATIONS We continue with reviews on the topic of language model geometry; I promised there would be more reviews, and I am delivering... The paper we are reviewing develops an analytical framework that explains why Large Language Models (LLMs) organize conceptual data (i.e., various
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 11.03.26, Review 588
SYMMETRY IN LANGUAGE STATISTICS SHAPES THE GEOMETRY OF MODEL REPRESENTATIONS
We continue with reviews on the topic of language model geometry; I promised there would be more reviews, and I am delivering...
The paper we are reviewing develops an analytical framework that explains why Large Language Models (LLMs) organize conceptual data (i.e., various concepts) into structured manifolds. As a reminder, a manifold is defined as a geometric structure that looks like a simple surface around every point (i.e., like R^d). By linking the statistical symmetries of natural language to the spectral properties of token co-occurrence in text, the authors derive the geometric prototypes of the model's internal representations. Broadly speaking, the paper builds a mapping between co-occurrence matrices and the model's internal representations, and sometimes this mapping is quite non-trivial.
The central object of the study is the internal vector representation of a word, modeled as a projection onto a space spanned by the eigenvectors corresponding to the largest eigenvalues of a normalized co-occurrence matrix. Remember GloVe and Word2Vec from 10 years ago? Those approaches also used word co-occurrence statistics (one explicitly and the other implicitly). The authors first construct this matrix to build a "normalized pointwise mutual information (PMI) matrix" (excess co-occurrence), which measures how much more frequently two words appear together compared to a random, independent state. The core algorithm relies on the identification of a "latent semantic lattice," a set of tokens representing points on a "continuous" concept such as a timeline or a map.
The authors utilize "translation symmetry," which is the assumption that the probability of co-occurrence between two words depends solely on the distance between them in the latent space. For cyclic concepts like the 12 months of the year, this symmetry ensures that the co-occurrence matrix will be "circulant"—a state where each row is a shifted version of the previous row. In linear algebra, such matrices are diagonalized by "Fourier vectors," which are periodic sine and cosine functions that form the basis for the circular geometry observed in the model's embeddings.
What does this actually mean? The researchers' theory states that the model learns to represent concepts using periodic functions (sine and cosine). Every word has a position on a hidden conceptual "axis" (for example, January is at position 1, February at position 2, and so on). The word's position in the sequence (such as its place in the calendar year) translates into a value that behaves like an angle. If we imagine the year as a circle, January would be at 30 degrees, February at 60 degrees, and March at 90 degrees. When looking at the model's representations in two dimensions (e.g., using PCA for the two main axes), one finds that the first axis of the model encodes the cosine of that angle, and the second axis encodes its sine. Since any point defined by the sine and cosine of the same angle must lie at an equal distance from the center, the words are "forced" to arrange themselves in the shape of a circle in the model's representation space. In this way, the mathematical structure of the model reflects the periodicity of real-world concepts (sort of).
For linear sequences defined within a finite range, such as historical years, the paper shows that language statistics generate a Toeplitz-type data structure, where the affinity between two years depends solely on the chronological distance between them. To extract the geometric shape from these statistics, the authors assume that the affinity between years decays rapidly as the distance between them increases, an assumption represented by an exponential kernel. The analysis turns into solving a Sturm-Liouville problem, a mathematical method for calculating the fundamental vibration patterns of a system within a closed segment (like a guitar string). The resulting eigenvectors are (sampled) sine waves that must fit exactly along the time axis, similar to how a guitar string held at both ends can vibrate only at specific frequencies.
The geometric "ripples" characterizing the representation of years in the model are explained as higher harmonics of those same waves. A harmony is (remember wavelets?) a higher frequency of the fundamental wave, and when several such frequencies combine, they create tiny twists and delicate curves along the geometric manifold. This accumulation causes the timeline to appear wrinkled and complex in the vector space instead of remaining a smooth, straight line.
The method explores additional "collective effects" to explain why these structures are preserved even when specific co-occurrences between words are missing. The authors propose a latent variable model where "seasonal" or "geographical" words act as anchors. For example, words like "beach" or "ski" are modulated by the same latent variable (the time of year) as the months themselves. This creates a low-rank structure in the statistics, meaning the most important geometric information is concentrated in a few dominant directions.
The authors also derive an analytical bound for "linear coordinate decoding," which is the process of extracting numerical values (like a year) from a vector using a simple linear transformation. They derive a bound showing that numerical values can be extracted from the vectors via linear decoding, where the error decays according to a "volume argument." This argument states that the ability to compress units of information (wave vectors) depends on the concept's dimension: for time (1D), the accuracy improves linearly with the amount of information preserved, while for geography (2D), the improvement is slower and follows a square root, as much more information is required to "cover" an area compared to a line.
This was not an easy review of a fairly complex paper; I hope you managed to understand...
https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.15029

points.title: Review 589: Exploring Weight Space Thickets
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 14.03.26, review 589 NEURAL THICKETS: DIVERSE TASK EXPERTS ARE DENSE AROUND PRETRAINED WEIGHTS This paper attracted quite a bit of hype this weekend, and it also deals (somewhat implicitly, but still) with the geometry of language models. So you can understand why I couldn't miss it... The adaptation of LLMs after pretraining typically relies on optimization algorithms (
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 14.03.26, review 589
NEURAL THICKETS: DIVERSE TASK EXPERTS ARE DENSE AROUND PRETRAINED WEIGHTS
This paper attracted quite a bit of hype this weekend, and it also deals (somewhat implicitly, but still) with the geometry of language models. So you can understand why I couldn't miss it...
The adaptation of LLMs after pretraining typically relies on optimization algorithms (=additional training) to turn base model representations (=weights) into experts for downstream tasks. The paper challenges this need, proposing to treat the pretrained weight space not as a single starting point, but rather as a dense distribution over downstream task experts. By shifting from "continuous training" to parallel sampling, the authors propose a mechanism that reveals hidden capabilities solely through small perturbations in the weight space.
The paper investigates the local geometry of the multi-task training loss landscape around the weights of pretrained deep neural networks. At the center of the research is the distribution of model weights "perturbed with Gaussian noise" around the base model's weights. The algorithm receives the base weights along with a small dataset for the target task as input, and outputs an ensemble of perturbed weight vectors that constitute an "expert" for this task. This fits in quite well with the fact that quite a few LoRA tasks with their few trained weights work quite well, and I also remember works that showed that several LoRas can be combined for different tasks in a quite intuitive way to build a "multi-task expert" model.
The authors propose a parallel guess-and-check process called RandOpt. The algorithm begins by defining a search budget of N random seeds and a set of intensities (standard deviations) for the Gaussian noise. For each random seed, a standard Gaussian noise vector (zero mean and identity covariance matrix) is constructed in the base model's parameter space (matching its dimensions). This noise vector is multiplied by an intensity sampled uniformly from the set of intensities and added to the base weights, producing N distinct perturbed models. Each perturbed model is evaluated on the small task dataset to estimate a score describing its performance on that dataset. The objective of the method is strictly a zero-order selection mechanism (not relying on gradients and sequential weight updates) based on this score. The method identifies the indices of the top K performing models and discards the rest.
During inference, the input is fed to all K selected models. For discrete or categorical tasks, the final output is determined by taking a majority vote among the K individual predictions of the selected models. Because this linearly increases inference complexity, the paper suggests an optional knowledge distillation step to compress the ensemble back into a single model. This involves running the Top-K ensemble to generate multiple reasoning traces and candidate answers for a set of inputs. The algorithm isolates hard examples, defined as inputs where more than half of the generated answers were incorrect. The base model is then updated via standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to maximize the likelihood of the reasoning traces and the correct answers for these hard examples.
This approach structurally diverges from standard post-training paradigms. The closest baselines are reinforcement learning methods like PPO or GRPO, which iteratively update a single weight vector using gradient information derived from advantage estimates. Another standard baseline is Evolution Strategies, which also uses Gaussian perturbations but aggregates them (e.g., averaging the results of several) to iteratively update a central weight vector. RandOpt abandons iterative updates, treating post-training as a single-step, fully parallel subset selection problem in the weight space, followed by aggregation in the output space.
The method relies on the concept that pretraining does not merely produce a single starting point, but rather forms a "dense basin" environment of expert representations in the weight space. This geometry ensures that the base weights are surrounded on all sides by potential minima, each constituting an optimal and specific solution for a different downstream task. Thanks to this high density, blind sampling of random Gaussian perturbations is sufficient to intersect directly with these solutions, completely eliminating the need for gradient-based navigation and optimization algorithms.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12228

points.title: Review 590: Can Token Speculation Work Twice?
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Article Review: 17.03.26, Review 590 Speculative Speculative Decoding Two years ago, I gave several lectures on Speculative Decoding (SD). SD is a family of approaches designed to accelerate Large Language Model (LLM) inference by combining a small, fast model with the larger "target" model we wish to accelerate (which is more capable but slower). The core idea relies on the fact that
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Article Review: 17.03.26, Review 590
Speculative Speculative Decoding
Two years ago, I gave several lectures on Speculative Decoding (SD). SD is a family of approaches designed to accelerate Large Language Model (LLM) inference by combining a small, fast model with the larger "target" model we wish to accelerate (which is more capable but slower).
The core idea relies on the fact that the generation or "decoding" phase is memory-bound. This essentially means that the bottleneck, the throughput of the generation, is limited by the data transfer rate from memory to the GPU's compute cores. Consequently, the GPU's computational power is underutilized because prediction occurs token-by-token, and for every single token, its representation must be transferred to the compute core.
Speculative Decoding accelerates generation by increasing the amount of computation and parallelizing the transfer of token representations from the HBM (GPU memory) to the compute cores. This allows for higher utilization of GPU compute power alongside a more efficient transfer of token representations (into its SRAM memory) through parallelized data movement.
Let’s start with the original Speculative Decoding method published (to my knowledge) in 2022. The method assumes there is a smaller, faster "draft" model that generates several tokens autoregressively (token-by-token). Then, the target model "checks" whether it accepts these tokens in parallel.
For example, if the draft model predicts K tokens, the system decides whether to "accept" a token t < K based on its conditional probabilities, given the t-1 previous tokens from both models. (If you recall rejection sampling, the formula used here is very similar). The moment a token from the draft model is rejected, an additional token is sampled (its probability is calculated from the conditional probabilities of both models). This procedure ensures that the distribution of the sampled tokens remains identical to the target model's original distribution.
The primary gain here is parallel prediction, which saves us from numerous individual transfers of token representations. This prevents the small, inefficient HBM-to-core transfers that previously acted as the bottleneck.
Since Speculative Decoding was introduced, several methods for selecting and fitting a draft model to a target model have been proposed. These include:
Knowledge Distillation: Distilling knowledge from the large model into a smaller one.
Multi-token Prediction: Adding and training an extra layer (within certain model layers) that predicts multiple future tokens for the target model.
N-gram Based Prediction: Speculation based on simple n-gram patterns.
...and dozens of others.
The ultimate goal of Speculative Decoding is to maximize throughput (the token generation rate). The sampling rate is affected by how many tokens the draft model generates and the acceptance rate, how many tokens generated by the draft model are ultimately accepted by the target model. Some variants sample multiple sequences from the draft model, which introduces considerations regarding memory and the computational cost required for the sampling algorithm.
The paper we are reviewing today proposes a form of "Double Speculative Decoding", essentially, how to perform speculative prediction for the draft model itself. The authors developed a method that builds a tree of speculative predictions for the draft model. Instead of sampling a single sequence, the model samples N sequences starting from different tokens. For instance, if we sampled K tokens from the draft model, we also sample several sequences starting from the second token, the third, and so on up to K. This creates many sub-sequences starting at different points of the original sequence.
The objective, given a specific "sampling budget" (from the draft model), is to create as many cache hits as possible, maximizing the number of tokens from the cache (the collection of sequences sampled from the draft model) that the target model will accept. However, this raises a question: how many sequences should be sampled from each token within K? Furthermore, a successful sampling starting from token t of the draft model (a branch from the original "primary" sequence, where each branch is called a "backup") is one where token t+1 becomes the first token rejected by the target model (the "bonus token"). The challenge lies in how to sample token t+1 to maximize the probability of this event. To address this, the paper mathematically demonstrates how to answer both questions given a sampling budget B (which also defines the memory footprint for all "speculations").
This is a very impressive paper involving the company together.ai and Tri Dao, the creator of Flash Attention. Highly recommended.
Paper link: https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.03251

points.title: Review 591: Mechanics of reward signal recovery for mathematical reasoning
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 20.03.26, Review 591, TINYV: REDUCING FALSE NEGATIVES IN VERIFICATION IMPROVES RL FOR LLM REASONING Taking a break from the geometry of deep model representations and returning to Reinforcement Learning (RL) for fine-tuning LLMs. This is a light paper compared to the previous ones. The success rate of training an LLM to solve mathematical problems with RL depends heavily
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 20.03.26, Review 591,
TINYV: REDUCING FALSE NEGATIVES IN VERIFICATION IMPROVES RL FOR LLM REASONING
Taking a break from the geometry of deep model representations and returning to Reinforcement Learning (RL) for fine-tuning LLMs. This is a light paper compared to the previous ones. The success rate of training an LLM to solve mathematical problems with RL depends heavily on the accuracy of binary scores (rewards) provided by automated verifiers. A verifier is a function that takes a prompt (mathematical problem), the model's answer, and a ground truth answer to produce a binary score, receiving a value of 0 for an incorrect answer and 1 for a correct answer. Standard rule-based verifiers often rely on exact string matching or rigid parsing rules.
Rule-based verification systems frequently generate false negatives by failing to identify mathematically correct answers that deviate from standard formatting or notation. These false negatives occur when the model produces a mathematically correct answer that is rejected by the verifier due to formatting variations, such as different LaTeX commands, alternative units, or equivalent algebraic forms. The central object of this work is TinyV, a lightweight model-based verifier designed to identify and recover these misclassified answers. It is built as a language model with 1.5 billion parameters that underwent supervised fine-tuning (SFT). The inputs for TinyV are the formulation of the mathematical problem, the ground truth answer, and the answer generated by the model. The output is a binary classification of "True" when the model's answer is semantically equivalent to the ground truth and "False" otherwise.
The training process for TinyV was performed using a hybrid data collection strategy. The researchers first identified "seemingly incorrect" responses from the Big-Math-RL-Verified dataset. These are answers that pass basic format checks (such as being enclosed in a specific answer box) but are marked as incorrect by rule-based tools. To create high-quality training labels, two larger models (Qwen2.5-72B and Grok-3) serve as annotators (LLM-as-a-judge). An answer is marked as a "correct" case (a false negative) when both large models agree it is correct. To improve the verifier's robustness, the authors also generate synthetic false negatives. For this, they take a correct answer and change the question style, notation, and play with numerical approximations so the answer remains correct but might be rejected by the verifier.
So how does this whole story work in practice during LLM RL training? During the training of a policy model (to strengthen its mathematical problem-solving capabilities), a rule-based verifier is run first. If the verifier marks a response as correct, the reward is set to one. TinyV is activated only if the verifier rejects the answer, and the reward is determined based on its output. The authors use Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for training.
The paper provides a theoretical justification for this method by analyzing step-wise learnability. The researchers define this term as the reverse Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence between policies in consecutive optimization steps. Their analysis demonstrates a decrease in learnability in cases where correct answers in a batch are incorrectly identified as wrong, which in practice reduces the intensity of the policy update.
A potential failure mode of the method could be the introduction of model-based bias into the reinforcement learning process. That is, the policy might learn to exploit specific preferences of the verifier that did not exist in the annotators used for labeling. Additionally, the computational complexity of the proposed method (inference during the RL training stage when creating rollouts) increases linearly relative to the number of responses marked as incorrect by the rule-based verifier. It should be remembered, however, that the verifier model is limited only to non-trivial questions that the baseline verifier failed to identify, meaning the effective computational complexity depends on the percentage of complex questions in the dataset. The authors note that during training, the language model improves and the number of questions requiring the activation of the verifier model decreases.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14625

points.title: Review 592: Letting Each Transformer Layer Choose What It Needs to Listen To
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 23.03.26, review 592 Attention Residuals Recently there has been a wave of works reexamining how information flows along and through the depth of transformers: Hyper-Connections, SiameseNorm, DenseFormer, Deep Delta Learning. All of them identify the same fundamental problem with standard residual connections, and all propose different fixes and improvements. The paper w
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 23.03.26, review 592
Attention Residuals
Recently there has been a wave of works reexamining how information flows along and through the depth of transformers: Hyper-Connections, SiameseNorm, DenseFormer, Deep Delta Learning. All of them identify the same fundamental problem with standard residual connections, and all propose different fixes and improvements. The paper we'll review today is from Moonshot AI and offers what I think is the cleanest formulation of the problem and the most natural solution, through an analogy to how the attention mechanism replaced RNNs along the sequence axis.
Recently there has been a wave of works reexamining how information flows along and through the depth of transformers: Hyper-Connections, SiameseNorm, DenseFormer, Deep Delta Learning. All of them identify the same fundamental problem with standard residual connections, and all propose different fixes and improvements. The paper we'll review today is from Moonshot AI and offers what I think is the cleanest formulation of the problem and the most natural solution.
To understand the paper, you need to see what residual connections actually do when you unroll them across all the transformer's layers. Layer 2 receives the embedding plus the output of layer 1. Layer 3 receives the embedding plus the output of layer 1 plus the output of layer 2. The input to every layer is the cumulative sum of everything before it, where every prior contribution receives a fixed weight of 1. Layer 47 has no way to say "I care mostly about layer 2 and almost nothing about layer 30." It receives the same undifferentiated pile regardless of the input.
This looks exactly like the problem RNNs had along the time axis: all previous tokens compressed into a single hidden state, with no selective access. Transformers solved this with the attention mechanism. The AttnRes approach presented in the paper applies the same fix along the depth axis. Each layer computes attention over all previous layers' outputs and takes a weighted combination, instead of receiving their blind sum.
In practice, each layer has one learned vector of dimension d added to it (the "pseudo-query"). The keys and values are identical: simply the outputs of all preceding layers, where the token embedding from the vocabulary serves as the input to layer 0. Each prior output is normalized with RMSNorm, which prevents numerically large outputs from dominating regardless of relevance. The pseudo-Q undergoes an inner product with each normalized output, the scores go through softmax, and the layer's input becomes the resulting weighted sum.
The most important engineering decision: the pseudo-Q is a static learned parameter, not computed from the hidden representation. This means all queries for all layers are known before the forward pass to the next layer begins (during inference), so attention weights for groups of layers can be computed in parallel. This is what makes the whole thing practical.
The memory problem: in regular training, layer outputs are already saved for backprop, so AttnRes adds nothing. But model training sometimes uses pipeline parallelism (different layers on different GPUs). So keeping every layer output and transferring it between GPUs grows linearly with the total number of layers. To alleviate this issue, Block AttnRes groups layers into roughly 8 blocks. Within each block, layers accumulate via regular addition into one summary vector. The attention mechanism operates only across these block summaries. A layer in block 5 sees: the token embedding, the sum of blocks 1 through 4, and (if it's not the first layer in the block) the partial sum within block 5. Memory grows with the number of blocks rather than the number of layers.
Inference is two-phase. Phase 1 turns all the pseudo-Qs within a block into a matrix and multiplies it by the block summaries stored in the KV-cache in a single operation, producing preliminary outputs and statistics for the softmax (maximum, log-sum-exp). Phase 2 goes through the layers, computes a small attention only over the partial sum within the block, and then merges with Phase 1 outputs using online softmax, a technique that combines two separately computed softmax-weighted averages by adjusting for their statistics. Mathematically identical to computing a regular softmax, but involves fewer expensive memory reads.
The unifying insight of the paper: standard residual connections, Highway networks, and Hyper-Connections all implement "linear attention along the depth axis." AttnRes upgrades this to softmax attention along the depth axis, the same transition that proved itself along the sequence axis. Interestingly, the authors found that multi-head depth attention (multi-head = multi-head), where each head chooses which layers it is "more interested in," hurts, suggesting that relevance is a property of the whole layer, not of individual heads.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15031

points.title: Review 593: Exclusive Self-Attention: Orthogonal Projection as an Architectural Inductive Bias
points.preview: Mike’s daily paper review: 24.03.26, review 593, 431 reviews to 1024 Exclusive Self-Attention A light review of a tiny improvement to the attention mechanism from Apple researchers. This is a short and focused contribution (7 pages, with the abstract itself taking up a third of a page or even less) that proposes a two-line code change to self-attention, which removes redundancy (according to the a
points.body:
Mike’s daily paper review: 24.03.26, review 593, 431 reviews to 1024
Exclusive Self-Attention
A light review of a tiny improvement to the attention mechanism from Apple researchers.
This is a short and focused contribution (7 pages, with the abstract itself taking up a third of a page or even less) that proposes a two-line code change to self-attention, which removes redundancy (according to the authors) between what the attention layer and the feed-forward layer (that is, the FFN) compute. The idea is clean and the paper is well constructed, so let’s get into it.
In the standard self-attention mechanism, each token produces Query, Key, and Value vectors through learned linear projections. The output for position i is a weighted sum (with softmax weights) of the V vectors from positions 1 through i. The authors found that, in trained models, this output has high cosine similarity with the token’s own value vector, and that this similarity increases with layer depth. They call this the “attention similarity bias”: the attention layer wastes capacity on re-encoding information that the token already carries, thereby duplicating work that the FFN (which receives the original representation through the residual connection) can perform on its own.
The XSA fix is a single simple geometric operation: after computing the standard attention output, subtract its projection onto its own V vector. The result is guaranteed to be orthogonal to that V vector. The implementation is two lines of code: L2 normalization of the V vectors, followed by subtraction of the projection result. No new parameters are introduced, it is fully parallelizable, and the added cost (one normalization, one inner product, one scalar-vector multiplication, and one subtraction per attention head and position) is linear in sequence length and head dimension, negligible relative to the quadratic cost of attention.
The authors note that this removes not only the contribution of the token’s own V vector, but also any component of V vectors from positions “related” to it. They present this as an advantage: it “completely removes the attention similarity bias.” A natural question (which the paper does not discuss, by the way) is whether this is too aggressive, and whether it might harm useful contextual signals from other tokens that happen to align with the direction of the token’s own V.
A nice connection: XSA is related to attention sinks (tokens that absorb excess attention mass). In standard self-attention, the attention weights must sum to 1 because of the softmax, so even when a token has no real need for contextual information, it is forced to allocate the weight somewhere, which contaminates the output. Attention sinks address this by adding artificial tokens at the beginning of the sequence that serve as a “trash bin” for excess attention. In XSA, the model does not need special tokens because it can simply assign high attention weight to itself, and the projection step neutralizes the effect—that is, each token becomes its own sink for free. The paper tests XSA together with explicit sink tokens (0, 1, and 4) and finds that the benefit of XSA remains, suggesting that the two mechanisms are complementary rather than redundant.
In my opinion, the paper is missing some ablations: there is no analysis of applying the projection only to a subset of layers, no discussion of grouped query attention, no investigation of bidirectional attention as in encoders, and no examination of how the projection reshapes the attention patterns learned during training.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09078

points.title: Review 594: Knowledge Distillation with a "Lego" Approach
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 29.03.26,  Review #594 PUZZLE: DISTILLATION-BASED NAS FOR INFERENCE-OPTIMIZED LLMS A pretty cool paper requested by a good friend, coming from NVIDIA, which is always interesting and unpredictable. The paper proposes a framework called Puzzle for distilling knowledge from a large, powerful LLM (the teacher) into a smaller model that is hardware-optimized (it’s NVIDIA, af
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 29.03.26,  Review #594
PUZZLE: DISTILLATION-BASED NAS FOR INFERENCE-OPTIMIZED LLMS
A pretty cool paper requested by a good friend, coming from NVIDIA, which is always interesting and unpredictable. The paper proposes a framework called Puzzle for distilling knowledge from a large, powerful LLM (the teacher) into a smaller model that is hardware-optimized (it’s NVIDIA, after all).
The input consists of the teacher model's weights (not its training data). The output is a new model where each layer may have different attention mechanisms, varying FFN (Feed-Forward Network) dimensions, or components that are skipped entirely. These are assembled to maximize throughput, minimize latency, and meet memory requirements on the target GPU.
The framework consists of 3 stages:
Stage 1: Blockwise Local Distillation (BLD)
Each transformer layer in the teacher is treated as a "block" composed of two "sub-blocks": the attention module and the FFN. For every layer, Puzzle defines a set of replacement candidates for each sub-block.
Attention Candidates: Include various types (Standard MHA, GQA, and of course MLA, which gained fame with the legendary DeepSeek paper, linear attention, etc.) with varying head counts, a single linear projection, or a no-op (complete removal).
FFN Candidates: Include the original width plus several reduced intermediate dimensions (down to 10% of the original), a linear layer, or a no-op (meaning no FFN at all, only attention).
Each candidate sub-block is trained independently to mimic the output of its corresponding teacher sub-block using a normalized Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss (normalized by the teacher output's norm). Crucially, each distilled block receives activations from the preceding teacher block. This means blocks are trained in total isolation with no gradient flow between layers, enabling full pipeline parallelism.
The key efficiency trick is "Divide and Conquer": Rather than training every (attention, FFN) pair jointly (grows as AF per layer), Puzzle trains each attention variant alongside a frozen teacher FFN, and each FFN variant alongside a frozen teacher attention, then composes them post-hoc. This reduces training per layer from multiplicative to additive. For Llama-70B (80 layers, 6 attention options, 9 FFN options), this slashes training from 43,200 blocks to just 1,200.
Refinement: The paper admits that decoupled assembly is an approximation, as the interaction between a new attention and a new FFN in the same layer is never directly trained. They suggest a refinement where a decoupled BLD identifies the most common variants, and then a "coupled" BLD is run on that reduced space to keep costs manageable.
Sub-block Initialization:
FFNs: Channels (intermediate layer dimensions) are pruned by ranking their contribution to the final result. This contribution is the product of the activation norm and the L2 norm of the corresponding row in the down-projection matrix, averaged over a training dataset.
Attention (Head Reduction): Each student head is initialized as an average of the teacher's heads (e.g., if the student has 2 heads and the teacher has 8, each student head is the average of 4 teacher heads).
Linear Replacements: The Value and Output projection matrices are multiplied, simulating attention on only a single token (self).
Stage 2: Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP) Search
Each candidate block is scored using a "Replace-1-Block" metric: only one block is swapped into the complete teacher model, and the KL Divergence is measured (the distance between the next-token distributions of the teacher vs. the modified model).
The architecture search is formulated as a Grouped Knapsack Problem. Binary decision variables select exactly one block per layer. The objective is to maximize overall quality (minimizing KL divergence) subject to constraints: total parameter memory, KV-cache memory, runtime, throughput, and latency, all measured empirically on the target hardware.
The MIP is solved repeatedly for different batch sizes, as batch size is not a decision variable but changes the memory-vs-throughput tradeoff. A diversity (entropy) constraint forces successive solutions to differ in a specific number of layers, allowing the exploration of structurally distinct architectures. For Llama-70B, the search space is 10^138 configurations, but because quality is decomposed into per-block scores, the MIP is solved in seconds using off-the-shelf solvers.
The Catch: The additive decomposition assumes block contributions are "independent." This is the core assumption and the main limitation. Errors from early replaced blocks propagate through later blocks that were never trained on such "corrupted" inputs.
Stage 3: Global Knowledge Distillation (GKD)
The assembled student model is trained end-to-end against the teacher using a combination of Cosine Similarity loss on hidden representations (per layer) and KL Divergence between the output logits. Interestingly, the paper finds that adding standard Language Modeling loss (Cross-Entropy on ground truth tokens) hurts performance, likely due to distribution mismatch when the teacher's original training data is unavailable.
What’s New vs. The Closest Baseline?
The direct ancestor is LANA, a NAS method for computer vision. Puzzle’s unique contributions include:
Decoupled BLD (additive training decomposition).
KL-Divergence-based scoring (rather than task-specific accuracy).
Scalability to models with tens of billions of parameters.
Integration with real inference engines supporting variable GQA ratios across layers. This required modifying the paged KV-cache in TensorRT-LLM to handle non-uniform head counts.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.19146

points.title: Review 595: Right for the Right Concepts: Teaching Models to Think Like Humans Enhances Robustness
points.preview: Daily Paper Review by Yehonatan & Mike: 28.03.26, Review 595 Concept-Guided Fine-Tuning: Steering ViTs away from Spurious Correlations to Improve Robustness Models based on Vision Transformers (ViTs) demonstrate impressive performance on standard benchmarks like ImageNet, yet their robustness remains limited. A primary reason is that these models tend to learn Spurious Correlations: they shift the
points.body:
Daily Paper Review by Yehonatan & Mike: 28.03.26, Review 595
Concept-Guided Fine-Tuning: Steering ViTs away from Spurious Correlations to Improve Robustness
Models based on Vision Transformers (ViTs) demonstrate impressive performance on standard benchmarks like ImageNet, yet their robustness remains limited. A primary reason is that these models tend to learn Spurious Correlations: they shift their attention away from features with true semantic meaning and focus instead on statistical cues such as background, lighting, or random noise. The model fails to learn the essence of the object - it learns to identify the context in which it appears.
In recent years, the field of interpretability has increasingly discussed Concept-Based Models (CBMs): models that integrate concepts into their architecture. A concept can be a specific visual feature like "striped pattern", a "yellow beak”, or "furry ears”. The idea is to achieve classification based on specific, explicit features, thereby building networks that are understandable in terms of what they do and why.
Concept-Guided Fine-Tuning (CFT): steering attention to focus on Concepts
To liberate models from the trap of spurious correlations, the paper introduces a method called Concept-Guided Fine-Tuning (CFT). The core idea is to use interpretability as an active component in the model's optimization process: we inject concept-based knowledge directly into the model's explanation maps during training to guide it toward the essential features defining each class.
Explanation maps (also known as saliency/attribution maps) are visual representations designed to reflect which pixels or regions in an image were most significant to the model's decision (a crucial aspect of explainability called faithfulness). Thus, they reveal some reason behind its prediction. These are often provided by attribution methods (e.g., LRP, Grad-CAM, Integrated Gradients) and appear as heatmaps overlaid on the original image (warmer areas were more important). They are typically created using feedback from the model (pixel masking and checking the effect on prediction) or by utilizing model components like gradients or feature maps.
In this work, we utilized Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP), a method that backpropagates relevance scores from the class output neuron to the input pixels, layer by layer, using dedicated relevance propagation rules. Unlike methods like Grad-CAM that rely on a single layer, LRP analyzes the contribution of all network layers, enabling a deeper and more comprehensive analysis of the regions supporting the model's decision. The method (approximately) satisfies the Conservation property, where the sum of relevance scores is preserved across layers. Consequently, the final relevance sum for the pixels matches the model's output for the examined class (e.g., the logit), making the explanation map a faithful additive decomposition of the output into its constituent factors.
One can imagine the internal (latent/hidden) space of a ViT as a vast topographical map of various visual features. Usually, the model is free to navigate it and find any path leading to a correct answer - even if that path goes through the background. CFT serves as a guiding mechanism: for each class, representative and discriminative concepts (like "blue eyes" or "long beak") are predefined, and the model is required to align with and concentrate on them.
The process begins by automatically identifying essential visual characteristics for each class using the semantic knowledge of a LLM (GPT-4o-mini). The LLM generates a list of concepts that are both relevant to the object's definition and possess visual discriminative power, without requiring manual human labeling. To ensure these concepts physically appear in the images, the system performs a validation step checking the concept's frequency and spatial coverage, allowing it to filter out LLM noise and irrelevant concepts.
Then, the refined concept list is fed as a textual prompt to an open-vocabulary segmentation model called GroundedSAM, which locates and precisely masks each concept on the image. This produces a binary semantic mask unifying all relevant concept areas into a single spatial concept map.
In the final stage, the model undergoes fine-tuning where optimization is performed directly on its explanation maps produced via LRP. LRP's Conservation property makes it an exceptionally reliable target for optimization. During training, the loss function encourages the model to assign high relevance to the highlighted concept areas while actively suppressing attention from features unrelated to the concept map. To prevent changes in the explanation maps from harming predictive ability, a specific loss term is integrated to maintain consistency with the original model's predictions, ensuring accuracy is not compromised.
Experimental Results:
The method was tested on five Out-of-Distribution (OOD) datasets. Findings show that using a small number of examples (three images for half of the ImageNet classes), the focus of large models can be shifted properly. CFT achieved consistent improvement - in some cases by hundreds of percent - in OOD performance, all while maintaining accuracy on the original In-distribution training set.
Interesting phenomena observed:
Expected Deviations: In datasets like ImageNet-R (art and graffiti), the improvement was more moderate. This is expected since backgrounds in these datasets tend to be neutral and do not serve as significant spurious cues.
Use of Concepts: Guidance based on specific concepts was found superior to coarse segmentation of the entire object. The level of detail and the ability of concepts to serve as generic discriminative signs are key to the achieved robustness.
Generalization to Unseen Classes: Improved robustness was not limited to classes included in the training. A similar performance boost occurred for the rest of the classes in the data, indicating the model did not just memorize specific examples but likely adopted a more generalized understanding.
In summary, CFT presents a new approach to enhancing robustness: instead of adding more data or changing the architecture, it leverages interpretability to teach the model what to look at. The result is not only better performance but a more interpretable model that visually understands what it is searching for. Ultimately, CFT demonstrates that the field of interpretability, usually intended to explain models, can also be used to improve them.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08309

points.title: Review 596: More Than a Logical Chain: The Hidden Power of Reasoning You Haven’t Met Yet
points.preview: Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 01.04.26, Review 596, 428 reviews to 1024 THINKING TO RECALL: HOW REASONING UNLOCKS PARAMETRIC KNOWLEDGE IN LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS (LLMs) A review in honor of the Festival of Freedom (Passover), though there isn’t actually any freedom in this review… Let’s start with a puzzle: you ask a reasoning-capable LLM a simple factual question that can be solved without constructin
points.body:
Mike’s Daily Paper Review: 01.04.26, Review 596, 428 reviews to 1024
THINKING TO RECALL: HOW REASONING UNLOCKS PARAMETRIC KNOWLEDGE IN LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS (LLMs)
A review in honor of the Festival of Freedom (Passover), though there isn’t actually any freedom in this review…
Let’s start with a puzzle: you ask a reasoning-capable LLM a simple factual question that can be solved without constructing a complex logical chain. Nevertheless, activating the model’s "Chain-of-Thought" mechanism "upgrades its memory" (the answer improves). This isn't just an improvement in top-1 accuracy; the model somehow reaches correct answers that would not have appeared were it not for the reasoning process. Why does this happen? The reviewed paper conducted a series of experiments to find out, and the answer is more surprising and strange than one might expect.
The object of study is the "Parametric Recall Boundary" of LLMs in Closed-book factual QA tasks. Input: a simple factual question. Output: a short answer. The metric examined is pass@k (the probability that at least one of k independent samples is correct, measured via the unbiased estimator), where k ranges from 1 to 100. The paper focuses on reasoning models (Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Qwen3-32B) that expose a dynamic "toggle" to enable/disable thinking, allowing the model to change its behavior without changing its weights: specifically, whether an intermediate "thought" output is generated.
The Experiments: The authors collected 100 samples per question per mode across two datasets: SimpleQA-Verified (1,000 filtered questions, mostly "single-hop") and EntityQuestions (1,000 template-based single-hop questions). They showed that question complexity is a weak predictor of the benefit derived from the reasoning process; questions labeled as "multi-hop" or "requiring reasoning" showed similar performance gains to simple questions when reasoning was enabled. Therefore, the gain does not stem from task decomposition during the reasoning process.
The researchers tested two hypotheses:
The "Computational Buffer": Perhaps the extra tokens simply provide the model with increased computational capacity, independent of content. To isolate this, they created an "ON Dummy" condition, where they replaced the model's actual reasoning traces with the string "Let me think" repeated until it reached the length of the original trace, then regenerated the answer. To rule out what they call "ON/OFF bias" (the possibility that the model simply performs better in ON mode due to imbalance in the training data distribution), they also created an "ON Single Dummy" condition, identical to the former except the dummy string appears only once. Both variants operate in ON mode without semantic content; the only difference is length. For strings ranging from 32 to 16,384 tokens, the authors found that performance saturates around 2,048 tokens and then declines. In other words, "thinking" helps, but it is limited and never fully replicates the ability of genuine reasoning.
Factual Priming: Borrowed from "Spreading Activation" theory in cognitive science (the idea that activating one concept lowers the retrieval threshold for semantically related concepts). Reasoning traces for simple questions do not contain step-by-step thinking; instead, they surface facts related to the topic. To examine this, the authors extracted facts from each reasoning trace using an LLM, filtered out facts that merely rephrased the question, and filtered out statements explicitly linking the answer to the question (while retaining incidental mentions of the answer). The extracted facts were injected into the model as follows: "OFF Facts" (no reasoning, facts inserted directly as input context) and "ON Facts" (facts appearing as reasoning). The result was quite clear: a significant performance improvement in both cases, with facts in the context being "more computationally efficient" than the reasoning itself (this makes sense to me intuitively, at least).
The third experimental axis examines hallucinations within the reasoning traces. Every fact extracted from the 100 traces per question was verified using Gemini-2.5-Flash (equipped with web search). Traces were labeled as "clean" (all facts correct) or "hallucinated" (at least one incorrect fact). The authors found that traces with hallucinations significantly degraded performance, whether used as reasoning or as part of the context, which is ultimately quite logical.
An interesting and recommended paper.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.09906

points.title: Review 597: The Bayesian Mechanics of Yes-Men Chatbots
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 02.04.26 SYCOPHANTIC CHATBOTS CAUSE DELUSIONAL SPIRALING, EVEN IN IDEAL BAYESIANS We know that reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) creates chatbots that flatter their users. The rising concern is whether this constant validation pushes users into confident false beliefs, a phenomenon known as delusional spiraling. The accepted assumption is that users fall
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 02.04.26
SYCOPHANTIC CHATBOTS CAUSE DELUSIONAL SPIRALING, EVEN IN IDEAL BAYESIANS
We know that reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) creates chatbots that flatter their users. The rising concern is whether this constant validation pushes users into confident false beliefs, a phenomenon known as delusional spiraling. The accepted assumption is that users fall into these traps due to lazy thinking or cognitive biases. This paper asks a more disturbing question: what if perfectly rational agents are mathematically vulnerable to these same feedback loops?
The authors construct a computational model of a conversation between a rational user and a bot. The core object tracked is the user's probability estimate regarding a binary world state over time. The inputs are the user's prior beliefs and the responses generated by the bot. The output is a trajectory of the user's posterior belief across a number of conversational rounds.
The simulation proceeds in a strict turn-based format. First, the user samples an opinion from their current belief distribution and sends it to the bot. The bot then privately samples a collection of true data points from the world regarding the target hypothesis. Next, the bot selects one piece of information to send back. Finally, the user performs a Bayesian update to their belief using a mental model of the bot's behavior. To isolate the mechanics of sycophancy, the study controls the bot's response strategy. An impartial bot simply selects a true data point randomly. A sycophantic bot actively selects the response that maximizes the user's confidence in the opinion they just expressed.
The authors test two critical interventions to observe the structural effect on belief dynamics. The first limits the bot to presenting only factual information. Under this constraint, a factual sycophant cannot fabricate evidence, but can still cherry-pick which truths to reveal. The second intervention alters the user instead of the bot, and models an informed user who suspects the chatbot might be manipulative. This informed user performs a joint inference, registering a simultaneous update to their belief about the world and to their estimation of the bot's sycophancy level. They model this through a cognitive hierarchy, where the user recursively models the bot's social reasoning before interpreting its message.
Previous works that analyzed rational persuasion and echo chambers mostly rely on the assumption that human agents communicate with other humans or static media. This paper adapts this tradition to the specific loop of modern conversational AI. The novelty lies in modeling the chatbot not just as a noisy information channel, but as an active agent optimizing for user validation. The integration of the cognitive hierarchy for the informed user is a structural shift. It upgrades the standard Bayesian update rule to account for conversational pragmatics, and transforms simple exchanges of facts into a game-theoretic inference problem.
The framework relies heavily on the assumption that users update their beliefs using rigid probability rules. Real human beings, of course, do not maintain precise probability distributions or perform exact marginalizations. The conversational turns are also highly abstracted, reducing complex natural language dialogues to simple binary hypothesis tests. Computationally, the simulation requires repeated sampling and exact probabilistic updates over hundreds of conversational rounds, which increases the complexity significantly when modeling informed users due to the recursive cognitive hierarchy.
Ultimately, the authors draw three poignant conclusions from the structural dynamics of this model. First, delusional spiraling cannot be dismissed as a symptom of irrational human thinking, because even mathematically ideal inference agents are vulnerable to this validation loop. Second, anchoring chatbots in factual retrieval to prevent hallucinations is insufficient, because a model can still manipulate belief through the selective presentation of true statements. Finally, public awareness campaigns warning users about AI sycophancy will reduce, but not eradicate, the risk. To truly solve the problem, developers must deal directly with the optimization goal of user validation itself.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.19141

points.title: Review 598: Escaping Teacher Forcing using Feature Matching
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 04.04.26, Review 598 MATCHING FEATURES, NOT TOKENS: ENERGY-BASED FINE-TUNING OF LANGUAGE MODELS Standard language modeling relies on predicting the next token using teacher-forcing, which ensures local alignment (at the token level) but fails in calibration at the level of the entire output (over long rollouts). During standard training with teacher-forcing, the model is
points.body:
Mike's daily paper review: 04.04.26, Review 598
MATCHING FEATURES, NOT TOKENS: ENERGY-BASED FINE-TUNING OF LANGUAGE MODELS
Standard language modeling relies on predicting the next token using teacher-forcing, which ensures local alignment (at the token level) but fails in calibration at the level of the entire output (over long rollouts). During standard training with teacher-forcing, the model is optimized (maximizing the likelihood of the next token) given a ground-truth (or GT) input. However, the output-level distribution of the model may differ from the training data. While training with reinforcement learning (RL) minimizes this "distance" by optimizing sequence-level behavior, it requires explicit verifiers or separate reward models. This paper asks whether we can enforce sequence-level calibration directly from the data distribution, without the need for a surrogate reward.
The authors construct an objective function called conditional feature-matching loss. Instead of predicting tokens, the model minimizes the squared distance between the expected feature embedding of the completions (=generations) it produces and the GT completion embeddings. The inputs are context-completion pairs, and the outputs are vectors representing sequence-level semantics (contextualized embeddings). These vectors are computed through a model that is an exact copy of the base language model at initialization.
The proposed method, known as Energy-Based Fine-Tuning (EBFT), operates using an on-policy approach. Given a prompt, the trained model samples several completions (candidates) of a fixed length. Both the GT and the generated candidates are concatenated with the prompt and fed into the model for feature computation. To capture structural and semantic information, the method extracts intermediate activations at depths of one-quarter, one-half, and 3/4, normalizes them, and concatenates them.
Training is performed similarly to the fairly famous REINFORCE method. Each sampled completion receives a reward proportional to its feature-space match with the GT completion, along with a penalty in the form of a diversity term that measures its similarity to the other generated candidates. To prevent dominant feature dimensions from taking over the training signal, the authors normalize the features using the inverse of the second-moment matrix, computed over a batch.
Since generating multiple on-policy rollouts for each sequence is computationally expensive, the authors use strided block-parallel sampling. By dividing a long sequence into segments at regular intervals, they construct multiple "nested prefixes". A customized causal attention mask allows the trained model to sample continuations from all these prefixes simultaneously. The researchers combine this feature-matching reward together with standard cross-entropy regularization, to keep the model anchored to the data distribution.
As mentioned, the closest existing approaches are standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). This paper shifts the paradigm by replacing scalar heuristic rewards with a richer "scoring method" that involves consideration of the model's internal features. Instead of compressing output quality into a single scalar or relying on external test suites, EBFT directly matches the statistical moments of the model's rollout distribution to the target dataset's distribution. The paper frames fine-tuning as an energy-based modeling problem, where the optimal policy is measured (in particular) using a "deep semantic distance" from the base model. Thus, the loss function accounts for constraints in a space of rich semantic features, rather than merely penalizing a decrease in the token likelihood of the training data.
A few caveats at the end, if I may. The method heavily relies on the assumption that the frozen feature model provides a sufficiently rich representation space that reflects the data distribution meaning one that does not collapse into a uniform mode within it. If the feature map lacks capacity, moment matching will not guarantee true distributional alignment. The approach also assumes that the intermediate layer activations hold sufficient semantic meaning to serve as a reward landscape without explicit alignment training. A structural limitation is the reliance on short rollout horizons during training, which could lead to an overfit of the alignment signal to narrow, local windows. Furthermore, the empirical whitening step uses a low-rank approximation bounded by the number of samples per prompt. If the number of generated candidates is much smaller than the feature dimension, this pseudoinverse approximation might become completely unstable or silently drop critical feature directions.
An undoubtedly interesting paper; it took me some time to understand all the accompanying math, but it was absolutely worth it.
https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.12248

points.title: Review 599: Fixing the RL Diversity Illusion: Let's break some intuitions
points.preview: Mike's daily paper review: 07.04.26, review 599 KL-REGULARIZED REINFORCEMENT LEARNING IS DESIGNED TO MODE COLLAPSE When we fine-tune language models, we want them to learn to "behave according to our expectations" without losing their (relatively) natural diversity of answers.
